question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the quantified level of fertiliser reserves; and (ii) the (a) organisations; (b) industry representatives; and (c) supply chain partners, with whom his Department has engaged with, since the start of the war in Iran, to monitor reserves and anticipate future supply challenges.
answer_text: My department does not hold information on the quantified level of fertiliser reserves. Since the war in Iran commenced, my officials have engaged with Defra officials and industry representatives on the availability of fertilisers for the Northern Ireland agriculture sector and future challenges in this respect.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any specific contingency measures his Department is implementing to address any fertiliser supply chain disruptions, rising energy costs and increased transport costs arising from the war in Iran; (ii) any financial support options being considered for farmers most affected; and (iii) any cross-border coordination that is taking place to ensure stability of agri-food trade on the island.
answer_text: Fertiliser supplies and fuel prices are determined by world markets which are outside the control of my Department.  However, through the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, my Department provides over £330 million in support to the farming sector on an annual basis. This includes the Farm Sustainability Payment, which is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and help withstand shocks that are beyond their ability to manage effectively and encouraging businesses to become more environmentally sustainable and efficient.  The Farming with Nature Package is planned to support farm businesses across all land types to make substantial contributions to environmental improvements and sustainability.  Environmental actions offered under the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme help to protect and improve soils and capture nutrients, and can help to reduce reliance on fertilisers. While, at this stage, an additional support package for farmers affected by the increase in fertiliser and fuel prices is not planned, I am continuing to monitor the situation. The implementation of any future support package would be dependent on the allocation of additional funds to my Department.  I am also supportive of the UK Government considering the rate of excise duty and VAT on fuel, particularly during periods of high fuel prices.Agri-food trade on the island remains stable and is continuing as normal.  Where any issues arise, my Department will liaise with the relevant bodies and stakeholders.  Technical advice and support to farmers is provided by the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), through a range of different forums including online and face to face meetings, press articles and through the CAFRE website. Farmers and growers requiring advice on managing the impacts of increased fertiliser and fuel prices should contact their local CAFRE Adviser.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to an all-island approach to animal disease and specifically bovine tuberculosis, to detail the number of farmers who manage one holding in both north and south jurisdictions for the intention of the Farm Sustainability Payment.
answer_text: I can confirm that in respect of the administration of the Farm Sustainability Payment, my Department does not hold data on businesses who manage one holding in both north and south jurisdictions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of (i) the contribution of social farming to promoting equality of opportunity and good relations across the Section 75 categories, including for people with disabilities, those experiencing mental ill-health, and individuals living in rural isolation; and (ii) how social farming initiatives have contributed to meeting the social and economic needs of people living in rural communities, in line with the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: Through the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework, my Department provides funding for social farming initiatives, such as Rural Support’s Social Farming Support Service and the Social Farming Capital Grant Scheme to help develop the Social Farming concept.  The outcomes from TRPSI initiatives continue to have a very positive impact on the lives of thousands of rural dwellers, demonstrating positive outcomes in tackling rural poverty, reducing social isolation, addressing loneliness, and improving physical and mental health and well-being.In 2025/26 alone, TRPSI initiatives will benefit an estimated 165,000 rural dwellers and this includes ninety-nine participants per week participating in social farming activities.DAERA does not have responsibility for the assessment of the contribution, or evaluation of the benefits associated with the social farming referrals.  These are managed by the referral organisation, who are, in the main, the Health and Social Care Trusts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any budget allocation for social farming initiatives, including a breakdown of capital and resource funding; (ii) the amount of funding that was allocated to social farming, or equivalent programmes, in each of the last five years prior to the British Government's withdrawal from the European Union; and (iii) the amount of funding that has been allocated to social farming, in each year since the British Government's withdrawal from the European Union, including any funding provided through replacement schemes.
answer_text: My Department has allocated £249,858 to Rural Support through a 3-year contract (2024/25 – 2026/27) to deliver the Social Farming Support Service which supports farmers delivering Social Farming and promoting the service for those who could benefit from participation.  In addition, since 2016/17, my Department has funded the Social Farming Capital Grant Scheme, which supports Social Farms to enhance their facilities and improve accessibility of their farm to support the continued development of social farm enterprises.Over the five years prior to the United Kingdom’s withdrawal from the European Union, £326,672 was allocated by my department to social farming. In the five years since the withdrawal, this allocation increased to £440,425. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how the war in Iran affects farmers' (i) access to fertilisers, including any impacts on fertiliser supply chains; (ii) energy costs; and (iii) cross-border trade.
answer_text: The prices paid by farmers for energy and fertiliser have risen sharply since the outbreak of war in Iran.  There have also been reports that supply issues are arising given associated difficulties in the Strait of Hormuz where substantial amounts of oil and fertiliser normally move through.  Cross border trade on the island of Ireland continues as normal albeit with higher fuel costs for transport.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will strengthen rights for women.
answer_text: My Employment Bill will provide many positive benefits for women by enhancing rights which support work life balance. The right to request flexible working will be a right from day one of employment, rather than after 26 weeks at present. Additionally, employees will be able to make two statutory requests for flexible working within a twelve-month period, instead of just one. These legislative changes will encourage employers to proactively integrate flexibility into job design from the outset.Introducing unpaid carer’s leave will support many women managing caregiving responsibilities for relatives. Eligible parents will also benefit from improved neonatal care leave and pay, ensuring that those whose newborns require extended medical attention can better focus on their child’s needs.  Revisions to paternity leave will afford families greater flexibility and extending redundancy protections for pregnant employees and those returning from family leave will further strengthen workforce participation among women. Women will also benefit from the full range of enhanced rights which my Bill will introduce, including the right to move from a zero hours to a banded hours contract and enhanced trade union rights. Together, these provisions are designed to foster a more inclusive, supportive, and adaptable workplace culture, where women are empowered to balance their professional and personal responsibilities with confidence.
department: Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of farmers who have not yet signed up to the required soil nutrient health training course; and (ii) any urgent steps he will take to ensure that farmers complete the course before the deadline for claiming Farm Sustainability Payments in 2026.
answer_text: At 2 March, 11,274 farm businesses had not yet signed up for the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme training course.  DAERA’s College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) manages the delivery of this course which is mainly delivered online. However, in-person courses are being delivered throughout Northern Ireland, to maximise uptake by farm businesses unable to access the online training. My Department continues to promote the importance of completing the training, as well as informing farmers of the conditionality linked to the Farm Sustainability Payment scheme, including the penalties that will apply from 2027 if the training is not completed. Since the Scheme was launched in 2022, promotion has included press and social media activity, engagement at agri-business outlets and livestock markets and targeted communication via text and postcard.   These efforts will continue as the deadline for claiming Farm Sustainability Payments approaches.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the delivery of the Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: As an Executive we are committed to the delivery of the Programme for Government and significant progress has been made. For example, as of 31 December 2025:We have agreed £129 million of transformation project funding, which focuses on health and social care, special educational needs, justice, and infrastructure.There has been significant investment of £55 million in early learning and childcare provision in 2025/26 resulting in 25,640 children benefitting from the Childcare Subsidy Scheme and over 2,500 additional full-time pre-school education places created.From April to November 2025, more than 188,876 red flag or time-critical patients have been seen, diagnosed or treated. Over £20 million invested in a Skills Fund to support 17,000 individuals and 1,100 employers.Funding of over £5 million has been provided to support the Change Fund for community-based action to End Violence Against Women and Girls.120 Special Educational Needs specific accredited training places in Further Education Colleges in 2025/26.A Housing Supply Strategy launched in December 2024.Warm Healthy Homes Strategy launched in February 2026.£83.7 million provided to the Supporting People Programme in 2025/26 for housing related support to help 20,000 individuals and families live independently.5,300 additional properties able to connect to the wastewater infrastructure through investment in the network.An action plan approved to protect and restore Lough Neagh; andThe Office of AI and Digital was established.The Executive will publish a PfG annual report on the progress that is being made in the coming months, to ensure transparency and accountability.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current status of the Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, including (i) when it is expected to open; (ii) how long the application period will run; and (iii) what options are planned for inclusion.
answer_text: I intend to open Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme as soon as possible, subject to necessary approvals and budget availability. My officials are working to finalise preparations, and information on the scheme will be available in due course at www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/sustainable-agriculture-programme.The application period is likely to run for 6 weeks. This will be confirmed in advance of the scheme opening, as part of a period of information and engagement to help farm businesses prepare for the scheme.It is planned that the scheme will include support for equipment and technology for farms to improve environmental performance and business efficiency, and will include options for the management of fruit, vegetables, crops, grassland and livestock, and for resource and slurry management, including Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment. The list of eligible items will be published when the scheme opens for applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he will take (i) to prevent the importation and retail of food products that may be produced to lower environmental or animal welfare standards than those required of farmers in the North, including beef imports from Brazil; and (ii) to ensure that consumers are not misled regarding food origin and production standards.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the importation of food products (including beef imports from Brazil) to Northern Ireland is under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.  I am advised that, under Regulation (EU) 1760/2000 all beef imported from a non-EU country and sold in NI must be labelled in accordance with the relevant EU regulations which requires the country of birth, fattening and slaughter to be indicated on labels. Where all the compulsory information is not available on non-EU country imports the following wording: 'Origin: Non-EU' and 'Slaughtered in [name of non-EU country]' must be on the label. There is no current requirement for mandatory labelling of the method of production for beef products.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment has he made of the carbon impact of large food corporations importing food products that the islands of Ireland and Great Britain are already self-sufficient in producing.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken an assessment of the carbon impact of importing food products compared to domestic production. International trade and the negotiation of Free Trade Agreements are not devolved to Northern Ireland and, in line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, imports of food products are under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when live animal exports and imports are expected to resume following the restrictions imposed due to bluetongue; and (ii) any conditions which must be met to allow their safe reinstatement.
answer_text: There are no restrictions on the import or export of Bluetongue Virus (BTV) susceptible animals if going directly to slaughter, either to/from Northern Ireland or to/from the EU and to Great Britain (GB).Exports to GB of susceptible animals for breeding and production (B&P) can occur with no restrictions unless they are (i) considered a high-risk animal and; (ii) originate from within the current temporary control zone (TCZ). More details are available here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland. It must be noted, however, that no susceptible animals for B&P can move into NI from GB.There are no moves of susceptible animals for B&P to or from Ireland.Based on the current certification requirements set by the EU, exports for B&P cannot commence from GB to NI, until 2 years from the last confirmed case of BTV. This also applies to moves for B&P to/from the EU unless the importing Member State implements a derogation to allow moves with or without national requirements for entry. More details are available here: https://food.ec.europa.eu/animals/animal-diseases/surveillance-eradication-programmes-and-disease-free-status/bluetongue_en.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of bluetongue vaccines administered since the outbreak; (ii) any assessment his Department has made of the likely impact of bluetongue disease during the forthcoming midge season; and (iii) any evidence that is available on sheep mortality rates in affected areas during the previous season to support farmers in making informed vaccination decisions.
answer_text: Please find the responses to your questions detailed below:(i) Since the first confirmed outbreak of Bluetongue Virus (BTV) on 29 November 2025, herd and flock keepers in Northern Ireland have applied to my Department for the use of 70,600 doses of the BTV-3 vaccine, based on data up to and including 10 February 2026.(ii) The likely impact of BTV and next steps, particularly during the forthcoming midge season, is currently under consideration by my officials who remain in close contact with key industry stakeholders and veterinary colleagues in GB and Ireland. If any changes to the current approach are implemented, these will be communicated widely in the normal manner.  (iii) My Department has no record of sheep mortality as a result of BTV in Northern Ireland. I encourage anyone who is considering BTV vaccination of their cattle and/or sheep, to discuss this with their own Private Veterinary Practitioner.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the final opinion from the south of Ireland on agricultural litter spreading; and (ii) to detail any steps his Department is taking in response to that opinion.
answer_text: My Department has not received any final opinions from the south of Ireland on agricultural litter spreading. My officials continue to engage with the Shared Environmental Services to understand what responses they have received from the south of Ireland regarding this matter and in relation to steps it intends to take.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment that has been made of the economic cost to the local economy in relation to the continuing rise in bovine TB levels, including the impact on farm businesses, associated industries, and wider rural communities.
answer_text: The removal of over 20,000 cattle from farms for the control of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in 2025 clearly affects productivity, disrupts breeding programmes, and reduces farm income. This premature slaughter sees milk output reduced, delayed sales, and restrictions on animal movements. This economic impact of bTB also extends beyond individual farms, with knockon effects on associated industries, including livestock markets and processors. This is in addition to the physical and mental health impacts that a disease incursion has on individual farmers and their families.Further, the costs for government continue to rise, with compensation for cattle slaughtered projected to exceed £55 million in the 2025/26 financial year.  This reflects the high number of reactor cattle, and higher market values for cattle.This is unsustainable, and my department is working at pace with key stakeholders to advance the actions, under the three pillars of people, cattle and wildlife, as set out in the TB Partnership Steering Group’s Blueprint for Eradication.  Only by working together can we address the unacceptable hardship and unsustainable financial burden caused by bTB in our rural areas. That is why tackling and reducing the spread of this destructive disease remains a key priority for DAERA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the rates revaluation exercise.
answer_text: As Finance Minister I am absolutely committed to support business growth and development. Stopping the Reval 2026 process allows space for sectors to provide the much-needed evidence to allow a full examination of the issues raised. I am in listening mode and will take time to consider the next steps. My focus is on supporting our public services, our local businesses, workers and families, and growing our economy for the betterment of all. I have listened carefully to the concerns of the hospitality sector about the implications of implementing the revised values in April. The conversation has started, I met with hospitality representatives on Thursday, I will look at any alternative methodology proposals that people want to offer. These need to be transparent and fair to every ratepayer and meet the need to support public services and the wider economy. Since becoming Finance Minister I have been unequivocal in my support for businesses. I have extended the Back in Business Scheme to support entrepreneurship by encouraging businesses to set up in long term vacant properties, the Rural ATM scheme to help people easily access cash and the Small Business Rate Relief Scheme which provides around 30,000 businesses with reductions, of between 20% and 50%, on their rates bill.I am on record setting out my ambition to go further, including to enhance support for small businesses. That is why my department launched a consultation on Small Business Rate Relief options in early December. I have already set aside additional funding of £10m in the draft budget to support those efforts. My officials are working at pace to allow these support measures, if approved by the Executive, to take effect from April 2026.Finally, and on foot of intensive engagement with businesses throughout the summer, members will be aware that I have committed to bringing forward proposals on a Business Growth Accelerator policy. This would support those businesses including those in hospitality who wish to expand their premises in the future. That proposal was put to my officials in their engagement with hospitality businesses and demonstrates that my officials have been listening through proactive engagement.
department: Department of Finance

answer_text: Cattle drinkers are being considered for inclusion within the Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, where they contribute to environmental benefits through improved biosecurity or improved grassland management.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department’s approach to biosecurity, including its importance in mitigating infectious disease threats facing the agricultural sector.
answer_text: My Department regards biosecurity as a cornerstone of animal health and disease prevention. Our approach is proactive and risk based, recognising the interconnection between animal, human, and environmental health.The agricultural sector operates in an environment where infectious diseases such as bovine Tuberculosis (bTB), Avian Influenza, and Bluetongue pose significant risks to animal health, farm productivity, and market access. Biosecurity is critically important in preventing disease transmission and mitigating the impact of disease outbreaks which can lead to severe financial loss, herd disruption, and long-term impacts on rural livelihoods. DAERA has comprehensive biosecurity guidance available online alongside a statutory Biosecurity Code for Northern Ireland farms, which sets out best practice for livestock keepers, vehicle operators, and those involved in food production. Robust biosecurity also remains the most effective defence against specific epizootic diseases such as Avian Influenza, protecting individual flocks and the wider poultry industry.  DAERA continues to deploy wide ranging communications reminding all flock keepers of the necessity of adhering to high levels of biosecurity.  Further information including comprehensive biosecurity guidance is available here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/avian-influenza-ai.   Further, my department is working to implement a new action to deliver veterinary-led, bespoke on-farm biosecurity advice as part of the Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication. Work is also ongoing with colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM) on a cross-border regionalisation pilot project which will include the deployment of enhanced biosecurity measures in the selected area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rationale behind the policy that provides only 50 per cent compensation for livestock affected by bluetongue disease, including the evidence or considerations that informed this decision.
answer_text: Compensation is payable only where it is deemed necessary to cull animals for disease control measures.Culling of animals will only take place for Bluetongue virus where such action is supported by evidence from appropriate disease surveillance and epidemiological evidence.Where culling is deemed necessary, compensation is payable at 50% of an animal’s market value, in accordance with Part II of Schedule 2 of the Diseases of Animals (Northern Ireland) Order 1981. This is in line with several other animal diseases.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail whether (i) any current or planned grant schemes support the installation of rubber matting or rubber flooring on concrete slabs in cattle housing, given its benefits for animal welfare and farm safety; and (ii) consideration will be given to including such flooring under future farm investment or animal-welfare related funding programmes.
answer_text: Such flooring is being considered for inclusion within the Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, taking account of potential benefits for ammonia reduction, animal health and welfare, and farm safety.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will ensure that the draft Future Operational Protocol to Assess the Impacts of Air Pollution on the Natural Environment, does not unintentionally create barriers to the adoption of pollution-reducing technologies on farms.
answer_text: Analysis of 100 planning applications submitted from January to October 2025 demonstrates the interim Operational Protocol has successfully supported sustainable farm development and the adoption and implementation of emission reduction technologies.  Of the applications reviewed, 49% had no air quality concerns, 40% had provided insufficient information to allow consideration and air quality concerns were identified in the remaining 11%.Of the applications that had no air quality concerns, 65% had incorporated ammonia reduction technologies.  100% of applications from the poultry sector and 86% of applications from the pig sector included mitigation. The cattle sector had the lowest number of applications that incorporated mitigation measures (18%).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether the ability to produce an accurate statement of environmental impact is fully embedded in the existing NI Environment Agency Operational Protocol, when assessing pollution from current farming systems.
answer_text: The interim operational protocol has been reviewed and scrutinised by several independent bodies, including the OEP and Planning Appeals Commission, which found it to be legally and scientifically robust. I am therefore content that it provides an accurate assessment of environmental impacts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work undertaken through their Department to end violence against women and girls.
answer_text: As an Executive, we are determined to do all that we can to end violence against women and girls.We have said from the outset that this was an issue we are committed to tackling, and we have made significant progress since we launched the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework and Delivery Plan in September last year.We are driving meaningful change across communities and organisations, with visible impact in schools, sports clubs, and the community and voluntary sectors.We are getting money directly to organisations with the experience to make a difference. Through the Change Fund, over £3.2 million has been invested in community-based action to support prevention and cultural change.We have also invested an additional £2.2 million to build capacity in the community and voluntary sector, amplify key messages, and scale delivery.A few weeks ago, Junior Minister Reilly and the deputy First Minister attended the launch of the Fair Game Initiative which we are supporting through the Change Fund. This innovative programme, which is a collaboration with the Irish Football Association, Women’s Aid and the PSNI, uses sport to challenge harmful attitudes and create safe environments for young people both on and off the pitch. It was such a positive event and highlighted what can be done when we all work together to address these issues.The 16 days of activism against gender-based violence has also been an opportunity to reinforce our messaging and to engage with our partners. Last week, we issued an Executive statement reaffirming our determination to tackle all forms of gender-based violence, abuse, and harm.We have also supported events both here in Parliament Buildings and on our social media platforms including an event with Women’s Aid and the Mothers’ Union, to amplify our collective approach to dealing with this issue.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the criteria that will be used by the Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Transformation Programme to determine whether existing powers under The Diseases of Animals (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 or licences issued under The Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 are the most appropriate legislative route for implementing any preferred wildlife intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 33962/22-27, whether the €9 million funding is being used to establish two new anaerobic digestion plants.
answer_text: Whilst both projects funded through the Shared Island Bioeconomy Demonstration Initiative include nutrient recovery from agricultural and marine biomass, neither project involves the establishment of any new anaerobic digestion (AD) plants. The REGENERATE project in Tyrone will make use of an existing AD plant, while the AIMBIO project based in Dublin will establish a small-scale demonstration marine biorefinery, not an AD plant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the rational behind Small Business Research Initiative measures, such as the Sustainable Utilisation of Layer Manure to prevent phosphates entering waterways, not being given time to be implemented before the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026 - 2029 reduces phosphate levels on farms; (ii) any transition support will be provided to farmers.
answer_text: The Sustainable Use of Layer Manure (SULM) research initiative aims to support the development of innovative, scalable solutions to manage nutrients from poultry layer manure, particularly phosphorus, and reduce environmental impacts across Northern Ireland whilst also unlocking new economic opportunities. It is one of a range of measures that aim to complement the revised measures proposed for the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) 2026-2029, some of which aim to reduce Farm Phosphorus Balances on more intensively stocked farms.  These proposed measures are subject to discussion within the NAP Stakeholder Task and Finish Group and have not yet been agreed. (ii) When the Nutrients Action Programme 2026 – 2029 has been finalised, my department will consider the range of support that is necessary to implement the agreed measures.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the meaning of the commitment in the Natural Environment Division Planning Improvement Plan to explore the role of Shared Environmental Services and the Natural Environment Division in relation to the assessment of impacts to European Sites.
answer_text: Officials in the Natural Environment Division of NIEA intend to review its advisory function under the Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 regarding European Sites. This will include consideration of the planning authorities’ responsibility under The Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995, to assess the impact of proposals on European Sites before determining an application. The planning authorities employ Shared Environmental Services to undertake this responsibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department's draft Future Operational Protocol to Assess the Impacts of Air Pollution on the Natural Environment will allow on-farm slurry separation or new anaerobic digestion facilities where they can demonstrate reductions in ammonia and phosphate losses.
answer_text: My Department welcomes applications for facilities that can demonstrate reductions in ammonia and phosphate losses from slurry to the environment, and seeks to encourage innovative technologies and techniques to achieve such reductions.Applications for all proposed facilities or developments requiring planning or permit authorisation are assessed under the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations 1995 (Northern Ireland) (as amended) (‘the Habitats Regulations’).  These Regulations require that competent authorities, public bodies, and decision-makers agree to a plan or project only after having ascertained that it will not adversely affect the integrity of sites of international importance (SACs, SPAs and Ramsar sites). In addition, ASSIs designated under the Environment (NI) Order 2002 are also granted protection.The draft Future Operational Protocol will play a key role in supporting statutory requirements and sustainable development, by ensuring that air pollution impacts from proposed facilities or developments, such as those mentioned, do not adversely affect the integrity of designated sites. There is no overarching policy in place to automatically recommend or grant approval for any particular type of application; rather, comprehensive and robust protection of sites from environmental damage requires that all applications are considered on a case-by-case basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail who is recognised as the competent authority under Annex II of the Windsor Framework for the purposes of sanitary and phytosanitary checks.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, sanitary and phytosanitary checks fall under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the funding allocated to each project under the Shared Island Bioeconomy Demonstration Initiative; and (ii) for his assessment of the potential economic benefits of the initiative.
answer_text: In response to the questions you have raised and as noted in the update to the AERA committee in September 2025 please be advised as follows	(i) Funding Allocated:The total €9 million fund is enabled by €7 million from the Irish Government’s Shared Island Initiative, €1.5 million from the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine, and £0.5 million from DAERA. €4.5 million has been awarded to each of the two projects: REGENERATE (Agri-Bioeconomy, led by Greenville Energy, Northern IrelandAIMBIO (Blue Bioeconomy, led by Teagasc, Ireland)(ii) Potential Economic Benefits:Key potential economic benefits from the Shared Island Bioeconomy Demonstration Initiative include:Job Creation & New Value Chains: The funded projects will create new economic opportunities by developing sustainable, circular models that convert agricultural and marine residues into high-value products, supporting new value chains in rural and coastal areas.Support for Local Producers: Local farmers, fishers, and agri-food businesses will benefit from new markets for waste and by-products, increasing income streams and resilience.Rural Innovation & Competitiveness: The initiative encourages collaboration between industry, academia, and local communities, fostering innovation and enhancing the competitiveness of rural economies.Sustainable Growth: By focusing on resource efficiency and climate action, the projects help rural areas transition to greener, more sustainable economic models, supporting long-term viability and environmental stewardship.These benefits are designed to deliver concrete economic, environmental, and social gains for rural communities across the island of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment been made of the risk of carbon leakage arising from the displacement of local agri-food production by higher emission imports as a result of post-Brexit trade agreements.
answer_text: The negotiation of international trade arrangements is not a devolved policy area and therefore responsibility lies with the UK Government. 	The only post EU Exit Free Trade Agreements (FTAs) implemented to date that could have significantly increased imports of agri-food commodities into the UK compared to the situation when the UK was a member of the EU are those with Australia and New Zealand.My Department continues to monitor imports from Australia and New Zealand under these FTAs and while there has been an increase year on year in imports, it is from a very low level and the volumes constitute a very small proportion of total UK domestic consumption. Therefore, the displacement of local agri-food production by imports is minimal to date. However, I acknowledge that there is the potential for imports from Australia and New Zealand to increase significantly over time given the terms of the FTAs.  I will continue to monitor the impact of FTAs on domestic production and will make representation to the UK Government as appropriate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of AgriSearch’s proposal to adopt a split-gas approach to methane emissions, similar to the model used in New Zealand as reported in the Irish Farmers Journal in October 2025, particularly in light of consumer concerns regarding the use of methane-reducing feed additives such as Bovaer.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 already makes express provision for methane emissions, stating that the net zero target for greenhouse gas emissions in 2050 does not require methane emissions to reduce by more than 46% from baseline. My focus is on implementing the existing legislation and its targets which were passed by the Assembly in 2022.

answer_text: he concept of Pillar 1 and Pillar 2 funds and transfers between them has not existed since the end of 2020 when the EU Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) ceased to apply.  This is prior to the commencement of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.Following EU Exit, the HM Treasury Spending Review and Autumn Budget in 2021 provided the Northern Ireland Executive with almost £1 billion for farmers, land managers and the rural economy in the three financial years up to 2025 to fund schemes which were previously funded under CAP Pillar I and II Schemes and successor schemes.In 2025/26, this funding of £332.5m Resource DEL per annum was baselined by HMT in the NI block grant and agreed by the Executive as “Executive Earmarked” for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) a timeline for when wildlife interventions proposed under the Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Blueprint will be brought forward; and (ii) how these actions will comply with the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985, particularly in relation to licensing for disease prevention.
answer_text: Within my department, a new Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Transformation Programme has been established to deliver the actions set out in the TB Partnership Steering Group’s Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication.  A key priority of this work concerns potential wildlife intervention options and I have recently asked officials to prepare a new public consultation on potential intervention options.The Member will be aware that in October 2023, a judicial review quashed my predecessor’s decision to advance a non-selective cull of badgers in targeted intervention areas as part of the 2022 Northern Ireland bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Strategy. Consequently, my department currently does not have a wildlife intervention policy in place.  Officials have been working to address the issues and gaps identified by the judicial review ruling, in order to provide me with detailed advice on potential wildlife intervention options.  This involves looking at the latest scientific evidence; updating figures relating to how much these interventions could cost; and taking forward the necessary environmental assessments.  This work will also inform the documentation published with the consultation.   While I am not yet in a position to state when this work will be completed, I am determined that this consultation will take place as soon as possible.  The responses received will help me to make an informed, science-led decision on the way forward. Officials are also considering the potential legislative options for delivering a wildlife intervention.  These options include existing powers under the Diseases of Animals (NI) Order 1981 and the potential use of licenses issued under the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985.Any decision I make regarding a wildlife intervention here will include a preferred legislative means for delivering that approach.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) when the first formal meeting of the Nutrients Action Plan stakeholder group will take place; and (ii) whether (a) the minutes; and (b) proposed recommendations will be published to ensure transparency and accountability in the policy development process.
answer_text: The first formal meeting of the NAP Stakeholder Task and Finish Group is intended to take place in the very near future. A range of stakeholders have membership on the Group and the approach to communications regarding their work will be agreed by the Group.  The proposals recommended by the Group will be brought to me for consideration. I will take them into account, along with any other relevant advice and considerations, and decide what should be included in the revised draft NAP 2026-29, which will be issued for further public consultation to enable full public engagement on the proposals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the evidence in the Review of the 2019 Nutrient Action Programme Regulations, that there may be a case for lowering the 80 per cent grassland rule for derogated farms to allow farms to grow more of their own feed to reduce phosphorus loss, improve water quality and cut reliance on imported feed, to outline (i) why was this not addressed in the Public Consultation on the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026-2029; and (ii) whether his Department will explore the potential impact of a more flexible derogation that in
answer_text: (i) During the review of the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP), my officials considered evidence for lowering the 80% grassland rule for derogated farms to encourage a greater use of home-produced cereals, with the aim of reducing reliance on imported feed and lowering farm phosphorus balances. However, analysis of current derogated farms found that the vast majority had above 85% grass coverage, and only a small number had between 80 and 85% grass coverage. Therefore, the benefit from a reduction to 70%, was determined to be negligible.In addition, there may be unintended adverse consequences, associated with increased uptake of maize as a forage crop on derogated farms if a 70% minimum grassland rule was introduced. These crops require heavy applications of slurry in the spring and are harvested late in the year. Consequently, there could be an increased risk of surface run off with nutrients and sediment to water ways as a result of local climatic conditions. Therefore, the recent NAP consultation did not propose a change to the 80% grassland rule.(ii) My Department is open to exploring the potential impact of a more flexible derogation through the NAP Stakeholder Task and Finish Group which I have recently set up as a next step in the development of the revised NAP 2026 – 2026. Any proposed changes to the derogation requirements would need to be evidence based, legally compliant and ensure that there is no detrimental impact on water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any action being taken to ensure phosphorus testing methods, such as the Olsen P test, give an accurate picture of plant-available P in basaltic soils to support more precise nutrient use.
answer_text: Basaltic soils cover nearly a third of the land area of Northern Ireland and it is known through previous research, that the Olsen P test may under-estimate the amount of plant available P in these aluminium and iron rich volcanic soils. The Soil Nutrient Health Scheme being delivered by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute includes specific research to develop and validate phosphorus recommendations for grassland, based on a new soil phosphorus test specific to basalt soils. The degree of phosphorus saturation (DPS) test is being examined as an alternative test, as it is deemed to more accurately reflect the plant available phosphorus in these soils. The experimental trials cover both intensive and extensively managed grassland across the basaltic soil area of Northern Ireland, which will be peer reviewed and published by early 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on plans for capital investment in Dean Maguirc College, Carrickmore.
department: Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, In light of the delay in sheep-focused co-design, (i) what assurances can his Department give that the needs of the sheep sector will be addressed within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme; and (ii) for his assessment of the risk that the sector will be disadvantaged due to ongoing competing priorities.
answer_text: Financial support is available within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme for sheep farmers via the Farm Sustainability Transition Payment in 2025 and the planned Farm Sustainability Payment from 2026 onwards. The Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, which closed for applications on 04 August 2025 was available to all farmers including the sheep sector. It is planned that this Scheme will open again in 2026. Further phases of the Farming with Nature package are also planned. 		A new Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme is also under development and is expected to launch later this year. This scheme will offer financial support for farmers to purchase equipment and technology to improve environmental performance and business efficiency. It is planned  that this new scheme will be open to all agricultural sectors, including sheep.	In addition to this support, it is my intention that work focused on sheep sector support, within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, in conjunction with stakeholders will commence towards the end of this year, once the legislation is in place to introduce the new Farm Sustainability Payment and Farm Sustainability Standards.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how the new Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme will ensure that funded investments go beyond existing statutory obligations; (ii) what criteria will be used to assess eligibility in this regard; and (iii) what constitutes existing statutory obligations in this context, particularly in relation to ammonia and nutrient management.
answer_text: My officials have considered any existing statutory obligations which could be relevant to potential investments under the proposed Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme i.e. relevant to equipment and technology. The proposed Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme will not provide support for investments which are required by an existing statutory obligation. The criteria applied to assess eligibility for support would be based on the statutory obligation itself.In the context of this proposed scheme, a relevant existing statutory obligation would be the requirement to use Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment (LESSE), under the Nutrient Action Programme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2019. Under those regulations, from 1 February 2022 holdings with an average in any calendar year of 200 or more livestock units of bovine animals or holdings with a total annual livestock manure nitrogen production of 20,000kg or more from pigs are required to use LESSE. Therefore, such farm businesses would not be eligible to apply for support for LESSE under the proposed scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Farming for the Generations pilot scheme.
answer_text: The Farming for the Generations pilot scheme is currently being delivered by Rural Support and is due to conclude in March 2026.   There are 69 farm businesses participating on the Planning for Succession element of the scheme. These businesses have nominated 55 successors to participate in the Preparing the Successor workshops.  Twelve people have completed the Recently Qualified training programme with recruitment for an additional tranche currently underway. Participants of both the Preparing the Successor and Recently Qualified programmes will have the opportunity to participate in a study tour to Great Britain in November 2025 as part of their ongoing personal development.  My Department will also provide funding for a further five people from this cohort to participate in the Study Abroad element of the scheme. The application process for this is now open and completed applications must be received by 27 October 2025. Travel will take place between 1 December 2025 and 28 February 2026.  It is encouraging to see the older generation also engage with the scheme and 81 people have participated in Leading Succession. Seven applications and 26 expressions of interest have been received to Farming in Partnership.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the British Government’s recent announcement on a consultation to align shotgun licensing with that of high-powered firearms and the potential impact this may have on rural communities.
answer_text: The Home Office has introduced a new requirement for all applicants in England, Scotland and Wales for a firearm or shotgun licence to provide two referees. Applicants in Northern Ireland are already required to provide two referees. Firearms licensing is a devolved matter and is the responsibility of the Department of Justice and the Police Service of Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the expansion of the Ulster University Magee campus.
answer_text: Since taking up office I have been clear that the expansion of Magee remains my Department’s top priority. I am delighted to report that substantial progress has been made in advancing this commitment, through the leadership of the TaskForce, significant investment and excellent partnership working between my Department and the University.In 2024/25, my Department invested approximately £12 million in capital funding, enabling the land required to support the ambition of a 10,000-student campus to be secured. I am also pleased to report that capital of £12.5 million is allocated for 2025/26, with progress expected to remain on track in line with the Taskforce Action Plan. For the academic year 2025/26 my Department has funded up to an additional 338 at Magee. The promotional activities funded by my Department and delivered by the University has also had a positive impact, showcasing Magee and Derry as a great place to study and live. I am delighted to report that this focus on fulfilling the commitment to Magee expansion is anticipated to see 6300 enrolments this year – an increase of 51% since 2018/19 – improving regional balance and delivering economic benefit for the north west.
department: Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the delivery of the Executive's key priorities since February 2024.
answer_text: The Executive has made a strong start and continues to deliver against the Priorities set out in the Programme for Government. As an Executive we are determined to work together and deliver for the public on the priorities set out in our Programme for Government.We are delivering on the issues that matter most including:  Securing additional funding of £1.3billion for public services.Agreeing £129million worth of transformation projects across health, SEN, justice and infrastructure.Significant investment in childcare and early years provision to support parents - The Childcare Subsidy Scheme has saved eligible working parents around £8million since it was launched in September 2024.  A package of measures to help tackle waiting lists and support GP services - Almost 59,000 additional outpatient, diagnostic and inpatient procedures have been delivered – which is well on track to meet the PfG target of 70,000.A strategy to End Violence Against Women and Girls, including £3.2million to fund community-based projects and initiatives.Significant investment in measures to protect Lough Neagh.An economic vision focusing on regional balance and productivity; and Creating a new Office of AI and Digital.But we know there is much more to do, which is why we have established a Delivery Unit, which will be instrumental in driving reform and transformation across government and wider public services, and ensuring our top priorities receive the scrutiny, support and attention required to deliver real improvements to people’s lives.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of whether his Department and the Forest Service can apply retrospective harvesting support to scheme-planted forestry; and (ii) how will he ensure that forestry policy does not penalise those who complied with past government schemes, including (a) addressing concerns around haulage costs; (b) the need for cooperative extraction schemes; and (c) the availability of replanting grants linked to harvesting.
answer_text: My Department does not provide support for costs associated with timber harvesting operations, including extraction and haulage, as harvesting and sale of timber produce takes place within a competitive commercial market environment. Support is available for woodland regeneration following felling operations under the Woodland Investment Grant provided by Forest Service. This is to assist replanting sustainable and resilient woodlands in line with an approved regeneration plan, as required under the forestry felling regulations. The Woodland Investment Grant is available for all woodlands, including those that have not been established through grant aid.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
