question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the climate impact of cattle culling, due to bovine TB, is included in his Department's greenhouse gas emissions accounting or modelling system.
answer_text: The greenhouse gas emissions relating to cattle livestock numbers are modelled on anonymised data drawn from the Northern Ireland Food Animal Information System (NIFAIS) as of 1st June.  This occurs on an annual basis and includes data for both dairy and beef cattle categorised by age and sex.  The emissions impact of cattle culling due to bovine TB and the replacement of those cattle will therefore be inherently accounted for in annual estimates.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the consideration his Department has given, following evidence from British sources in the report Acting on methane: opportunities for the UK cattle and sheep sectors, that premature culling, due to bovine TB, increases methane emissions through reduced herd longevity and higher replacement rates.
answer_text: Premature culling of cattle due to bTB can impact on GHG emissions, due in most part to a combination of reduced lifetime productivity and where there are elevated stocking rates. While there have been no studies to date analysing this in NI, we know that better animal health has positive impacts for productivity, sustainability, the environment and climate change. That is why my Department, alongside key stakeholders, is committed to advancing the Blueprint for Eradication in Northern Ireland which was published in April 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the steps his Department is taking to ensure fair access to (i) training; and (ii) opportunities, for farmers who may struggle with new communication methods.
answer_text: Under the Farming for Sustainability - Knowledge Transfer Scheme – All Farm Businesses will have access to information and training. This scheme offers the opportunity for farmers to use knowledge, co-operation and innovation to improve the sustainability and performance of their businesses. Training is offered both face-to-face and online to provide fair access to all. For example, Soil Nutrient Health Scheme training is currently being delivered and can be accessed via the CAFRE website. Additional dates for face-to-face training will be released this autumn.My Department ensures fair access to opportunities for farmers who may struggle with new communication methods through:A comprehensive communications plan, including issuing of Press releases, Opening letters ahead of schemes launching, SMS reminder/update texts for key dates in scheme calendar.A Grants and Funding Hub on the homepage of the DAERA website that provides centralised, comprehensive information on grant and funding opportunities. Webpage content follows DAERA’s Accessibility Design Principles to ensure the widest range of customers can access the content. Additionally, farmers have the option to subscribe to receive SMS text alerts when new opportunities become available. Face-to-face digital assistance at local offices. Regular sharing of information on opportunities with relevant agricultural stakeholders at quarterly Stakeholder Engagement Forums. While taking a 'Digital First' approach, the Department facilitates alternative communication upon request for those individuals experiencing vulnerability or digital exclusion.  Braille, large print, paper and other language formats for any guidance is offered.  Paper correspondence is available if all efforts to enable, support, or educate customers in digital communication have been exhausted.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to introduce an Areas of Natural Constraints scheme to provide targeted support for farmers in disadvantaged areas.
answer_text: I do not intend to introduce an Area of Natural Constraint (ANC) scheme for farmers. Since the ending of ANC payments, there has been no significant change to agriculture in LFAs relative to the lowland in Northern Ireland either in livestock numbers or farmed areas.  There is no evidence of land abandonment to any significant extent in Northern Ireland and the Farm Sustainability Payment, planned for introduction in 2026, will continue to provide support for agricultural activity in all areas of Northern Ireland, including the disadvantaged areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the environmental consequences of failing to effectively address bovine TB.
answer_text: There has been no scientific analysis specifically of the impact of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) disease rates on the environment conducted by my Department.However, as highlighted in my answer to AQO 2255/22-27, I recognise that high animal health – including bTB eradication - can significantly contribute to environmental sustainability and the important role it can play both directly and indirectly in delivering on key pledges within my Department’s Corporate Plan 2025-27. High levels of animal health result in improved productivity which not only reduces feed, water, land, and energy use per unit of food but also lowers methane emissions for the same output.  As you are aware, I agreed to and published the TB Partnership Group’s Blueprint to eradicate bTB in Northern Ireland, in April 2025. This represents a new way forward for stakeholder and government cooperation with a range of recommendations now being advanced which seek to place Northern Ireland on a pathway towards eventual eradication of bTB, delivering significant benefits to people, animals and the environment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how much land has been planted under forestry schemes since 2008.
answer_text: The total area of land planted under forestry grant schemes since 2008 is 4,798 hectares. Just over 4,000 hectares (83%) was broadleaf woodland and just under 800 hectares (17%) was coniferous woodland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has undertaken an assessment of the environmental impacts associated with poor animal health.
answer_text: There have been no specific assessments of the impact on the environment of poor animal health conducted by my Department. However, I recognise that high animal health can significantly contribute to environmental sustainability and the important role it can play both directly and indirectly in delivering on key pledges within my Department’s Corporate Plan 2025-27. High levels of animal health result in improved productivity which not only reduces feed, water, land, and energy use per unit of food but also lowers methane emissions for the same output.  In addition, healthier animals reduce the need for antimicrobial use in agriculture having a positive impact on the environment by mitigating the spread of antimicrobial resistance and reducing antimicrobial pollution of soil and water sources. Maintaining optimal animal health reduces emissions intensity, enhances resource efficiency, and minimises pollution risks which is why I believe it is important to integrate animal health into climate and environmental policy to ensure we deliver on our climate change targets whilst supporting sustainable agriculture and rural communities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the overall annual cap on payments under the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme; (ii) how does it limit his Department’s total expenditure; and (iii) whether land currently being farmed but designated as Tier 1, 2, or 3, such as (a) Special Areas of Conservation; (b) Special Protection Areas; (c) Ramsar sites; and (d) Areas of Special Scientific Interest, are excluded from the Scheme.
answer_text: In the first tranche of the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme the cap on individual claims each year is set at £9,500 per farm business. This is to aid management of the budget in year based on the projected number of applicants and work completed.The main source of funding for the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme will be from DAERA’s Capital DEL fund. DAERA’s allocation for Capital DEL is £119.5m for 2025/26.  £4.8 million Capital DEL has been allocated for the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme in 2025/26.The Farming with Nature Transition Scheme will not be available on farmed land designated as Tier 1, 2, or 3, such as Special Areas of Conservation, Special Protection Areas, Ramsar sites, Areas of Special Scientific Interest, or other priority habitat and species areas in the 2025/26 Scheme year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline where the budget previously allocated to the Environmental Farming Scheme Higher, particularly for farmers exiting Tranche 4, is now being redeployed, given the absence of a replacement Scheme.
answer_text: Funding for the Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) Higher tranche 4 participants is Resource DEL from the NI Executive Earmarked budget for agriculture, agri-environment and rural development (£329.4m). Funding no longer allocated to EFS will be redeployed across other elements of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme in 2025/26, including the first phase of the Farming with Nature. The main funding for the first phase of the package is additional monies from the DAERA capital budget.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how many entitlements are (i) currently in the system; and (ii) available for use in the next scheme year.
answer_text: The number of entitlements currently on the system is: 954,677.46.The number of entitlements available for use in the next scheme year will not be known until January 2026, following the conclusion of both the 2025 entitlement confiscation exercise under the two-year rule, and the establishment of new entitlements following the processing of applications to the 2025 Regional Reserve by Young Farmers or New Entrants.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the roll-out of Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, including regional coverage and farmer uptake ; and (ii) how the data gathered is being used to support more targeted and sustainable land management practices.
answer_text: The first three Zones of the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) are largely completed, with over 90% uptake of eligible farm businesses registering for the scheme (over 18,000 farm businesses). This covers Counties Down, Armagh, Fermanagh, Tyrone and Derry/Londonderry. Zone 4 is the final Zone of the Scheme and it opens for registration on Monday 16 June and will close on 1 September 2025. Any unregistered farm businesses from the previous three Zones can also register during this period.The data collected at farm level is integral to helping farmers adapt their land management practices including improved nutrient management.   Providing farmers with the baseline data, on the nutrient health status of their soils, and through appropriate training on understanding their soil analysis results, will help farms to manage their soils more sustainably, which will deliver a range of environmental and economic benefits. Individual farm data is only provided to the farm business.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the full projected costs for bovine Tuberculous in 2025-26; and (ii) how these compare to the total costs incurred in the previous financial year.
answer_text: The cost of the bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Programme for the 2025/26 financial year is very difficult to predict at this early juncture in the financial year. However, current estimates project the bTB Programme to cost in the region of £65 to £66 million for 2025/26 financial year. The bTB Programme is a multifaceted programme with many factors contributing to overall programme cost. Compensation paid for animals removed for the control of bTB represents a significant proportion of the overall programme cost. Compensation is determined by animal incidence and the market value of cattle, both of which are difficult to predict going forward. The bTB Programme costed £60.1 million in the 2024/25 financial year.  The current estimate represents a £5 to £6 million increase on the previous year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Transboundary consultation on the Dalradian Curraghinhalt Project.
answer_text: The transboundary public consultation period which allowed members of the public in the south of Ireland the opportunity to express their views on the project prior to the resumption of the public local inquiry concluded on Wednesday 23 April 2025.Approximately 1,600 responses have been received, and my officials are currently taking time to review and collate the responses. It is intended these responses be then send to the Planning Appeals Commission (“PAC”).Once this information is provided, it is considered this will give the PAC sufficient detail to arrange the reprogramming of the public local inquiry.
department: Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department’s review of the mineral licensing policy.
answer_text: The review of mineral licensing policy, which is enshrined in the Mineral Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969 and associated legislation, is ongoing. To inform the scope of the review, independent research was commissioned in 2021 into the economic, social and environmental impact of mineral exploration and mining which identified three key themes for the review to focus on: Research on domestic supply opportunities, Stakeholder Engagement and Updating the regulatory framework for mineral licensing. To ensure compliance with Section 5 (2)(b) Environmental Assessment of Plans and Programmes Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004, the Department is undertaking a Strategic Environmental Assessment and it is expected that this will take up to 18 months to complete. The assessment will help ensure that any new policy will protect the environment and serve local communities. My Department is also planning for a Call for Evidence: it is expected that the Call for Evidence will be issued in September 2025.The review of the minerals policy will also be informed by developments in the EU with the adoption of the Critical Raw Materials Act in March 2024, and the UK Critical Minerals Strategy 2022 and refresh of 2023.  Both set out a range of critical raw materials/minerals which are essential to support the green transition. The review will continue to consider the policy position in terms of both the development of a circular economy in minerals, and the robust regulation of exploration and primary production.The Department has received legal advice that indicates that due to the length of time it will take to complete the review of the legislative framework, a moratorium on mineral prospecting licensing would not be legal.
department: Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether, in preparing the revised Operational Protocol, consideration has been given to the principles behind the approach of nutrient neutrality in England and Wales, that have been upheld by the Court of Appeal in England and Wales, which allows projects to come forward as long as they do not lead to any increase in nutrient loading in designated sites, even if those sites are in unfavourable status already.
answer_text: In developing the revised Operational Protocol (currently subject to consultation, my officials were aware of and did consider the nutrient neutrality approach and the implications of related legal cases such as R (Wyatt) v Fareham BC and C G Fry & Son Ltd v Secretary of State for Levelling Up Housing and Communities [2023] EWHC 1622 (“Fry”). As you may be aware, the latter case has since been referred to the Supreme Court and was heard on the 17 and 18 February.  You may also be aware that the Office of Environmental Protection (OEP) has expressed concern over the approach proposed for use in England.These cases confirm that competent authorities are precluded from sanctioning development which is environmentally harmful and that statutory bodies are required to take steps to avoid further deterioration of our most valuable habitats and species. In the absence of measures needed to reverse the deterioration and promote restoration of protected sites, a ‘neutrality’ approach limits ambition only to preventing further deterioration, with no consideration of tackling existing pressures. This creates the risk of producing a ‘lose-lose’ scenario, whereby protected habitats will continue to remain in unfavourable condition in the long term and continue to create barriers to future development. That is why I want to develop a strategic and co-ordinated approach to the delivery of habitat management and restoration alongside emission reductions which will deliver room for sustainable development and improve the status of our protected sites. Regarding air quality, Site Nitrogen Action Plans (SNAPs) may provide a mechanism to deliver our duties to avoid deterioration caused by existing air quality pressures. With sufficient reduction in pressures on and improvement in the status of protected sites, a nutrient neutrality approach may, in future, become an acceptable general principle for assessment of air quality impacts across the wider countryside.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24002/22-27, how the proposed revised Operational Protocol will address a proposal for a replacement building which results in a net modelled reduction in emissions when compared with emissions from the existing building which it is replacing.
answer_text: Where an application demonstrates that the upgrading or replacement of existing facilities can deliver the requirements of environmental legislation, my Department will support that application. However, where the residual pollution from such an application continues to cause environmental damage, this would be contrary to those legal duties and permitting authorities would have difficulty granting approval. Of the 104 agricultural planning consultations received by my Department this year, only one application has provided evidence that shows there would be a reduction in polluting emissions.  After assessing the application using the interim operational protocol, NIEA has determined that there are no air quality concerns with that particular case.I have instructed my officials to investigate the appropriateness of implementing pollution reduction plans based on the strategic approach adopted by Natural England through Site Nitrogen Action Plans (SNAPs). SNAPs are specific to individual protected sites and are designed to identify key emission sources around the site, develop a pathway towards sustainable emission levels which support ecological recovery, and promote abatement and mitigation measures.  This would help to define the relative contributions of existing facilities to achieving emission reduction goals and support delivery of the Department’s protected sites favourable condition target in the recently adopted Environmental Improvement Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24002/22-27, whether a proposal that does not (i) lead to any increase in emissions when measured against existing baseline conditions; and (ii) impede or hinder the ability of other measures coming forward to address any exceedance of critical levels or loads would comply with the revised Operational Protocol.
answer_text: Where it can be demonstrated that a plan or project with its predicted emissions does not undermine or hinder the achievement of the conservation objectives for a protected site, it would comply with the revised operational protocol.		However, it should be noted that achievement of these conservation objectives (to reduce nitrogen deposition to levels at or below the critical load for the relevant habitat and achieve favourable condition) is dependent on ambitious reductions in polluting emissions affecting the site. Where emissions remain above the critical load and level and there is no strategy to achieve the aims of the conservation objectives in place, the ability for permitting authorities to give permissions will continue to be necessarily limited. 		It is for that reason that I have instructed my officials to investigate the appropriateness of implementing pollution reduction plans, based on the approach adopted by Natural England through Site Nitrogen Action Plans (SNAPs), these plans will be aimed at delivering sufficient reductions to ensure that favourable status is achieved, allowing any  reductions arising from a to be considered as part of or alongside a broader programme of nitrogen reduction measures which, if implemented, would lead to a restoration of favourable conservation status.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, including (i) how it is contributing to water quality improvements; (ii) the number of farms that have signed up to date; and (iii) the number of farmers that have completed the associated training to ensure effective use of the soil analysis data.
answer_text: Final registration for the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) rollout will open in June this year. Registration will be available to farmers in Zone 4 and those in Zones 1-3 not previously registered. Soil sampling of registered farms will take place between October 2025 and February 2026.The SNHS will provide farmers with baseline data on soil nutrient levels and farm carbon estimates. Soil nutrient data is provided to farmers approximately 4-6 weeks after their fields are sampled and it is anticipated that farm carbon estimates will be issued to farm businesses in 2026.   (i)  The SNHS provides farmers with individualised, field scale, data on soil nutrient levels, LiDAR derived runoff risk maps and CAFRE led training on interpreting and using their SNHS data. This enables farmers to make more informed nutrient management decisions helping increase nutrient use efficiency and reducing excess runoff to waterbodies.In addition, SNHS runoff risk mapping identifying overland flow pathways will be available for all Northern Ireland’s agricultural fields. Water quality monitoring has shown that a large proportion of the annual load of phosphorus to rivers occurs during short, heavy rainfall events. This risk mapping will facilitate development of targeted interventions at farm and catchment scale, helping reduce nutrients, particularly phosphorus, reaching our waters.(ii) To date, the number of farm businesses registered for the SNHS (Years 1, 2 and 3) is 18,660.(iii) In total, 5,774 individuals representing 5,702 farm businesses have completed the SNHS training to date.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) whether a different policy is required in relation to treating replacement buildings differently to new buildings within the Future Operational Protocol; and (ii) when a replacement building cuts ammonia emissions, the proposed strict thresholds should not apply.
answer_text: The legislation and resulting assessment methodology does not distinguish between different types of development. Different approaches to the environmental assessment of plans and projects can only be justified by scientific evidence. The existing pollution exceedances are the result of existing activities and developments; therefore all development types need to demonstrate that they will avoid deterioration of, and will not undermine the ability to achieve favourable conservation status of Northern Irelands most important natural habitats and species. As part of the process of Strategic Environmental Assessment, the environmental report on the Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol is currently out for public consultation.  I welcome the submission of new evidence to inform the revised Operational Protocol as part of that process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 23654/22-27, why no specific measures have been outlined to address the risk of carbon leakage resulting from increased mushroom imports, should local production decline despite current assurances regarding the continuation of the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme.
answer_text: I have been very clear that DAERA will support Producer Organisations (POs) with a head office in Northern Ireland (NI) undertaking a three-year programme under the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme (FVAS), until a suitable successor scheme is in place. This will provide valuable, continuing support and stability for the local mushroom sector. Since, there is no change in existing policy/support available to the sector over the next few years, no decline in local mushroom production is anticipated as a result of DAERA policies or the Agriculture Bill. Therefore, a carbon leakage assessment has not been carried out. On the contrary, there is an opportunity to improve environmental and economic outcomes from improvements to the existing scheme and/or its successor.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any actions his Department is taking to mitigate the impacts of climate change on water quality; and (ii) the data and monitoring being used to inform this work.
answer_text: My Department leads on the Executive’s cross cutting Climate Change agenda, working with all Departments and sectors to develop and implement Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan (CAP) and the Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP). DAERA uses climate change projections published by the Met Office (UKCP18). Climate Change is likely to exacerbate existing pressures acting upon the water environment due to the longer drier spells and more intense rainfall events. Water quality is a top priority for me as outlined in the Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP) and inclusion within the Programme for Government. Progress continues on the implementation of the Lough Neagh Action Plan, and it is important that the publication of the 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan progresses as soon as possible through the Executive. Measures within the 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan are designed to drive the improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland, including from the impacts of climate change. My Department monitors the water environment and regularly publishes updates as official statistics on the DAERA website: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/ni-water-framework-directive-statisticsScience and Innovation across my Department will also help to support the climate change agenda. Robust evidence is used to inform, monitor and evaluate policy development and help meet our statutory obligations. My Department supports research on climate change through AFBI and the Co-Centre for Climate, Biodiversity & Water. An ongoing DAERA commissioned project under the DAERA Directed AFBI research work programme is investigating the impacts of weather and climate change on nutrients and biological metrics in lakes in Northern Ireland. The project, entitled “Lake ecosystems – climate change risks factors and assessment of biological metrics” is due to complete in January 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 22788/22-27, to detail his plans for addressing the lack of resources to progress sheep support.
answer_text: The Future Agricultural Policy Decisions for Northern Ireland, announced by my predecessor in March 2022, did not include proposals for sheep support at that stage but indicated that work would be undertaken to explore options for support that would improve the overall performance and resilience of the sheep sector. On 29 January 2025 I announced the timeline for the roll out of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) over the next two years (subject to necessary business case cover and legislation in place). While I wish to see a sheep support scheme added to the SAP’s remit, delivering on this timeline is now the key focus for the resources available, particularly in relation to the Farming with Nature Package (FwN), which is my immediate priority. However, as the FwN Package and other schemes included in the current timeline move towards delivery, I will ensure that resource is diverted to progress co-design work on sheep sector support including development and approval of policy, business case and any required legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how his Department intends to support and promote the environmental benefits of grass-fed beef production as part of a Just Transition for the agri-food sector in meeting Climate Change Act 2022 targets; and (ii) how this approach will contribute to improving productivity, reducing emissions, and enhancing the north of Ireland's international reputation for sustainable food.
answer_text: I welcomed the registration of Irish Grass Fed Beef as a Protected Geographical Indication under the EU schemes. I am aware that further software development is necessary in order to verify the grass-fed nature of the beef produced in NI before it can be marketed as ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’.  My officials will work with the industry as to how developing this software could help to achieve Climate Change Act 2022 targets.In addition, my Department’s Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme is aimed at improving the efficiency of the beef sector and reducing greenhouse gas emissions, thereby contributing to meeting the targets set by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  By reducing the age at slaughter of clean beef animals, the Scheme encourages improvements in productivity and environmental performance to increase profitability, reduce emissions and enhance our reputation for sustainable food. CAFRE is providing training to help farm businesses understand the necessary changes to management practices needed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the six Sustainable Utilisation of Livestock Slurry (i) Phase 1; and (ii) Phase 2 schemes.
answer_text: Phase 1 of the Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) on the Sustainable Utilisation of Livestock Slurry (SULS) programme was completed in August 2023.  SULS Phase 2 commenced in October 2024 and aims to demonstrate the innovative concepts developed in Phase 1. Phase 2 was open to all Phase 1 suppliers and three were successful. Two suppliers have now signed contracts with a combined value of £8m. These suppliers are BH Estates and the Centre for Competitiveness. A third supplier is expected to be awarded a SULS Phase 2 contract very shortly. Considerable progress is being made and the SULS project is on target to meet its objectives, including the core objective to cumulatively remove 1,000 tonnes of excess phosphorus from NI annually by October 2027. The first stage of the project involves on-farm separation of slurry with the separated solid fraction being used as a feedstock for anaerobic digestion. As the project develops, phosphorus will be extracted from farm in the separated solid fraction, clean renewable energy will be generated and co-products from AD (such as organo mineral fertiliser, biogenic CO2 and low carbon cement) will be valorised and exported.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Development Proposal for Dean Maguirc College, Carrickmore.
department: Department of Education

answer_text: The opening date for the 2025 Entitlement Transfer Service will be Thursday 3 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he still intends to take the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol to the Executive for final approval, as laid out in the Draft Ammonia Strategy Consultation.
answer_text: The matter raised in your question, in respect of the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol, is under consideration, and I will make a decision in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) why his Department decided to consult on the Revised Operational Protocol after it was published on its website; and (ii) whether this was an oversight within his Department.
answer_text: The decision by my Department to consult on the Revised Operational Protocol after it was published on the DAERA website was in response to a request for clarity from stakeholders, and it was decided that  the scope of the consultation should be widened. The closing date has been extended to ensure sufficient time is provided for responses to this important step in the process of Strategic Environmental Assessment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to organise briefing sessions or public engagement sessions in relation to the consultation on the Revised Operational Protocol.
answer_text: The channel for the public to engage with the consultation on the Revised Operational Protocol is through consultation on the Environmental Report as part of the process of Strategic Environmental Assessment. This also invites responses on the associated documents including the Update on the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and the Revised Operational Protocol.  My Department does not intend to organise briefing sessions or public engagement sessions in relation to the consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 8475/22-27, to detail any steps his Department has taken to ensure that the duty to have due regard under Section 29 of the Climate Change Act 2022, which requires Departments to consider the special economic and social role of agriculture, including the distinct characteristics of biogenic methane, is integrated into decision-making processes for policies and proposals, beyond economic and carbon reduction assessments, particularly in relation to (i) maintaining agricultural output while meeting climate targets
answer_text: To meet DAERA’s obligation under the Climate Change Act, an evidence led and co-design approach to policy development has been adopted to ensure that policies and proposals to meet Climate Change targets contribute to the stronger resilience, improved environmental sustainability and enhanced productivity of our agriculture sector. This is particularly evident in the policies developed and launched at the end of January 2025 under the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he intends to balance the need for environmental betterment with the financial and practical constraints faced by farmers under the Operational Protocol.
answer_text: The new Operational Protocol will be developed, following consultation, to protect our sensitive habitats whilst enabling sustainable farm development. I recognise that farmers and the agri-food industry may require financial support to reduce ammonia emissions from new and existing developments, this is currently being considered as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 23654/22-27, whether the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme will remain open for the full three-year period following submission of a new operational programme for approval in September 2025.
answer_text: DAERA anticipates that eligible Producer Organisations will submit a three-year programme for approval in line with the maximum programme duration permitted under the existing Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme (FVAS) law (Article 33(1) of the CMO Regulation). This would mean any approved programmes would run from January 2026 to December 2028. However, the final programme duration may be subject to change in the event that a suitable successor scheme is developed and launched before the scheduled conclusion of approved programme(s). Any amendments to existing FVAS rules would require the successful passage of the Agriculture Bill and appropriate secondary legislation. Discussions are ongoing with relevant stakeholders on this basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total percentage reduction in artificial nitrogen use by farmers compared to five years ago.
answer_text: Table 1 shows the total nitrogen content of fertilisers purchased by the Northern Ireland agriculture sector for each year from 2019 to 2024. The data indicates that the quantity of artificial nitrogen used by the sector decreased from 72,308 tonnes in 2019 to 70,123 tonnes in 2024, representing a decrease of 3 per cent.  Fertiliser purchases are influenced by various factors including market and weather conditions. Table 1: Nitrogen content of fertiliser purchased by NI agriculture sectorYearTonnes201972,308202084,261202176,685202267,313202358,890202470,123Source: Statistical Review of Northern Ireland Agriculture & Northern Ireland Fertiliser Statistics.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 23174/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 23174/22-27 was answered on 7 March 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the new Tuberculosis Partnership Steering Group will produce a delivery plan for TB by the end of March 2025.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group (TBPSG) has met frequently since its establishment in late January 2025.  It is considering the Chief Veterinary Officer’s proposals in a spirit of co-design and is working with the Department to develop and implement a new delivery plan.  I understand that this work is progressing well and that the Group discussed in detail a proposed draft delivery plan at its meeting on 27 March 2025.  I am assured that this work is nearing completion, and I look forward to receiving final draft proposals shortly.It is my intention that this delivery plan will set out our priorities where action on this costly disease can be taken in the short term alongside setting out the steps required to enable other key actions to be implemented.  In some instances, this will require further public consultation or new legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the timeline for notifying successful applicants to the Central Good Relations Programme and CRC Core Funding Programme for 2025/2026.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the £31 million reduction in non ring-fenced resources funding in the 2025-26 budget compared to 2024-25 budget, (i) to provide a detailed breakdown of the specific programmes affected by this budgetary change; and (ii) whether (a) a rural needs assessment; and (b) an equality impact assessment was conducted to evaluate the impact of this budgetary change.
answer_text: I am responding on the basis that I believe the £31m reduction in Non Ring-fenced Resource DEL funding in the 2025-26 draft budget compared to 2024-25 budget has been sourced from Table 4.2 ‘Non-ringfenced resource budgets for NI departments various baseline positions’ of the NI Fiscal Council’s report ‘The NI Executive’s 2025-26 Draft Budget: an assessment’. The 2024-25 Final Plan figure of £627m is based on the Final January Monitoring position which includes in year allocations from HM Treasury for implementing the Windsor Framework as well as Executive in year allocations for Bovine Tuberculosis Compensation, operational costs and Lough Neagh. These allocations are not reflected in the draft 2025-26 opening budget figure of £596m within Table 4.2.  It is expected as in previous years for HM Treasury to confirm 2025-26 funding for implementing the Windsor Framework in the coming months.  This would increase the £596m for DAERA in 2025-26 along with any other Executive allocations at in year Monitoring Rounds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on a cap under the Farming With Nature Scheme, given concerns that without one it may primarily benefit large landowners rather than food producers.
answer_text: The Farming with Nature (FwN) Package forms part of the programme of change being progressed through the DAERA Sustainable Agriculture Programme. The use of a cap within the FwN Package is currently under consideration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) how prioritising large farms for Business Sustainability Groups (BSG) aligns with his Department's commitment to equality of opportunity under its equality scheme; and (ii) the contribution that small farms can make to BSGs, given their role in sustainability and innovation.
answer_text: Farming for Sustainability - Knowledge Transfer Scheme is an integral component of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP). It will provide support to all farm businesses across Northern Ireland in the form of broad information, training and peer-to-peer learning groups.  Support to farm businesses through peer-to-peer learning groups will be delivered in the form of Business Sustainability Groups (BSGs) and Themed Groups. Both offer the opportunity for farmers to use knowledge, co-operation and innovation to improve the sustainability and performance of their businesses. Farm size is not stipulated within the eligibility criteria for either group.However selection criteria devised for BSGs, considers farm size, age and gender and will only be used if the programme is oversubscribed. The selection criteria was developed in collaboration with the Agriculture Programme Stakeholder Group (APSG). In accordance with DAERAs obligations under section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, a screening exercise of the Farming for Sustainability - Knowledge Scheme has been completed.   The effects of the scheme at Northern Ireland level will not have an identifiable differential impact on the main equality categories.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the opening date for the 2025 Entitlement Trading Service; and (ii) any steps his Department is taking to ensure farmers are adequately informed about the process ahead of the 15 May 2025 deadline.
answer_text: I intend to open the 2025 Entitlement Transfer Service later in March.I have already communicated this to farmers, agents and agricultural consultants  by letter, electronic secure message,  press and the normal social media platforms. Similar communications will be issued when the opening date of the Entitlement Transfer Service is confirmed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 17467/22-27, whether his Department has engaged with planning authorities or stakeholders in the North to assess the potential implications of An Bord Pleanála’s October 2024 decision on agriculture development in Co. Cavan, particularly regarding slurry and manure application policies and their alignment with EU case law and recent decisions by the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: My Department has not engaged with planning authorities or stakeholders in relation to this decision.  Officials are not required to give weight to the decisions of An Bord Pleanála.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he proposes to address the issue of betterment in the operational protocol to ensure that farmers are encouraged and enabled to carry out environmental improvements that actively contribute to statutory targets for the reduction of ammonia.
answer_text: The term ‘betterment’ is often used loosely to describe improvements to developments that reduce emissions. However, in its correct application under environmental legislation, ‘betterment’ should be restricted to improvement over and above what would otherwise be required to avoid deterioration of designated sites. Essentially, a decision maker must demonstrate that the residual emissions will cause no adverse impact to the integrity of the designated site, and where this is the case, the Interim Operational Protocol would support the application. The proposed revised Operational Protocol also includes a mechanism to take account of betterment as part of any strategic reduction approaches which deliver statutory targets for ammonia and nature recovery.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to his decision to commence the Suckler Cow Scheme (SCS) on 1 April 2025, whether his decision has been equality screened under Section 75 of the 1998 Act to assess its impact on spring-calving farmers from January to March 2025 under the SCS who may be excluded from payments.
answer_text: An Equality Screening Exercise has been completed on the Suckler Cow Scheme and no differential impacts on Section 75 categories were identified.Farm businesses with Spring calving cows will not be excluded from payments. The Suckler Cow Scheme year runs from 1 April to 31 March. Those farmers with cows calving from January to March 2025 will be eligible for payments for year 1 of the Scheme providing their cows calve again within the first year of the Scheme and within the 415-day year 1 target. Analysis of NIFAIS data shows that 4% of suckler herds have calving events solely between January and March.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 22788/22-27, to detail (i) a timeline for when the sheep-focused co-design work will resume; and (ii) any specific measures being taken to ensure that staffing resources will not continue to delay this for sheep farmers.
answer_text: Work on sheep-focused co-design via the established Agricultural Policy Stakeholder Group forum, has been paused at present due to competing priorities. We will move forward with this work when staff resource is available to do so.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, under the Shared Island Initiative, to detail (i) the key objectives of the AgriFood Infrastructure Enabling Growth project; (ii) how it contributes to cross-border economic development in the agri-food sector; (iii) the specific challenges in the agri-food sector the project is designed to address; (iv) how the project aligns with the goals of the Shared Island Initiative; (v) the roles that local government authorities, both north and south, play in delivering this project; (vi) how the project promotes cross-border supply ch
answer_text: The AgriFood Infrastructure Enabling Growth project is an Irish Government Initiative, funded under the Shared Island Local Authority Funding Scheme.  The project is led by Galway County Council in partnership with Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council; Leitrim County Council and BIA Innovator Campus, Galway.  DAERA are not involved with this project and therefore cannot comment further.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has conducted a carbon leakage assessment on the impact of not providing adequate support for the mushroom sector under the horticultural pilot scheme.
answer_text: The Pilot Horticulture Scheme, which has been developed by CAFRE, seeks to provide education, knowledge transfer and innovation programme support for those entering and working within all sectors of Northern Ireland’s production horticulture industry including the mushroom sector. Therefore, I can confirm that a carbon leakage assessment on the impact of the Pilot Horticulture Scheme was not carried out.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he plans to prevent carbon leakage if mushroom imports increase due to local industry decline as a result of proposal for discretionary funding under the Agriculture Bill.
answer_text: I have been clear from the start that by seeking to make Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme (FVAS) support discretionary, I am not signalling any plans to end support for the horticulture sector here. Therefore, the Agriculture Bill, if enacted, would not of itself lead to any decline in local supply or increased mushroom imports. Nevertheless, I have listened to stakeholders’ concerns and recently written to the AERA Committee Chair and directly to key stakeholders, to confirm that I will not be closing the FVAS at the end of this year. This will allow Producer Organisations with a head office in Northern Ireland to submit a new operational programme for approval in September and officials are engaging with stakeholders on this basis. I have also instructed officials to begin preparatory work on a suitable replacement scheme to be developed in co-design with the horticulture sector, informed by the ongoing policy review of the current FVAS.That work will provide an opportunity to consider improvements to the existing FVAS to better meet the needs of the industry and, importantly, to deliver improved environmental and economic outcomes, including carbon reduction.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of the all-island Protected Geographical Indicator status for grass-fed cattle, including (i) how the North's participation is being facilitated; (ii) any progress since approval by the European Commission; and (iii) any outstanding issues affecting full recognition and benefits for food producers.
answer_text: In order for beef produced in NI to be marketed under the name 'Irish Grass Fed Beef', it needs to be verified that it meets the requirements set out in the Protected Geographical Indication’s technical specification. My understanding is that to make such verification possible, the major outstanding issue is that a grass-fed database will have to be developed. Geographical Indications are industry-led and it is therefore up to industry to fund any such software development in line with its desire for NI’s inclusion in the PGI. I am not aware that industry has made any progress to resolve this issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of reported dog attacks on sheep in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) local council area; and (ii) parliamentary constituency.
answer_text: My Department publishes a high-level summary of dog control related statistics each year from information collated from all 11 councils.  Under the Order, livestock worrying is classified as attacking, chasing or killing farmed animals, including cattle, pigs, poultry and equidae, as well as sheep. Therefore, my Department neither holds the information you are seeking nor is dog attack data recorded in the format you have requested.  You may wish to contact the relevant councils directly to determine if they can supply this information in this form.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any additional financial support measures that will be provided to poultry farmers affected by the avian influenza outbreak, particularly in light of the recent culling and the impact on the sector’s economic contribution.
answer_text: Although the recent avian influenza outbreak has had a direct impact on those individual businesses where birds have been culled, the impact at sector level is modest.  According to the most recent farm census total poultry numbers in Northern Ireland stands at close to 23.7 million with the most recent incursions of avian influenza requiring around 110k to be culled.The industry has mitigated and managed any impact on supply chains, and all involved, including my officials, are working as hard as possible to control the outbreak.I currently have no plans to introduce additional financial support measures, however my officials continue to monitor the situation closely. In the event of escalation and materialisation of significant impacts, appropriate measures will be considered, as necessary, to support this important sector of our local food production industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the £332.5 million funding commitment in the Draft Budget 2025-26 for agriculture is subject to further Executive approval or additional conditions for the allocation of the ring-fenced budget.
answer_text: On 19 December 2024 the Executive agreed that this funding should be provided as an earmarked allocation to my Department for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development as part of its agreement on the Draft Budget 2025-26.  This is subject to Executive approval of the Final Budget 2025-26 in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department is ensuring (i) effective disease control; (ii) cross-border coordination; and (iii) biosecurity enforcement in response to the confirmed high pathogenicity avian influenza cases across the north and south of Ireland.
answer_text: (i)  My Department has well-established, robust disease control contingency plans in place to manage the risk of disease incursions to protect our livestock, farmers and wider industry.  This includes a Notifiable Epizootic Avian Disease Control Strategy which outlines procedures for responding to suspect cases.  Contingency plans are regularly rehearsed and include the activation of the Central Epizootic Disease Control Centre and the Local Epizootic Disease Control Centre, declaring relevant disease control zones, confirming measures within the zones and carrying out certain actions at an infected premises which are mandatory as legislated for in EU and domestic law. (ii)  I have been in regular contact with Martin Heydon TD, the Minister for Agriculture, Food and the Marine in the south about the ongoing outbreaks of avian influenza (AI) in Northern Ireland (NI).  The Chief Veterinary Officer and his team have also been maintaining close contact with their counterparts in the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine as the situation develops.  This collaborative working will help ensure that the industry across both jurisdictions is protected as much as possible as we progress through the remainder of this AI risk period. (iii)  As you will be aware, I introduced an Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) across NI on Saturday 18 January 2025.  The measures in the AIPZ include stringent, mandatory biosecurity measures to help prevent the spread of the disease from wild birds or another source to poultry.  Bird keepers, whether commercial or backyard, must by law maintain high biosecurity standards and fully comply with all requirements that have been implemented to control this devastating disease.I subsequently took the decision to introduce a housing order, effective from Monday 17 February 2025, for all poultry and captive birds.  I worked closely with Minister Heydon to ensure similar steps were taken in the south.Reports of any non-compliance to the mandatory biosecurity measures and the housing order will be considered by my Department and follow up action including enforcement will be taken where necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an assessment has been made of the number of producer organisations based in Great Britain that are eligible for support from the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme through his Department.
answer_text: Under the existing assimilated law governing the legacy Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme in the UK, DAERA is the appropriate authority responsible for providing financial assistance for the operational programmes of Producer Organisation(s) whose head office is located in Northern Ireland. Article 14A of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2017/891 sets out the rules on determining the head office as follows: Article 14AHead office of a producer organisation1. The head office of a producer organisation must be located in the constituent nation in which the organisation achieves the majority of the value of marketed production calculated in accordance with Articles 22 and 23.But the head office may be established in the constituent nation where the majority of producer members are located, if both the appropriate authority for that constituent nation and the appropriate authority for the constituent nation referred to in the first subparagraph consent.2. Notwithstanding paragraph 1, the head office:(a) may not move to another constituent nation until the end of the implementation of an ongoing operational programme; and(b) may be maintained in a particular constituent nation for as long as the appropriate authority for that constituent nation and the appropriate authorities referred to in paragraph 1 consent.On that basis, two Producer Organisations who currently have their head office in England may be eligible for DAERA financial assistance under the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme after December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has conducted a detailed risk assessment to evaluate his Department’s potential liability in the event that the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme provisions remain unchanged.
answer_text: As you will be aware DAERA is currently completing a review of the legacy EU Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme. In tandem, officials are finalising budget projections for 2025/26 and preparing for the next Spending Review.		As part of these processes, officials have been assessing DAERA's potential financial liability under a range of scenarios, including continuation of the existing Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme (FVAS) provisions. They are also taking into consideration emerging policy in other parts of the UK and Ireland, as well as evolving structures in the horticulture industry.		In the absence of proposed new powers under the Agriculture Bill, DAERA could become financially liable for increased FVAS support that would benefit both GB and NI members of UK Producer Organisations in the mushroom sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an assessment has been made on the administration costs of the current Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme for the benefit of the sole north of Ireland-based producer organisations.
answer_text: As you will be aware DAERA is currently completing a review of the legacy EU Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme (FVAS). In tandem, officials are finalising budget projections for 2025/26 and preparing for the next Spending Review.  As part of these processes, officials are assessing DAERA's potential financial liability under a range of scenarios.  They are also taking into consideration developing policy in other parts of the UK and Ireland, as well as evolving structures in the horticulture industry. This assessment covers both scheme funding and administration costs.  There is currently only one producer organisation with its head office in Northern Ireland, but that position could change.  Given the additional requirements for processing of claims, payments and checks, the overall costs of scheme administration would be expected to increase with the number of producer organisations funded.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an estimate has been made on the potential administration costs for a future Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme with an increased number of producer organisations.
answer_text: As you be aware from the response to AQW 23175/22-27, the Rural Payments Agency will no longer deliver the devolved CMO schemes after December 2025 and DAERA will need to cover its own costs for future administration of agricultural support programmes, including continuation of the existing FVAS. Officials are assessing DAERA's potential financial liability under a range of scenarios.  They are also taking into consideration emerging policy in other parts of the UK and Ireland, as well as evolving structures in the horticulture industry. This assessment covers both scheme funding and administration costs.DAERA officials have obtained an estimate of average administration costs per UK producer organisation (PO) which may be used for budgeting purposes. However, each PO and its operational programme will be different. In addition, subject to the successful passage of the Agriculture Bill, there may be scope to streamline some of the current operational rules of the FVAS.These costs will be included in DAERA financial planning exercises and subject to appropriate business case(s) cover.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether (i) the Rural Payments Agency will continue to administer the (a) Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme; and (b) other food promotion and information schemes in the future; and (ii) this will include covering administration costs.
answer_text: The Rural Payments Agency will no longer deliver devolved Common Market Organisation (CMO) schemes after December 2025. In practice, this refers to the legacy EU Fruit & Vegetable Aid Scheme only as there are no longer any live programmes under the legacy EU Agri-Food Information and Promotions Scheme.DAERA will need to cover its own costs for future administration of agricultural support programmes, including for the continuation of the existing FVAS from 2026. These costs will be included in DAERA financial planning exercises and subject to appropriate business case(s) cover.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will provide additional funding for the repair and maintenance of rural roads.
answer_text: The rural road network makes up around 75% of the total network in the North and therefore a significant proportion of the overall budget allocated for Structural and Essential Maintenance in 2025-26 will be targeted towards these roads.There is a rolling programme of resurfacing across the road network, including rural roads, and my Department works hard to ensure that available funding is targeted at areas of greatest need. For a number of years now my Department has also allocated budget to a Roads Recovery Fund, which is primarily targeted towards the lower tier network. In 2024/25, some £10m has been allocated to the Roads Recovery Fund, of which £8m has been directly targeted towards rural roads. These improvements are targeting many short lengths of mainly rural roads that are in particularly poor condition. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and I recognise that many rural roads are in need of repairs as a result of this under funding. The level of maintenance and resurfacing of rural roads that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2025-26 final budget determination for my Department, however, I am giving careful consideration to continuing this important funding for mainly rural roads as part of my deliberations in regards the 2025-26 budget process.
department: Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of occasions he has issued a ministerial directive since taking office, including the specific issues these directives address.
answer_text: I have not issued any Ministerial Directions since taking up office.  As Minister I have not at any point asked my Accounting Officer to take a course of action that would put her into conflict with the personal responsibilities she carries as Accounting Officer.  I am committed to ensuring that public money is used wisely and disbursed within the rules set out in Managing Public Money Northern Ireland (MPMNI).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any progress made on the development of a sheep scheme; and (ii) how staffing resource can be justified as a barrier to its implementation, as reported in the Irish Farmers Journal on 15 February 2025.
answer_text: Whilst it is planned that sheep-focused co-design will take place via the established Agricultural Policy Stakeholder Group forum, work on this has been paused at present due to competing priorities. A number of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme measures will however be available to sheep farmers including the planned Farm Sustainability Transition Payment, Carbon Footprinting Project, Farming for Sustainability-Knowledge Transfer, Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme and Farming with Nature as they come on stream.It has been necessary over recent months to prioritise staffing resource to progress key elements of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme and we will move forward with the sheep-focused co-design work when staff resource is available to do so.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Bovine TB Eradication Strategy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how the current budget constraints, including the reported lack of funding for avian influenza and bluetongue, will impact the ongoing implementation of the all-island Animal Health and Welfare Strategy; and (ii) any contingency plans in place to address potential future disease outbreaks.
answer_text: I can confirm that whilst the budgetary position remains challenging my Departmental officials continue to work closely and frequently with colleagues in DAFM on all elements of animal health and welfare both through the structures of the North South Ministerial Council and other well-established fora as appropriate. This has been demonstrated recently in the collaborative response to both the risk of FMD presented by the confirmed case in Germany and the recent outbreaks of avian influenza detected in Northern Ireland with officials liaising with counterparts in DAFM on a regular basis. My Department has well established robust disease control contingency plans in place to manage the risk of disease incursions to protect our livestock, farmers and wider industry.  These contingency plans are regularly rehearsed and initialactions include restricting suspect premises, conducting veterinary investigations, taking samples from susceptible species for testing at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute.On confirmation susceptible species on the holding are humanely culled and the infected premises is cleansed and disinfected.  Disease control zones are established that restrict the movement of animals, products and things likely to spread disease.  The size of the disease control zones and the measures within is based on extensive research and risk assessments to reduce the risk of disease spread.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 20490/22-27, (i) whether he will give further consideration to the Ulster Farmers’ Union’s proposal to delay the Suckler Cow Scheme to 1 January 2026, in light of ongoing concerns from farm businesses, particularly those with spring-calving herds; and (ii) to detail any factors that would influence a decision to revise the scheme’s start date further.
answer_text: My officials recently met with UFU representatives to discuss their concerns. Informed by that discussion, I have agreed that the Suckler Cow Scheme should commence on 1 April 2025.		I am not therefore planning on revising the Scheme’s start date and subject to budget and legislation the Scheme will commence on 1 April 2025.	A series of Awareness Events will be held across Northern Ireland during late February and March to highlight the benefits of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, including the Suckler Cow Scheme, and encourage farm businesses to take appropriate preparatory actions to participate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 21337/22-27, to detail (i) what constitutes exceptional circumstances under the legal framework for badger culling; and (ii) whether, based on the Chief Veterinary Officer’s review of bovine TB, the current situation meets those criteria.
answer_text: There is currently no primary or secondary legislation in operation which specifically provides for the culling of badgers in Northern Ireland to address bTB disease levels and spread. In October 2023, a judicial review quashed the former Minister’s decision to take forward a non-selective cull of badgers in areas of high badger density and high bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) incidence in cattle as part of the Northern Ireland bTB Eradication Strategy. Consequently, my Department currently does not have a wildlife intervention policy.My officials are reflecting on the issues and gaps identified by the ruling in order to provide me with detailed advice on potential wildlife intervention options.  This involves looking at the latest scientific evidence; the potential legislative options relating to an intervention; and updating figures relating to how much these interventions could cost.  At my request and in parallel with this work, the Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) has undertaken a comprehensive review of bTB in Northern Ireland which considers fully all the factors which are contributing to our current high rate of disease. The CVO review consists of almost 40 proposals across three key pillars – the importance of ensuring that we all – government and industry - work in partnership to reduce disease levels; proposals to limit and prevent the spread of TB from cattle to cattle; and proposals to address the transmission of bTB from wildlife to cattle.This review has been published and can be viewed at the link below:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/chief-veterinary-officer-review-bovine-tuberculosis-northern-ireland-november-2024 As I have made clear, any decision I take will be science led. I would also reaffirm that a full public consultation will be required before I am in the position to take an informed decision on any wildlife intervention. In addition, the implementation of any wildlife intervention may also likely require legislative change.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any plans his Department has to support the regeneration of rural villages and small towns.
department: Department for Communities

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will intervene to ensure Max, a puppy in the care of Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council, is released into a foster home pending adulthood, with breed assessment deferred until he matures given the lack of evidence confirming whether he is of pit bull type.
answer_text: Local councils have responsibility for enforcing the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 (The Dogs Order). My Department has no legal power to direct councils as to how their statutory obligations are fulfilled.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) what constitutes exceptional circumstances under the legal framework for badger culling; and (ii) whether the situation in relation to bovine TB meets the criteria.
answer_text: In October 2023, a judicial review quashed the former Minister’s decision to take forward a non-selective cull of badgers in areas of high badger density and high bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) incidence in cattle as part of the Northern Ireland bTB Eradication Strategy. Consequently, my Department currently does not have a wildlife intervention policy. My officials are reflecting on the issues and gaps identified by the ruling in order to provide me with detailed advice on potential wildlife intervention options.  This involves looking at the latest scientific evidence; the potential legislative options relating to an intervention; and updating figures relating to how much these interventions could cost.  At my request and in parallel with this work, the Chief Veterinary Officer has also undertaken a comprehensive review of bTB in Northern Ireland which considers fully all of the factors which are contributing to our current high rate of disease.  This includes role of wildlife in disease maintenance and spread.  This review has been published and can be viewed at the link below:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/chief-veterinary-officer-review-bovine-tuberculosis-northern-ireland-november-2024
