question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the public competition to recruit a new Victims Commissioner.
answer_text: The selection panel have recently completed interviews with all eligible candidates for the competition.  We will now meet with the candidates deemed appointable by the panel over the coming weeks.  An appointment will be made as soon as possible after that process has concluded.  The appointment process is regulated by the Commissioner for Public Appointments and, as such, it must comply with the Code of Practice for Ministerial Public Appointments.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the next stage of the A5 Western Transport Corridor project.
department: Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any steps his Department is taking to assess the feasibility and implications of banning cages for laying hens;  and (ii) how he plans to balance animal welfare considerations with the potential economic impact on the poultry sector.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage with colleagues across the UK and Ireland to monitor the progression of any plans for a complete ban on cages for laying hens. However, I am aware that many producers here have already stopped using cages for laying hens in response to requirements of large food supermarkets. Therefore, I have no plans to introduce legislation for a ban on cages at this juncture. Should my position change, a full analysis of implications and economic impact to the industry would be carried out.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the recent outbreak of foot and mouth disease in Germany; and (ii) any measures being implemented to ensure the agriculture sector is protected from potential risks, particularly in terms of import controls, biosecurity, and contingency planning.
answer_text: The emergence of Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD) in Germany is of significant concern, not only for our livestock owners but also for the entire agricultural sector across the UK, Republic of Ireland and the EU.On notification of the outbreak in Germany my officials immediately carried out a tracing exercise to identify if any susceptible species, germplasm or products of animal origin had been imported to Northern Ireland from Germany within an established risk window.Furthermore, portal controls in line with the requirements laid down in legislation are implemented at points of entry to Northern Ireland.  These controls include identity and physical checks on consignments and the screening of passenger luggage and parcels arriving through airports.  In addition, DAERA implement a system of certification verification to ensure that animals and products arriving here have been certified in line with the required animal and public health attestations.My Department has well established robust disease control contingency plans in place to manage the risk of disease incursions and protect our farmers and food security. The FMD response plan outlines clear procedures for responding to suspect cases, including rapid diagnostic testing, protocols to restrict animal movements and the establishment of disease control zones to mitigate for onward spread of the disease. This plan was tested extensively during a UK national exercise in 2018. Communications reminding livestock keepers of the necessity of adhering to high levels of biosecurity are being regularly disseminated. In addition, veterinary practitioners and livestock keepers have been urged to remain alert to any unusual symptoms and report these immediately to a veterinarian or directly to DAERA.Officials are maintaining close and frequent communication with their counterparts across these islands to facilitate a comprehensive response. Finally, I updated my Ministerial colleagues at the Executive meeting on 16 January 2025 on the developments in Germany and informed all MLAs via a Written Ministerial Statement published 17 January 2025. This statement is available at https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/globalassets/documents/official-reports/written-ministerial-statements/2022---2027/bv169_daera-17012025.pdf and provides further information on the actions taken to date and future steps to be taken.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) why his Department is proceeding with the Suckler Cow Scheme from 1 April 2025, despite concerns raised by the Ulster Farmers’ Union (UFU) about the impact on farm businesses, particularly spring-calving herds, and the lack of consultation and communication with farmers; and (ii) whether he will consider the UFU’s proposal to delay the scheme to 1 January 2026.
answer_text: My officials recently met with representatives from the UFU for a constructive discussion on their concerns.	The Suckler Cow Scheme was initially planned to commence on 1 January 2025.  However, the UK budget announcement on 30 October 2024 removing the earmarked status for the budget for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development along with the lack of multi-year funding from HM Treasury, created a degree of uncertainty and prevented the delivery of the communications necessary to prepare farm businesses for the introduction of the Scheme in January 2025.Therefore, subject to budget and legislation, I decided to move the opening of the Suckler Cow Scheme to 1 April 2025 to allow time for communications to take place and enable farm businesses to fully avail of the Scheme and maximise its uptake. More details will follow soon.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) when his Department plans to establish the new bovine TB partnership, as outlined in the Chief Veterinary Officer's November 2024 review of bovine TB; and (ii) whether representatives from the farming community will be included in its development and decision-making process.
answer_text: Many individuals and groups are directly involved in or are impacted by bovine Tuberculosis (bTB).   It is therefore vital that an effective partnership working model is established between government and the farming industry alongside environmental, veterinary and other stakeholders as we collectively seek to tackle bTB in Northern Ireland.I have therefore agreed the formation of a new TB Partnership Steering Group (TBPSG).The TBPSG is intended to promote shared ownership, responsibility, coordination and direction setting. Ultimately creating the drive and concerted effort needed for progressing disease eradication. At this juncture, twelve organisations/groups have been invited to nominate one representative each to attend meetings of the TBPSG. To date, the following have agreed to participate in the TBPSG;Association of Veterinary Surgeons Practising in Northern Ireland Dairy Council for Northern Ireland Northern Ireland Agricultural Producers’ Association Northern Ireland Badger Group Northern Ireland Livestock Auctioneers’ Association North of Ireland Veterinary Association Public Health Agency Ulster Farmers’ Union Ulster Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ulster Wildlife Officials are currently progressing arrangements for the inaugural meeting of the TBPSG, which is anticipated to meet for its inaugural meeting before the end of January.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she intends to revisit the criminal legal aid payment.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 11 - AQO 1327/22-27		ANSWERI advised the Assembly in my statement on 2 December of plans to increase fees payable for public legal services in criminal and civil and family proceedings.Work is continuing at pace and I plan to consult on proposals later this month. As also advised in December, I also intend to initiate a root and branch review of the remuneration frameworks for legal aid to examine the work we are asking practitioners to undertake, and the added value and the fees that should attach to ensure fair, proportionate remuneration which is essential to an effective justice system.
department: Department of Justice

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide clarity on his Department's position in relation to securing a ring-fenced budget for agriculture, like Pillar 1 Direct Payments, to ensure continued support for the sector given the ongoing challenges and changing agricultural policy post-Brexit.
answer_text: In light of the change in treatment as a result of the Chancellor’s Budget on 30 October 2024 I formally asked the Minister of Finance that the £332.5 million Resource DEL that has been baselined in the block grant is treated as ‘Executive Earmarked’ funding for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development purposes.On 19 December 2024 the Executive agreed to my request to earmark this funding to my Department as part of the wider agreement on the Draft Budget 2025-26.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the procurement process for carbon calculators to assist farmers in measuring the carbon footprint of their farms; and (ii) whether these tools can also support efforts to attain Protected Geographical Indication status for grass-fed cattle.
answer_text: The procurement competition to appoint a Carbon Calculator for the Carbon Footprinting Project is ongoing. A legal challenge was brought following the issue of the intent to award letter for the Carbon Calculator contract. As this is now the subject of legal proceedings, the Department is unable to comment further.There is potential for the carbon calculator to provide support towards attainment of ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’ PGI status. It remains up to industry to propose how such a crossover might be accomplished.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the development and implementation of a Just Transition fund for Agriculture.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture but I first need certainty on the level of funding being made available in the years ahead . The nature and scale of a future bespoke Just Transition Fund for Agriculture will be determined by emerging needs and the advice from the Just Transition Commission. The level of funding available will also need to be considered in the context of budgetary pressures and priorities across the NI Executive over the years ahead but it is clear to me that the level of change required must be delivered not just with determination and ambition but also with fairness.I will therefore be considering, in conjunction with Executive colleagues, the best approach to take to deliver this and to the other requirements placed upon us by the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, learning where appropriate from the experience of others. As a start, on 19 December 2024 the Executive agreed a £12.3m Capital DEL Earmarked allocation for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture as part of its Draft Budget 2025-26.As we work to finalise the 2025-26 Budget, informed by the responses to the consultation, and as we prepare for the outcome of the Spending Review, I shall be making the case for further allocations to support the just transition we all want to see.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail future policy and support plans for the horticulture sector in the context of his Department's Green Growth Strategy.
answer_text: Although the proposed Green Growth Strategy (GGS) is not sector specific the Northern Ireland (NI) horticulture industry has a significant role to play in meeting the strategy’s vision of developing a net zero emissions, nature rich society where we are reaping the economic, social, health and environmental benefits from a thriving green economy.DAERA’s new Farm Support and Development Programme (FSDP) is being co-designed with the Northern Ireland industry and other key environmental stakeholders. The aims of the GGS have been considered in the FSDP’s development from implementation to evaluation. Within the FSDP a new Pilot Horticulture Programme is being developed which is planned to launch during 2025. Pilot Horticulture SchemeThe Pilot Horticulture Scheme will provide NI growers with focused facilitation, agronomy, business and supply chain expertise as well as funding support for innovation through three pilot proposals: Sustainable Sector Growth Groups, Innovation Driver and Support, and a New Growers Academy. Sustainable Sector Growth Groups – aims to increase productivity by providing specialist agronomy, business development and supply chain professional advice to participating self-selected groups of growers incentivising them to share information and explore market opportunities collectively. This will help them develop business specific growth plans to meet growth targets over a four-year implementation period. Innovation Driver and Support Pilot – will provide growers with viable ideas with support for investment in systems, technology and crops that are new to NI to enable improved productivity, sustainability and resilience. New Growers Academy - will provide the opportunity for new growers to attend professional development training, education and knowledge transfer activities and events that will allow them to develop the knowledge and skills required to start a horticulture enterprise or improve their existing horticulture businesses’ productivity, sustainability and resilience. They will also receive agronomy, business and supply chain advice and complete a Level 2 qualification in horticulture.These proposals aim to help foster the productivity, sustainability, resilience and supply chain integration  of the NI production horticulture sector through improved collaboration, innovation,  education and knowledge transfer.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of confirmed cases of blackleg in cattle in each of the last five years, broken down by county.
answer_text: Blackleg disease is not notifiable in Northern Ireland for any species, including cattle.  The Department therefore does not hold the information requested.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the analysis and impact assessment of the British Government’s inheritance tax proposals, particularly in relation to their implications for farmers, will be completed and published.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has undertaken a deeper analysis which builds on the original analysis on the impacts of the inheritance tax changes on local farms, that was published in the Assembly Library on 6th November 2024.  This deeper analysis takes into account residential property, farm buildings, machinery and livestock as well as land. This analysis was published on the Department’s website on 10th December 2024 and can be found at  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/news/new-analysis-shows-half-ni-farms-potentially-impacted-by-inheritance-tax-changes.The analysis finds that when taking these other factors into consideration, the average land value estimations increase from £15,000/acre to £21,000/acre, resulting in around half of NI farms being impacted by the policy change.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 16725/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 16725/22-27 was answered on 29 November 2024.
department: Department for Communities

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his capital investment plans for Dean Maguirc College, Carrickmore.
department: Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any plans his Department has for investment in rural settlements.
answer_text: The Department invests considerable resources in delivering a wide range of services to the public throughout Northern Ireland in both rural and urban settings.We deliver this investment both directly and through our Arms Length Bodies.It includes ensuring the availability of good quality and affordable housing, delivery of social security, child maintenance and pensions, supporting the Voluntary and Community Sector and a range of investments in the public realm, heritage, sports, arts, language, culture and much more.My key priority is to deliver real, positive and lasting change for all the people in Northern Ireland by focusing on programmes and policies that are transformative for people’s lives and for our society.This investment supports people, builds communities and shapes places.The key activities that we are striving to deliver during 2024-25 are detailed in the Department’s Business Plan.
department: Department for Communities

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any grants and funding support available to farmers to help address financial pressures and invest in sustainable practices; and (ii) any information on upcoming programs or changes to existing support for farmers.
answer_text: I fully appreciate the need to support farmers to ensure they can operate sustainably while playing their full part in the action needed to protect and enhance our environment, improve animal health and reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Considerable work is ongoing through co-design with the Northern Ireland agricultural industry and other key food and environmental stakeholders to introduce a transformational new Farm Support and Development Programme to  support farmers on this journey. The Programme is comprised of a suite of schemes and measures that will help businesses no matter where they farm to maximise the sustainable returns they can achieve from the assets at their disposal.My Department hopes to be able to provide clarification on the changes in relation to the new farm support payments in the near future, subject to underpinning legislation being passed and confirmation of future budget.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 17036/22-27, (i) to clarify whether his Department’s decision to revise the Equality and Disability Duties Screening Report, rather than undertake a full EQIA to consider the impact on groups defined by religion and political opinion, complies with their equality scheme and commitments to transparency and accountability; and (ii) to detail what specific data was collected to support the conclusion of no differential impact.
answer_text: Impact assessment is an ongoing process which requires screening documents to be revisited and revised where significant changes are made to the policy or strategy. Detailed consideration of stakeholder responses to the draft Ammonia Strategy led to significant changes being made to the proposed mandatory measures in the draft Ammonia Strategy including the removal of some of the proposed measures. The revised Equality Screening Report will be published alongside the updated Ammonia Strategy and other revised impact assessments together with a public consultation on the Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) Environmental Report for the revised Ammonia Strategy.  As the revised Equality Screening reflects the changes made to the revised Ammonia Strategy including the removal of proposed mandatory measures, no specific data was required to be collected to support the conclusion of no differential impact.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of An Bord Pleanála's October 2024 decision on agricultural development in Co. Cavan, to outline how this planning case could inform his Department's approach to supporting sustainable agriculture and balancing the needs of agriculture development with environmental considerations.
answer_text: My officials have regard to all relevant caselaw when providing conservation advice to planning authorities. I note with interest the approach of An Bord Pleanála. However, my Department’s statutory advice regarding consideration of slurry and manure application at planning stage, where necessary, is consistent with UK and EU caselaw and recent decisions by the Planning Appeals Commission in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the recently announced changes to the inheritance tax threshold and the lack of a ring-fenced budget for agriculture in the Chancellor’s Budget 2024, to outline what specific actions he will take to support family farmers to ensure continued viability of the agriculture sector.
answer_text: The recent announcement in the Budget regarding the limitation of agricultural property relief and business property relief at a rate of 100% to a combined value of £1m has caused great concern in the agricultural community.  I have expressed my concerns to the NI Secretary of State and urged him to reconsider this and engage with the farming community to respond to the concerns being raised.  I will continue to ask UK Government Ministers to engage more with the farming community on the issue and reconsider the approach taken.The decision that was made for funding for farming and fishing to no longer be ring-fenced and to come as part of the block grant is a concern for me.  I want to see that funding ring-fenced for Northern Ireland and I will work with Executive colleagues to seek to secure that.  Moving forward this funding will be essential in delivering my priorities of tackling climate change, protecting our natural environment and supporting a sustainable, resilient and productive agri-food sector through the delivery of my Department’s transformational new Farm Support And Development Programme.  I will do all that I can to support the farming industry in Northern Ireland with the resources that I have.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the measures being taken to address the 101 agricultural planning cases that require further information; (ii) how his Department will ensure timely resolution to avoid further delays; and (iii) an update on the backlog of agricultural planning consultations, specifically addressing the steps being taken to expedite the six delayed cases and ensure future consultations meet the 21 day statutory target.
answer_text: (i) Since the 19 December 2023 when the Department instructed the NIEA to stop using the Ammonia Operational Protocol, NIEA has receipted an agricultural planning caseload of 314.The Agency has responded to 284 cases, 178 of which have been assessed under the case by case.  Of these 178 responses, 107 cases have been identified as requiring further information. Following a request for further information, the consultation is closed off. It is a matter for the planning authority as to when or if they reconsult the Department prior to making its decision. We will consider each consultation when it is received from the planning authority with the additional information that is required for us to provide advice on the application.(ii) I want to assure you that the delays experienced are taken seriously, and officials are taking a number of steps to reduce the delays including;Securing staff resource to work on these applications; Streamlining the process internally; and Engaging with Planning Authorities to ensure all the necessary supporting information is submitted with the application. (iii) There are currently 30 agricultural consultations with the Department, with 7 being in a backlog and 23 cases still within the 21-day statutory target.  As set out at (ii) above, my officials are taking steps to reduce delays and meet the 21 day target for responses and will continue to focus efforts on responding to open consultations as efficiently and effectively as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the regulations to control and manage predator populations; and (ii) how these regulations balance wildlife conservation with the protection of agricultural interests.
answer_text: Predators are a natural and critical component of our biodiversity and of any balanced and functioning ecosystem. Many predatory bird and mammal populations are still recovering from historic persecution and reappearing in areas from which they had been exterminated. There is no legislation specific to predators nor, as applied, to the control and management of their populations. The Wildlife Order (Northern Ireland) 1985 (as amended) provides protections for species named in the schedules, including all predatory birds and some predatory mammals, also prohibiting certain methods of taking or killing them. Species considered particularly significant at a European level receive protection through the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc)(Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007. (ii)The above legislation makes provision for specified activities that would otherwise be unlawful to be carried out under licence from DAERA, subject to licence conditions, thereby balancing conservation and other interests.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the spread, impact and containment efforts for bluetongue disease; and (ii) to detail the measures that are recommended to protect livestock.
answer_text: From 26 August 2024 to 30 October 2024, there have been 148 premises confirmed with Bluetongue Virus Serotype 3 (BTV-3) in England and two cases confirmed in Wales.  The two cases in Wales were found in sheep moved legally to Wales from the East of England.  Further sampling and testing in Wales indicates that the disease is not spreading locally.The Restricted Zone (RZ), where movement restrictions are in place, has been extended and now covers 20 counties in England.  Animals can move into the RZ, and within the RZ, without a licence.  Animals can move to a designated slaughterhouse outside of the zone using a general licence.  For all other moves, keepers must apply for a specific licence.The main impact on Northern Ireland (NI) continues to be that movements of animals from Great Britain (GB) to NI remain suspended.Details of the latest situation in GB, including a map of the control zones, can be accessed at https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/bluetongue-information-and-guidance-for-livestock-keepers#latest-situationWith respect to recommended measures to protect livestock, my officials are actively engaged in disseminating information through various channels, including DAERA social media platforms and stakeholder meetings to heighten awareness of the disease and its clinical signs. I strongly encourage livestock keepers to report suspect cases to my Department immediately and I continue to emphasise the need for responsible sourcing of animals and germplasm.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 21341/17-22, to detail (i) the progress made since 2021 to increase the LiDAR-based carbon stock estimation project; and (ii) any new findings or insights that have emerged in relation to carbon management techniques for hedgerows and trees.
answer_text: (i) I can confirm that my department commissioned a LiDAR survey of the entire land area of Northern Ireland in 2022. The LiDAR survey data can only be collected when leaves are off trees and hedges and during specific weather conditions, for example, when there is no cloud cover. Since 2022 a significant amount of data has been collected and it is anticipated that the LiDAR survey will be completed and processed by October 2025. The LiDAR data will be used to provide participants in the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) with farm maps which show the estimated carbon stored in trees and hedges above ground (above ground biomass) and they will also be provided with data estimate on the soil carbon stocks from the soil sampling and analysis.  These farm maps will be completed for all Scheme participants by Summer 2026.(ii)  The primary effort to date on the above ground biomass estimation has focused on the complex interpretation of the acquired LiDAR datasets and the development of a robust baseline estimate of above ground biomass.  Once this baseline is generated, it will allow the development of more tailor-made advice and management options.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 16726/22-27, to detail (i) why revisions were made to the Equality and Disability Duties Screening Report for the draft Ammonia Strategy rather than proceeding with an Equality Impact Assessment, particularly given the potential impact on religion and political opinion; (ii) any specific information gathered to conclude there would be no differential impact among these specific Equality groups; and (iii) why a thorough investigation into the draft Ammonia Strategy policy’s potential consequences has not taken pl
answer_text: The draft Ammonia Strategy was published and underwent public consultation from 4 January to 3 March 2023, accompanied by an Equality and Disability Duties Screening Report. Following consideration of stakeholder responses to the consultation on the draft Ammonia Strategy, a number of changes have been made to the mandatory measures proposed in the draft Ammonia Strategy, and a revised Ammonia Strategy is being prepared for publication. The Equality and Disability Duties Screening Report has been revised to reflect the changes to the mandatory measures and is being prepared for publication. and (iii) The revised Equality and Disability Duties Screening Report has been produced in line with the published guidance, namely “Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 - A Guide for Public Authorities, 2010”, published by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland.The revised Ammonia Strategy and Impact Assessment Reports will be published later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the proposed changes to Agriculture Property Relief by the British Government and the impact on family farms that rely on this support; and (ii) to detail any engagement his Department has had with the British Government to raise concerns about consequences for small family farms.
answer_text: His Majesty’s Treasury does not consult in advance of fiscal events, and therefore I was not aware of the detail of any changes made to Agricultural Property Relief in advance of the budget. Any representations made to the UK Government concerning changes to taxation matters would fall to the Minister of Finance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the draft ammonia strategy ensures that efforts to reduce emissions do not compromise food production, particularly for small family farms.
answer_text: The draft Ammonia Strategy is being developed to include both mandatory and voluntary on-farm emission reduction measures. These reflect that there is a diverse range of farm business types and sizes in Northern Ireland and therefore the measures adopted by farm businesses will depend on the individual farm’s circumstances.The voluntary measures in the Ammonia Strategy include a range of alternatives to technology-based emissions reduction measures applicable across a range of farm sizes, depending on individual farm characteristics. The proposed measures,  that were included in the consultation, include longer grazing seasons, reductions in crude protein levels in livestock diets, selection of livestock genetics to improve nutrient use efficiency, and the establishment of tree plantations to reduce the impact of ammonia emissions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of individuals that have reoffended after being convicted under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011; and (ii) the number of animal cruelty-related deaths recorded where no convictions were made, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In the period 2019 to 2023, the most recent five-year period for which information is available, three individuals who had previously been convicted at courts of an offence under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, were convicted of a further offence under the same legislation.Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at courts where there was a conviction for at least one offence under the legislation specified.3. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI, Harbour Police, Airport Constabulary, NCA or PBNI.4. Because of the small numbers involved, and to enable useful information to be provided, figures for those convicted in individual years during the period have been aggregated.In relation to part (ii) of the question, the Department of Justice does not hold information in its prosecutions and convictions datasets on the number of animals who died as a result of an animal cruelty offence, so we are not able to provide an answer to this part of the question.
department: Department of Justice

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to set out the timescale for the Executive’s budget 2025/2026.
answer_text: The funding envelope for 2025-26 was announced on 30 October with the settlement for further years to be announced in spring 2025. As a result, the Executive will only be able to set a single year Budget for 2025-26 before the start of the financial year in April. It is my intention, subject to Executive agreement, to publish a draft Budget by early December to allow time for a full 12 week consultation before a final Budget is agreed by the Executive prior to the start of the new financial year.
department: Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the impact the transition from working tax credits to universal credit has had on small family farms; (ii) how his Department plans to address the financial challenges small family farms face following this transition; and (iii) any specific support available to assist small family farms during this transition.
answer_text: Your question was referred to me as my Department has responsibility for Universal Credit. All existing claimants of Working Tax Credit (WTC) are in the process of moving to Universal Credit (UC). This move will continue until the closure of the tax credits system at the end of March 2025.All claimants migrating to Universal Credit (known as Move to UC) will receive a letter explaining all of the help and support available to them during and after the migration process. Tailored support to meet the specific circumstances of the claimant, including the impact of being self-employed, farm owners, etc. can also be provided at the department’s Jobs and Benefits offices.When making a Universal Claim during the migration period, claimants will receive Transitional Protection if their monthly entitlement to Universal Credit is less than their monthly entitlement while on legacy benefits, including Working Tax Credits. This ensures that claimants are not worse off financially at the point of their move to UC. There is dedicated Move to UC guidance on nidirect which can be found at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/move-universal-credit
department: Department for Communities

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that the equality and disability screening template demonstrates that the spatially targeted measures in the draft ammonia strategy will impact on religious belief and political opinion, whether an Equality Impact Assessment will now be required.
answer_text: An Equality and Disability Duties Screening Report for the draft Ammonia Strategy was published in January 2023. The Screening Report is being updated to reflect revisions made to the draft Ammonia Strategy following the public consultation held from 4 January to 3 March 2023. The updated Equality and Disability Duties Screening Report will ensure my Department gives due regard to its duties regarding Equality Impacts and Rural Needs Impacts, in line with the published guidance, namely “Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 - A Guide for Public Authorities, 2010”, published by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland. The revised draft Ammonia Strategy and Impact Assessment Reports will be published later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the importance of the sheep sector to the agri-food industry, particularly in Less Favoured Areas, (i) why there has been a lack of sheep research commissioned by his Department and the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute, as highlighted by Agrisearch; and (ii) to detail any plans in place to address this gap.
answer_text: My Department has supported sheep research over many years at AFBI, with a number of active and recently completed projects, covering various aspects of animal health and wider sustainability issues of our sheep sector. I welcome the formation of the AgriSearch Sheep Working Group which will help to develop recommendations for future sheep research and innovation.  I look forward to hearing the outputs of that work and my officials will continue to engage with AgriSearch when developing its plans for future research and innovation support.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department plans to utilise the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme as a tool to ensure compliance with (i) Article 6.3 of the Habitats Directive; and (ii) Article 28 of the Environment Order (NI) 2002 in protecting and enhancing our natural habitats.
answer_text: Farmers who participate in the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) are provided with information which is intended to help them make informed decisions about agri-environmental and nutrient management on their farm. They receive results and advice about nutrient requirements based on the field classification they have provided when registering for the scheme. Where a field is within a designated site, no recommendations will be given for application of nutrients to the field to affect compliance with the conservation objectives of the site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15491/22-27, given the Future Agriculture Policy’s requirement to finish cattle at proposed ages and meet calving intervals for carbon targets which will require farm buildings that promote efficiencies, how the Air Quality Impact Assessment will ensure that both environmental protection and decarbonisation efforts are balanced without creating unintended consequences for agriculture production.
answer_text: I am committed to improving the environmental performance of the Northern Ireland agricultural sector, both in terms of greenhouse gas emissions and nutrient pollution.  I do not anticipate any issues between the proposed carbon reduction measures in the future agricultural policy and the interim air quality assessment procedures, as the proposed carbon reduction measures are not likely to require permissions which will necessitate an air quality impact assessment. The cattle productivity measures of reducing the age of cattle at slaughter and reducing beef cow calving intervals can be achieved through a combination of improved management on farm of animal health, genetic selection, grazing management and fodder quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15755/22-27, whether compensation was paid by his Department to the meat industry as a result of Civil Service outage.
answer_text: Regarding the question on whether compensation was paid to the meat industry because of the Civil Service outage of the 21st of August 2023, it can be confirmed that no compensation was paid to the meat industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15489/22-27, to detail (i) how his Department has ensured due regard to its duties under (a) Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998; and (b) the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 during the revision of the impact assessments for the draft Ammonia Strategy; and (ii) the specific measures that have been taken to assess and address potential inequalities identified through this process.
answer_text: To ensure that my Department gives due regard to its duties regarding Equality Impacts and Rural Needs Impacts, it follows the published guidance, namely “Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 - A Guide for Public Authorities, 2010”, published by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland.Rural Needs Impact Assessments are completed in line with “A Guide to the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 for Public Authorities (Revised), April 2018”, published on the DAERA website.The draft Ammonia Strategy was published and underwent public consultation from 4 January to 3 March 2023. The Equality and Disability Duties Screening report and Rural Needs Impact Assessment report were published alongside the draft Ammonia Strategy. These are now being updated, in line with the published guidance. These will reflect the revisions made to the Ammonia Strategy following consultation and will assess and address potential inequalities arising because of these revisions. The revised draft Ammonia Strategy and Impact Assessment Reports will be published later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of delivering the Strule Shared Education Campus.
department: Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14881/22-27, to detail (i) the steps his Department is taking to develop a new sheep scheme similar to the Agri-Climate Rural Environment Scheme in the south of Ireland, aimed at enhancing sustainability and reducing greenhouse gas emission in both uplands and lowland farms; and (ii) any collaborative efforts being made across the island to address climate change.
answer_text: The Sheep Industry Taskforce produced its report to identify the needs of the sheep sector in Northern Ireland and a proposed package to encourage its development. I met recently with the Taskforce to discuss the report.My officials are currently working to better understand the strategic needs of this sector and I will consider deploying levers to meet those needs.  It is too early in the policy development process to give any indication of what might be included in any sheep scheme moving forward. Any decision to introduce new policies or programmes will be in the context of budget availability within the earmarked farm support funding envelope.  (ii) 	My officials are in regular dialogue with counterparts in the Irish government on a wide range of climate change related issues, including through the North South Ministerial Forum.My Department also has well established partnerships with DAFM and Research Ireland, which fund a range of collaborative research involving researchers at AFBI, QUB and UU. Several projects are addressing aspects of climate change, the most notable of which is the Climate+Water+Biodiversity Co-centre.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the annual budget allocated for the Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) for each year since its inception; (ii) for a breakdown of administration costs for managing the EFS; (iii) for a breakdown of the financial support provided to farmers; and (iv) , given post-Brexit funding uncertainties, to detail the projected long-term costs associated with maintaining and expanding the EFS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15262/22-27, given the Strategic Investments Board's (SIB) review of the TB Strategic Partnership Group (TBSPG) and SIB highlighting the need for scientific opinion and stakeholder engagement, (i) how his Department plans to address the rising rates of bovine TB (bTB) and associated compensation costs; (ii) to detail the status of the recommendations made by the TBSPG report on the eradication of bTB; and (iii) whether the recommendations were based on scientific opinion.
answer_text: The Strategic Investment Board (SIB) recently completed a review of the TB Eradication Partnership (TBEP), an independent advisory body established in 2018 following a recommendation made by the TB Strategic Partnership Group (TBSPG).  The scope of this SIB review did not extend to the earlier TBSPG.  My Department and I recognise the ongoing requirement for independent scientific advice relating to the control and eradication of bovine TB and for this advice to take account of the most recent developments in the field.  This continuing need, along with the importance of effective stakeholder engagement, is clearly noted in the SIB’s review of TBEP.The TBSPG published its proposals in 2016.  These formed the basis of the Northern Ireland (NI) TB Eradication Strategy which was launched in March 2022.  The proposals were subject to scientific appraisal by members of the partnership, Dr Cecil McMurray and Dr George McIlroy.  This scientific appraisal was peer reviewed by Professor Simon More.  The TBSPG proposals were also subject to behavioural analysis by Dr Philip Robinson. Since the NI TB Eradication Strategy was launched, four actions have been implemented in full and progress has been made on a number of others.  However, it is recognised that progress on some actions has been hindered by budgetary and resource pressures while a judicial review quashed the previous wildlife intervention policy. Consequently, I have asked the Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) to undertake a review of TB policy and the TB programme, including the actions contained in the TB Eradication Strategy.  I am looking forward to considering the CVO’s findings in the coming weeks and I intend to publish a new delivery plan by the end of the year. This plan will seek to help farmers address the current high levels of bovine TB incidence in Northern Ireland and will clearly set out our priorities over the remainder of this Assembly mandate.   Ultimately, our intention is to reduce the economic impact of this costly disease, both to farm businesses and the resultant pressure on my Department’s budget.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the review of Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation Framework to be completed in 2025, to detail (i) the key areas being assessed; (ii) how the outcomes of this review will influence future funding and policy decisions for rural communities.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework has been in place for 8 years and my Department has commissioned an independent review which is examining how effectively it has been implemented. The review will assess the effectiveness of the Framework as an initiative to work in partnership with other public authorities in tackling poverty and social isolation in rural areas.  It will also examine the extent to which the objectives and intended outcomes have been achieved and how the measures delivered have contributed to addressing the priority areas for intervention.  Additionally, the review will examine how the implementation, monitoring and evaluation arrangements set out in the Framework have been implemented and how effective the programme of measures delivered under the TRPSI Framework has been in targeting the target groups.  The review, which is due to report back in early 2025, is engaging with a range of stakeholders, including public authorities and rural community organisations.  The findings and recommendations emanating from the review will be considered alongside the evaluations of other pilots and initiatives, available research and approaches across other jurisdictions to inform the development of an evidence-led rural policy which reflects the changed rural landscape.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14616/22-27, and given the differences in government support available north and south of the border, how his Department plans to (i) ensure mushroom growers in the north can compete equally with those in the south of Ireland, specifically in terms of financial aid, innovation, and capital investment; (ii) address concerns that the proposed schemes may not address the needs of the mushroom sector; and (iii) ensure that the schemes provide meaningful and effective support for the industry’s specific challenges.
answer_text: The development of future policy and support via the Supply Chain and Horticulture workstreams of the Farm Support and Development Programme continues and this will help to address the challenges and explore opportunities that exist for the horticulture sector, which includes mushrooms. The planned Pilot Horticulture Scheme will provide focused knowledge transfer, innovation, supply chain and capital support to the production horticulture sector through three schemes: Sustainable Sector Growth Groups, Innovation Driver and Support, and New Growers Academy. Whilst an element of the Pilot Horticulture Scheme will seek to improve communication, collaboration, and technical knowledge between growers across commercial horticulture, I acknowledge that mushroom growers had to embrace this concept to survive and indeed grow their businesses.Mushroom growers in Northern Ireland grouped, on a voluntary basis, to form three mushroom Producer Organisations (POs) operational in NI (with only one grower who also is the only organic mushroom grower in NI not in a PO). They  receive significant collective benefit from active membership of their POs with access to agronomy support, sourcing materials, having efficient supply chain linkages, T&D opportunities, and access to capital funding programmes for business competitiveness and sustainability to adopt ‘best in class’ growing and harvesting facilities,Mushroom growers will be eligible to make an application to proposals under the Pilot Horticulture Programme - Sustainable Sector Growth Groups, Innovation Driver and Support and New Growers’ Academy as they see benefit.Work is also ongoing to develop a Capital Investment Measure which will provide financial support to help primary producers in the agricultural and horticultural sectors improve business efficiency and environmental performance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the all-island framework on disease control can enhance early detection and rapid response to a bluetongue outbreak, to ensure the island of Ireland is protected against the introduction and spread of the disease.
answer_text: The island of Ireland is recognised as one epidemiological unit, and it is imperative that disease control actions taken forth in both jurisdictions are done in close cooperation.  DAERA officials are therefore currently engaged on a regular basis with colleagues from the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM) with respect to our ongoing surveillance to mitigate against any incursion of Bluetongue virus (BTV) and our proposed disease control response should it occur.The all-island animal health and welfare strategy, which was commissioned by the North South Ministerial Council (NSMC), encourages collaboration and ongoing engagement between both jurisdictions, north and south, in terms of the approach to disease surveillance and control measures (which will include BTV).Ongoing work commissioned and directed by both jurisdictions in relation to animal health is recorded and agreed through an ongoing Action Plan.  This is drawn from an agreed Work Programme. This facilitates close cooperation on animal health policy and research between both jurisdictions.  This includes the sharing of information, learning from best practise and collaborating on consistency of approach with regards to disease preparedness and response.In addition, both departments participate in the All-Island Surveillance Forum (AISF) which provides for continual collaboration on disease surveillance across the island. The AISF is a strategic group focussed on optimising delivery of policy and senior veterinary recommendations for animal health surveillance. The most recent meeting of the AISF took place on Thursday 26 September 2024, at which BTV and the monitoring of the current situation across Great Britain and mainland Europe was discussed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how the Environmental Farming Scheme (i) has enhanced sustainable food production; (ii) strengthened the rural economy; (iii) supported farmers in Less Favoured Areas; and (iv) addressed low incomes.
answer_text: The main objectives of the Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) are focused on enhancing biodiversity, conserving carbon and improving water quality. There is a comprehensive monitoring and evaluation framework which is assessing progress against these objectives, and it does not specifically measure other outcomes of the scheme.  However, I can provide some insight into how EFS may have contributed to the parameters in question:The measures implemented though EFS deliver environmental improvements which help to support sustainable agriculture and food production.Since 2017, there have been over 6,200 EFS agreement holders with £67m paid directly to farmers, representing a substantial investment in the rural economy.1646 EFS agreements across Higher and Wider Levels are on farms in Less Favoured Areas (LFAs).  These represent 26.5% of all EFS agreements, with a total of £20m funding provided to EFS agreement holders in LFAs.Payments rates for EFS are on a “costs incurred and income foregone basis” for the environmental actions that farmers implement. Therefore, funding provided by EFS can help to support farm incomes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that many designated Special Areas of Conservation are concentrated in Less Favoured Areas (LFAs), (i) how his Department will ensure farmers in LFAs are not disproportionately impacted by spatially targeted measures contained in the Draft Ammonia Strategy (DAS); (ii) to detail (a) the number of farms impacted; (b) the amount of land affected; and (c) the total production losses related to proposed spatially targeted measures; (iii) why his Department has not conducted a full (a) Equality Impact Assessment; and (b) Rural
answer_text: Following consultation on the draft Ammonia Strategy in 2023, officials in my Department have taken stakeholder responses into consideration, further reviewed the proposals, and my Department is now finalising the draft strategy. My Department plans to publish the revised draft Ammonia Strategy to accompany an eight week consultation on a Strategic Environmental Assessment Environment Report by the end of October. All other impact assessments completed for the draft Ammonia Strategy are currently being updated to reflect changes made following the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Future Operational Protocol to Assess the Impacts of Air Pollution on the Natural Environment - Call for Evidence, to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure the proposed approach to replacement agricultural buildings does not introduce barriers to decarbonization efforts on farms, particularly when upgrading or replacing old agricultural housing systems.
answer_text: The new air quality assessment procedure has been developed to ensure only development which does not cause significant impacts on the natural environment is progressed. Measures to decarbonise agricultural production are being developed in line with the carbon sequestration and methane emission reduction principles outlined in the Future Agricultural Policy consultation document. I am, therefore, content that the proposed approach to assessing air pollution will not introduce barriers to the implementation of the Department’s decarbonisation measures.

answer_text: (i)/(ii)/(iii)I have stressed to the Secretaries of State for Northern Ireland and for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs the need for funding decisions to reflect the importance of just transition and also made the case that there should be a separate, specific and additional capital fund to support just transition inagriculture.  My Department has also been engaging with the Department of Finance on this and raising it with HMT as part of the broader budgetary processes.  This is now being considered by the respective departments.(iv)The Chancellor is due to provide further clarity on the funding available as part of her Budget announcement on 30 October 2024 and I shall assess the associated implications after that.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Strategic Investments Board's review of the TB Eradication Partnership; and (ii) when this report will be made available to the public.
answer_text: The Strategic Investment Board’s (SIB) review of the TB Eradication Partnership (TBEP) has recently been completed and the review has since been shared with former members of the TBEP who were in post in December 2023.  I want to once again put on record my thanks for their commitment and advice to DAERA during their five-year tenure.  The review has also been shared with the AERA Committee.The SIB review acknowledged the need for independent expert advice on the way forward and our approach to tackling bovine TB.  It also noted the need for scientific opinion and the need to ensure clear mechanisms for wide stakeholder engagement in this area.  My Department will now consider the SIB findings.  The timeline for next steps with relation to any future partnership bodies or reform of stakeholder engagement will be further outlined within the ongoing review of bovine TB being progressed by the Chief Veterinary Officer. The SIB review of TBEP will be shared with members of the TB Stakeholders Working Group ahead of their next meeting scheduled for 9 October 2024.  It is my intention to publish the report on the Departmental website shortly after that meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how he will ensure his Department's draft Ammonia Strategy does not disproportionately impact small farm businesses; (ii) how the draft strategy accounts for (a) small; and (b) large farm sizes; and (iii) any alternatives to technology-based solutions being considered for small farmers to assist them to adapt without compromising their economic viability.
answer_text: It is important that the Ammonia Strategy delivers the most effective measures to protect our environment through providing pathways for farmers to reduce their ammonia emissions.  The Ammonia Strategy will comprise both mandatory and voluntary on-farm emission reduction measures. The impact of measures proposed in the draft Ammonia Strategy was considered through the responses to the public consultation in 2023.  A further opportunity to provide views on the proposed mandatory ammonia reduction measures will be included as part of the consultation process during the 2024 Nutrients Action Programme Review. This will ensure I have all the relevant information available to inform the Ammonia Strategy, taking into consideration the views of all stakeholders. The on-farm emission reduction measures will apply across the diverse range of farm business types and sizes in Northern Ireland. Not every measure will apply on every farm as the specific measures will be determined by individual farm characteristics. The voluntary measures in the Ammonia Strategy include a range of alternatives to technology-based emissions reduction measures applicable across a range of farm sizes, depending on individual farm characteristics. These include longer grazing seasons, reductions in crude protein levels in livestock diets, selection of livestock genetics to improve nutrient use efficiency, and the establishment of tree plantations to reduce the impact of ammonia emissions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether current ammonia thresholds are harmonised on an all-island basis; (ii) any environmental risks posed by the different ammonia thresholds across the island of Ireland; (iii) any efforts in place to monitor ammonia emissions on an all-island basis; and (iv) whether there is a plan for all-island co-operation on ammonia pollution control.
answer_text: The critical level for ammonia, that is the concentration of ammonia, above which environmental damage occurs is harmonised across Europe. The ammonia thresholds used in environmental impact assessments vary between jurisdictions and are not harmonised on an all-island basis, with each jurisdiction having its own approach to the assessment of potential impacts on the environment based on the specific factors such as existing pollution levels. The assessment approach used by the Irish Environmental Protection Agency can be found at:https://www.epa.ie/publications/licensing--permitting/industrial/ied/Assessment-of-Impact-of--Ammonia-and-Nitrogen-on-Natura-sites-from-Intensive-Agriculture-Installations-2023.pdfBoth jurisdictions have a duty to assess transboundary impacts. I am content that the new interim assessment approach adopted by NIEA provides sufficient safeguards for the natural environment with respect to air pollution.DAERA is engaged with the Department of the Environment, Climate and Communications in Ireland in developing a cross-border air quality project call under the PEACE PLUS programme. The programme is funded by the European Union, the Governments of the United Kingdom and Ireland, and the Northern Ireland Executive and will be administered by the Special EU Programmes Body. Discussions between officials in both jurisdictions have highlighted ammonia as one of the strands of work in which there can be common engagement. It is envisaged that this work strand would focus on monitoring and modelling capabilities for ammonia on an all-island basis, as well as a cross-border forum for engagement on this issue. It is envisaged that the Air Quality Call will go out later in the year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the financial support he will make available to potato growers who lost their crops to unforeseen weather conditions and flooding; and (ii) any direct engagement he is having with those farmers impacted.
answer_text: I can confirm that there are presently no plans to implement a financial support package for potato farmers who have lost crops due to poor weather conditions and flooding. The case for a financial support scheme for potato growers affected by flooding in counties Armagh and Down during November 2023 has been rigorously examined by DAERA. A business case was developed to test the need for and value for money (including the net additional NI economy impact) of a potential support package. However, the conclusion of this process was that government intervention was not likely to meet the threshold associated with managing of public money required in order to justify and authorise this expenditure. For this reason, DAERA has not implemented a support scheme. In March 2024, I met a number of potato growers who were impacted by last years flooding. I have had no direct engagement with farmers on this matter since that time.My officials from the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) have continued to provide technical and advisory support to local potato growers to enhance resilience to weather and related risks to their crops.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any data available on the carbon footprint impact factor of sheep; and (ii) how the carbon footprint of sheep varies between lowland and severely disadvantaged land.
answer_text: The 2022 Greenhouse Gas (GHG) data published by the National Atmospheric Emissions Inventory indicates that sheep directly account for 327 kt CO2e or 5.3% of the total agriculture sector emissions. Sheep will also account for a proportion of the GHG emissions generated by fuel use in agricultural vehicles and emissions from nitrogen fertilisers and lime applied to agricultural land.Analysis of the Northern Ireland Devolved Administration GHG Inventory data through RSK ADAS software indicates that emissions from ewes on upland categorised farms are approximately 8% lower than the emissions from ewes on lowland categorised farms.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQT 502/22-27, (i) whether funding for Just Transition is separate and additional from the current earmarked budget for agriculture; (ii) to detail what the £330 million covers and for how long; (iii) whether the separate capital funding is additional money he is bidding for; and (iv) where that funding is coming from.
answer_text: I am committed to establishing the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture in the months ahead but I first need certainty from the UK Government on the level of funding being made available in the years ahead.   I have already stressed to the Secretaries of State for Northern Ireland and for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs the need for funding decisions to reflect the importance of just transition and also made the case that there should be a separate, specific and additional capital fund also to support just transition in agriculture.£329.4m was a HM Treasury earmarked allocation to DAERA in 2024-25 for Agriculture, Agri-Environment and Wider Rural economy and that is largely resource funding guaranteed until the end of this financial year.  Future provision of funding is a matter for the new UK Government. (iii)/(iv) As noted above, I am making the case that there should be additional capital funding over and above the earmarked funding normally provided directly by the UK Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether locking out the sheep sector of any future sheep scheme reflects his Department's purpose of sustainability at the heart of a living, working, active, landscape valued by everyone.
answer_text: The overall aim of my Department’s Farm Support and Development Programme is to transition to a more sustainable farming sector by seeking to implement policies and strategies that benefit our climate and environment, while supporting our economically and socially significant agriculture sector.I am aware of the contribution the sheep sector makes to the livelihood of many NI farm businesses and the importance of sheep farming to wider rural economy.My officials are currently working to better understand the strategic needs of this sector and I will consider deploying levers to meet those needs.  It is too early in the policy development process to give any indication of what might be included in any sheep scheme moving forward.  Officials recently met the Sheep Industry Taskforce to discuss the report and agree next steps.  I look forward to receiving an update on these discussions.  Any decision to introduce new policies or programmes will be in the context of budget availability within the earmarked farm support funding envelope.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the recommendation in the report on the Strategic Dialogue on the Future of EU Agriculture that a Temporary Just Transition Fund should be established outside the Common Agriculture Policy; and (ii) whether funding for Just Transition must be from a separate budget from the agriculture budget.
answer_text: I support the EU’s approach outlined in the Strategic Dialogue report and am committed to setting up a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture. We need to ensure that the work that we carry out to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and reach net zero will be fair and that we support people on that journey. That is why we need to provide those incentives and support. I will be considering in conjunction with Executive colleagues the best approach to take to this and to the other requirements placed upon us by the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, learning where appropriate from the experience of others.I am committed to establishing the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture in the months ahead but the level of funding available for it will clearly need to be considered in the context of budgetary pressures and priorities across the NI Executive and the funding made available by the UK Government.  In that regard, I have already stressed to the Secretaries of State for Northern Ireland and for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs the need for funding decisions to reflect the importance of just transition and also made the case that there should be a separate, specific and additional capital fund also to support just transition in agriculture.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: My officials have recently met the Sheep Industry Taskforce to discuss the report and agree next steps.  I look forward to receiving an update on these discussions in the coming weeks and I plan to meet with the Taskforce in the near future to discuss this further.Any decision to introduce new policies or programmes will be in the context of budget availability within the ear-marked farm support funding envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the difference in government support available, how he will ensure the mushroom sector in the north can compete equally with growers in the south.
answer_text: My officials are developing future policy and support for the sector via the Supply Chain and Horticulture workstreams of the Farm Support and Development Programme to help address the challenges and explore opportunities that exist for the horticulture sector, which includes mushrooms.A new Pilot Horticulture Scheme is currently being developed that will have a bespoke range of support measures, tailored for the specific needs of this sector, and to maximise the industry’s potential. It will provide focused knowledge transfer, innovation, supply chain and capital support to the production horticulture industry which includes mushrooms through three schemes: Sustainable Sector Growth Groups, Innovation Driver and Support, and New Growers Academy.Work is also ongoing to develop a Capital Investment Measure which will provide financial support to help primary producers in the agricultural and horticultural sectors improve business efficiency and environmental performance. Officials at CAFRE provide knowledge transfer, training, and innovation support services across the agri-food sector to equip farmers to address today's challenges and develop their businesses in a sustainable way. This support extends to the mushroom sector.I met with the Northern Ireland Mushroom Growers Association earlier this year regarding support for the mushroom sector and continue to engage with them on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will make a final decision on the payment per hectare under future sustainability payments.
answer_text: The policy intent of the new Farm Sustainability Payment is to provide a balance between providing a safety net which will help farm businesses withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond its ability to manage effectively and encouraging farm businesses to be sustainable, efficient, competitive and to manage risk proactively.  Over time and in line with the capacity for delivery and uptake of the new Schemes within the Farm Support and Development Programme, its budget will reduce to a much lower level as funding is redirected to the new schemes.  We must also remember that the budget available for agricultural support will evolve over time. Therefore, there is no fixed end point for the value of payment rates per hectare and this is a matter that will be subject to ongoing review and judgement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the draft Rural Policy Framework, including the total amount of funding allocated in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: A total of £291,000 funding has been allocated by my Department in the 2024-25 financial year towards the final Rural Policy Framework pilot.As Minister, I have recently established a new Rural Policy Unit, which has been tasked with developing future rural policy for Northern Ireland following the UK’s exit from the EU, which will consider the learning from the 11 pilots developed under the Rural Policy Framework.  	Whilst this work is ongoing, I wish to reassure you that my Department continues to deliver funding to rural communities through a range of NI Executive and Departmental interventions. These include the City & Growth Deals, the Complementary Fund, the PEACEPLUS Programme, the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework and the inter-departmental Covid Recovery Small Settlements Regeneration Programme.I trust you find this information useful.
