question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide further information on his review of the Nutrients Action Programme, including how he will ensure that any changes to the Nutrients Action Programme will create conditions that enable farmers to farm.
answer_text: A review of the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) is currently being progressed.  The NAP has been reviewed on three occasions since it was first introduced in 2007.  Regular reviews are a statutory requirement and assess the effectiveness of the NAP in protecting and improving water quality.The current review is assessing detailed analysis of water quality data and considering what additional measures are needed to help reduce nutrient losses to waterways from agriculture sources.  The proposed revisions to the NAP will be subject to public consultation later this year.  In parallel there will also be a consultation on the associate Strategic Environmental Assessment and Habitats Regulation Assessment.My Officials will engage stakeholders on the proposals for the revised Action Programme as part of the review process and a key stakeholder group comprised of some fifty representatives of agricultural and environmental organisations has already been established.  The measures in the NAP are based on the Code of Good Agricultural Practice and are underpinned by science, the majority of which is local research by the Agri Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI).  My Department and AFBI will continue to work to ensure that the NAP supports efficient and environmentally sustainable farming.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) how a capital investment scheme, under the Farm Support and Development programme, will operate for individual farmers; and (ii) how will it enable individual farm planning applications.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward a Capital Investment Measure under the new Farm Support and Development programme. It will initially focus on capital support for technology and equipment to help farm businesses reduce ammonia emissions, carbon emissions and nutrient losses. Proposals for a scheme are being co-designed with stakeholder organisations, and it is not possible to set out precisely at this stage how the proposed scheme will operate for individual farmers. However, I want to ensure that new initiatives and schemes are simple and easy to access.Where technology or equipment adopted by an individual farm business provides mitigation to reduce or offset potential environmental impacts of a development proposal, this will be considered in provision of advice by the Department to local authorities as part of their deliberations on planning applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will provide a pathway for farmers to reduce emissions if they cannot apply the betterment approach under the draft ammonia strategy operational protocol.
answer_text: The Ammonia Strategy for Northern Ireland, which I plan to publish later this year, will provide a pathway for farmers to reduce their ammonia emissions. The mandatory and voluntary on-farm emission reduction measures in the Ammonia Strategy include measures applicable on cattle, pig, and poultry farms. Not every measure will be applicable on every farm as the specific measures applicable at an individual farm level will be determined by the specific farm characteristics. My Department is currently considering support mechanisms through the Farm Support and Development Programme Capital Investment Measure and Knowledge and Innovation Schemes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of anaerobic digestion plants, broken down by county.
answer_text: Anaerobic digestion (AD) facilities that accept waste materials to be treated are authorised by my department, depending on the scale of the activity, by Pollution Prevention and Control Permits or Waste Management Licences.Currently 47 anaerobic digestion facilities are authorised by my department broken down as; Antrim 5; Armagh 8; Derry 9; Down 10; Fermanagh 0 and Tyrone 15. Information on the location of AD facilities that are not subject to authorisation by my department is not held.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the rationale for refusing planning permission where a farmer has demonstrated a reduction in emissions on a designated site in a future operation protocol.
answer_text: The decision to grant or refuse planning permission is a matter for the appropriate planning authority. However, before any permission is granted the planning authority must undertake an environmental assessment.  NIEA’s new operational protocol provides conservation advice based on scientific evidence to inform that assessment. The focus of the NIEA advice is on the residual environmental impact of the development rather than on the demonstration of a reduction. This is in line with established case law, as well as compliance with The Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farms that participated in the Environmental Farming Scheme between 2014 and 2020, broken down by (i) Severely Disadvantaged Areas; (ii) Disadvantaged Areas; and (iii) lowlands.
answer_text: The Environmental Farming Scheme was launched in 2017 and the first businesses were paid in 2018. Table 1 details the total number of Environmental Farming Scheme businesses who were paid in the period 2018-2020, broken down by land type. Most EFS agreements include land of more than one land type. Table 1: Total number of Environmental Farming Scheme businesses who were paid in the period 2018-2020, by area type.Land Type Number of Environmental Farming Scheme businesses paid in period 2018-2020Severely Disadvantaged Area, Disadvantaged Area, and Lowland2,391Disadvantaged Area and Lowland1,160Lowland705Total4,256
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the European Commission’s proposals on animal welfare during transport, whether responsibility for (i) leading on this policy; (ii) consulting with the industry; and (iii) representing the north's views to the EU, lies with his Department or the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
answer_text: The previous government made the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 in April 2024 and, as a result, responding on and implementation of relevant future EU legislation was removed from my ministerial responsibilities and Assembly scrutiny.  At present, therefore, determining the way forward for Northern Ireland in relation to the European Commission’s proposals on animal welfare during transport is a matter that rests with the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.I shall be seeking an early meeting with the Defra Secretary of State to understand the approach the new Government is taking to ensuring full and effective implementation of the agreement reached in the Windsor Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail his obligations as set out under annex 2 of the Protocol in relation to Legacy EU Common Market Organisation (CMO) Schemes; and (ii) how future legislation on EU CMO schemes will deliver on what is set out under annex 2 of the Protocol.
answer_text: While some parts of Regulation (EU) No 1308/2013 (the Common Market Organisation Regulation (CMO)) are referred to in Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework (formerly known as the Protocol on Ireland/Northern Ireland), none of those relate to aid under legacy EU CMO support schemes. Therefore, I have no specific obligations under Annex 2 in that regard.The proposed Agriculture Bill would provide specific primary powers to make the Fruit & Vegetable Aid Scheme discretionary and modify assimilated law related to that scheme and the legacy EU Promotions Scheme. Any future legislation on legacy EU schemes, or indeed alternative policies, would need to be compliant with our wider national and international obligations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to his Department's statement of 19 December 2023 on Ammonia Standing Advice, to detail (i) the number of planning applications awaiting case-by-case and site-by-site specific advice; (ii) the number of planning applications that have received this advice since 19 December 2023; and (iii) when the new ammonia strategy and updated standing advice will be agreed and in place.
answer_text: In DAERA’s role as a statutory consultee the Department retains information on planning consultation information associated with a range of development types.  We are unable to advise on the number of planning applications awaiting case-by-case and site-by-site specific advice however we can provide the total number of agricultural planning applications ongoing.  In answer to your questions I can advise as follows: From 19 December 2023 to 04 July 2024 the NIEA has a total of 51 agricultural planning applications awaiting case officer assessment.  We have issued responses for 144 agricultural planning applications since 19 December 2023, 99 of which have been assessed under the case-by-case approach.  The reworked Ammonia Strategy and the new Operational Protocol to assess the impacts of air pollution on the natural environment will be finalised later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how much Agricultural Property relief has been worth to farmers in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Inheritance Tax is not a devolved matter.  Consequently, my Department does not hold data on Inheritance Tax and the application of Agricultural Property Relief.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assurances his Department can provide for the continued availability of Tegretol in pharmacies.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department and other operators within the supply chain. Through UK wide medicines shortages management groups, my Department has been made aware of supply disruptions affecting various strengths and preparations of carbamazepine products including Tegretol® products. Current information appears to suggest supply disruptions are now resolving for all affected Tegretol® products. However, as medicines are manufactured as part of a complex, global and highly regulated industry, medicines supply disruptions can occur for a variety of reasons, therefore no one can provide an absolute guarantee that there will not be any disruptions in the future.Whilst supply disruptions can’t always be prevented, there are a range of national and locally well-established tools and processes to mitigate risks to patients. Several of these tools and processes have been implemented.Carbamazepine 100mg/5ml oral suspension, which includes Tegretol 100mg/5ml liquid, was added to the restrictive export and hoarding list on 22 May 2024. This list prevents the export of medicines from the UK and wholesalers hoarding them, thus ensuring they can be made available to patients across the UK.Guidance has been issued to healthcare professionals for those products that have been affected, including most recently a Medicine Supply Notification for Tegretol® 100mg/5ml liquid (MPS 3203), providing them with advice and actions to take to ensure patients continue to have access to appropriate treatments. Healthcare professionals can also register with the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service’s to access the medicines supply tool, which is updated regularly by DHSC, providing information on medicines shortages including anticipated re-supply dates.
department: Department of Health

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to bring forward an Areas of Natural Constraints scheme, similar to the south of Ireland and Scotland.
answer_text: Whilst I am always keen to learn from others about interventions that can support farmers, I have no plans to reinstate Area of Natural Constraint (ANC) payments for farmers. 		Since the ending of ANC payments, there has been no significant change to agriculture in LFAs relative to the lowland in Northern Ireland either in livestock numbers or farmed area, and incomes on LFA farms continue to compare favourably with lowland counterparts.	There is no evidence of land abandonment to any significant extent in Northern Ireland and the Farm Sustainability Payment will continue to provide support for agricultural activity in all areas of Northern Ireland, including disadvantaged areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 13314/22-27, to provide a list of insurance companies for woodlands.
answer_text: In my previous answer (AQW 13314/22-27) I provided a link to suppliers of forestry services for Northern Ireland through which landowners can seek advice on woodland insurance cover. My Department has no plans to hold or publish a list of companies within the commercial insurance sector that provide cover for woodlands in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) concerns raised by farmers on the use of grass in some anaerobic digestion plants instead of food waste or manure; and (ii) whether this is contributing to an existing shortage of grass, limiting local supply of conacre ground hay and silage for sale, which is driving up prices.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns being raised in relation to the use of grass in anaerobic digestion (AD) plants.  While my Department has a particular focus on the use of separated slurry as a feedstock for AD, the use of grass is inevitable given insufficient supply of other inputs. There are no reports of farmers being unable to obtain sufficient grass but additional demand for grass by anaerobic digestion plants is likely to create an upward pressure on land rental prices.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his plans for capital investment in Carrickmore Health Centre.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group, through the Primary Care Infrastructure Development Programme, is taking forward planned investment in primary care premises across Northern Ireland, based on a “Hub and Spoke” model.Carrickmore Health Centre is a spoke of the Omagh hub, and it is recognised that due to the growing size of the GP Practice, it is now operating in space which is well below its capacity requirements.  It has therefore been identified as a priority for capital investment.   The capital plan for a number of Trust projects, including Carrickmore Health Centre, is currently being finalised and timescales for a solution for Carrickmore Health Centre will be dependent on the outcome of this capital plan and confirmation on availability of funding through the Department’s overarching future capital plan.  The project will be subject to the usual business case approvals.
department: Department of Health

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether the sunset clause in the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Act 2023 applies to the north; and (ii) whether he has considered an extension of the sunset clause via an order to enable a policy review and future policy direction to be completed regarding draft agriculture policy and EU legacy schemes.
answer_text: The sunset provisions specified in Section 1 and Section 2 of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Act 2023 apply to Northern Ireland and these came into force at the end of 2023.  I can confirm I have not considered an extension of these sunset provisions via an order. Extension of these powers expired in 2023 and there are no other powers available to consider this. There are other powers available within the Act that provide for revocation and reform of assimilated law for example, and these expire in June 2026. I have not considered extension of these powers either.  To do so for Northern Ireland would require primary legislation to be brought to the Assembly. Some powers are available to modify agricultural policy and EU legacy schemes in Northern Ireland under Schedule 6 to the UK Agriculture Act 2020.  However, these do not provide adequate cover to modify all relevant legacy EU schemes, such as the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme and Agri-food Promotions Scheme. New primary legislation will be required to modify those legacy schemes. I have asked officials if there are further gaps in the primary powers that we require for the new Farm Support and Development Programme, and I will advise members of the outcome of my considerations in due course.  Any Bills would require the agreement of the Executive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 13088/22-27, (i) whether he is aware that timber growers previously insured with Marsh are no longer able to access growers insurance that covers fire, theft, and damage; and (ii) what steps he will take to address this.
answer_text: I am advised that Marsh is a multinational insurance broker and risk advisor, as such it determines it’s commercial approach to the provision of insurance in Northern Ireland. My Department does not influence insurance companies’ decisions in these matters.  		I previously advised that insurance cover is available for Northern Ireland woodlands.  A list of suppliers of forestry management services is published on my Department’s website available here. Woodland owners can avail of advice on forestry related matters, including obtaining insurance cover, from the suppliers listed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on his plans for a new agriculture bill; (ii) to detail the issues that this bill will cover; and (iii) how the bill will co-exist with existing provisions related to the north in Schedule 6 of the Agriculture Act 2020.
answer_text: I am considering whether to bring an Agriculture Bill forward during the current mandate.  I have asked officials if there any gaps in the primary powers we require for the new Farm Support and Development Programme and I will advise members of the outcome of my considerations in due course.  Any Bill would, of course, require the agreement of the Executive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he is considering a gradual phasing out of the use of cages for battery hens; and (ii) whether sufficient financial support will be provided to aid the move to cage-free products.
answer_text: Non-enriched cages, commonly referred to as “battery hens” have been prohibited under European legislation and domestic legislation here since 2012.  I am aware that the Scottish Government has launched a consultation on proposals to phase out the use of enriched cages. As the majority of the major retailers propose to cease buying eggs from enriched cages by 2025, my Department has no immediate plans to reform legislation. My Department will, however, continue to engage with Scottish Government colleagues on the progression of their consultation and any proposals arising from it. My Department’s Farm Business Improvement Scheme (Capital) has offered almost £12million in grant support to the poultry industry in the past, including support for the construction and fit out of free range and organic laying hen units. I intend to bring forward a Capital Investment Measure under the new programme of Farm Support and Development, which will initially focus on support for technology and equipment to help farm businesses reduce ammonia emissions, carbon emission and nutrient loss.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has considered grants for hen housing, particularly for laying hen and pullet farmers, similar to the Laying Hen Housing for Health and Welfare grant in England with those with flocks of 1,000 birds or more.
answer_text: I am aware of the Defra scheme, which supports projects to upgrade, refurbish or replace existing hen houses in England. I have no plans at this stage for a similar scheme in Northern Ireland.My Department has offered almost £12million in grant support for local poultry enterprises under the Farm Business Improvement Scheme (Capital). This included support for the construction and fit out of free range and organic laying hen units.My officials are developing a Capital Investment Measure under the new programme of Farm Support and Development, which will provide financial support to help primary producers in the agricultural and horticultural sectors improve business efficiency and environmental performance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the difficulties experienced by timber growers, namely forestry and woodland owners, in accessing insurance from companies who have recently withdrawn from the insurance market; and (ii) what steps his Department will take to address this.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any specific issues for landowners in accessing insurance for their woodlands. The insurance market is a commercialised and competitive sector within which I understand insurance cover for woodland can be obtained. My Department has no plans to intervene in the operation of this commercial sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether current levels of support will continue beyond the next three financial years in relation to the 2021 HM Treasury Spending Review and Autumn Budget commitment of £1 billion for farmers and land managers.
answer_text: Working with the Finance Minister and with my devolved administration counterparts, I am seeking to engage collectively with HMT on earmarked funding allocation for Agriculture, Agri-Environment and the Wider Rural economy beyond 2025.It is imperative that any settlement reflects DAERA’s policy objectives and ambitions and as an absolute minimum maintains the current budget allocation uplifted for inflation.Future provision of funding for agricultural, environmental and rural support will be a matter for the next UK Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the use of Bovaer to reduce methane emissions in cows.
answer_text: Bovaer is a synthetic methane suppressing feed additive manufactured by the Dutch company Royal DSM. The use of Bovaer to reduce enteric methane emissions has been widely researched globally with over 50 trials reported in scientific journals.Research has demonstrated that the typical reduction in enteric methane emissions from feeding Bovaer to cattle is in the range of 20% to 30% of daily enteric methane emissions, depending on the animal diet.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to list the investment areas under (i) City Deals; and (ii) PEACE PLUS funding for Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: In the 2024-25 financial year my Department will be investing in City & Growth Deals in the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council area under the Tourism and Regeneration pillar. The projects that will be invested in are Dungiven Regeneration, Bushmills Connected Regeneration Project and  Cushendall Innovation Centre (Phase 2). The expected expenditure across the three projects for the 2024-25 financial year is £30,000, this will be used to cover project development costs.	Under PEACE PLUS my Department is partnering with various Departments in the Republic of Ireland and the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB) to take forward 5 PEACE PLUS Investment Areas which will see funding of €165.8m being invested in strategic projects until 2029.  The investment areas are as follows:	IA 2.4 – Smart Towns & Villages, 	IA 4.2 – Rural Regeneration & Social Inclusion, 	IA 5.1 – Biodiversity, Nature Recovery & Resilience, 	IA 5.2– Marine & Coastal Management and 	IA 5.3 – Water Quality & Catchment Management.	Applications for funding are currently being assessed and until that exercise has been completed and Letters of Offer issued by SEUPB it is not possible to provide a detailed budget breakdown for 2024 – 25.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the increase of the minimum claim size from three hectares to five hectares to access payments under the Future Sustainability Payment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the impact that (i) increased costs; and (ii) increased farmgate prices has on farm level incomes; broken down by (a) sheep sector; (b) cattle sector; and (c) dairy sector.
answer_text: I appreciate that there have been movements in input costs and farmgate prices over the past year.  The impact of these changes will be shown in the farm income statistics due to be published by my Department in the middle of July at the following link: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/ni-agricultural-incomes. National statistics rules prohibit the release of these statistics or comment on them by officials prior to the pre-announced release date.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of his Department's budget for 2024-25, what plans he has to provide further financial assistance to farmers to improve the natural environment though the purchasing of Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment.
answer_text: To date 1514 businesses have been grant aided to a value of £10.48m for Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment (LESSE) through the Farm Business Improvement Scheme-Capital (FBIS-C). A further 204 businesses have received letters of offer for LESSE from FBIS-C that have not yet been claimed, with a grant value of approximately £0.38m. Budget allocation is in place for 2024-25 to meet these remaining claims.My officials are developing a Capital Investment Measure under the new programme of Farm Support and Development, which will provide financial support to help primary producers in the agricultural and horticultural sectors improve business efficiency and environmental performance. I intend that support will be focused on assisting the industry to meet Net Zero targets, to encourage investment in carbon reduction technology, and to invest in precision equipment to reduce emissions to air and water. Consideration of further support for LESSE is part of this development work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of the impact on the agriculture sector if rewetting of peatland and planting of trees on agricultural land is claimed under Land Use and Land-Use Change and Forestry (LULUCF) sector instead of agriculture under the UK National Atmospheric Emissions Inventory; and (ii) his engagement with the British government to upgrade the UK National Atmospheric Emissions Inventory to ensure agriculture claims the offsetting of emissions via rewetting and tree planting and not by LULUCF.
answer_text: In 2021, the greenhouse gas inventory for Northern Ireland reported emissions for the agriculture sector of 6,198.2 ktCO2e.  The respective total for emissions attributable to the LULUCF sector was 2,325.9 ktCO2e. Within the LULUCF sector the following estimates, for activities associated with a change in use from agriculture to forestry/wetlands, have been recorded.  For the period 1990-2021 these activities have been net source of greenhouse gas emissions. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their delivery plan for the Strategic Framework Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: Ending Violence Against Women and Girls remains one of our key priorities. We are committed to bringing forward the Strategic Framework and Action Plan for consideration by the Executive Committee as soon as possible.We are actively considering a number of options for delivery in consultation with officials.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for capital investment in Carrickmore Health Centre.
answer_text: The capital plan for a number of Trust projects (including Carrickmore Health Centre) is currently being finalised within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of the Department of Health (DoH). Timescale for a solution for Carrickmore Health Centre will be dependent on the outcome of this capital plan and confirmation on availability of funding through the DoH’s overarching future capital plan.  The project will be subject to the usual business case approvals.
department: Department of Health

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farmers impacted by the new eligibility requirements for Farm Sustainability Payments.
answer_text: An initial check against Departmental records has identified that: Approximately 2,100 businesses will be impacted by the historic years exercise. This exercise identified farm businesses that solely produced grass/grass silage and/or maintained land suitable for grazing or cultivation but undertook no further agricultural activity during an entire two year historic reference period, published as 2020 and 2021, will not be eligible to claim the Farm Sustainability Payment. Approximately 1,400 businesses will be impacted by the increase in minumim claim size from 3 ha to 5 ha.There is an overlap between these two sets of eligibility criteria and my Department is currently determining the extent of this overlap.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the level of support that will be available to farmers under the new Future Agriculture Policy; (ii) how this compares to the Basic Payment Scheme; (iii) whether specific support measures eligibility criteria will restrict the support available to some farmers; (iv) the rate of payment per hectare under the Farm Sustainability Payment; and (v) whether this will match Basic Payment Scheme payment levels.
answer_text: Funding for the programme comes largely from the Treasury’s earmarked funding for agriculture, environment and rural support which is guaranteed to the end of the current financial year. It is imperative that any future settlement reflects my Department’s policy objectives and ambitions as an absolute minimum. Working with the Finance Minister, I am seeking to engage collectively (and with DA counterparts) to stress to HMT the importance of starting now to work together to provide a five-year annual baseline budget, adjusted for inflation for each of the Administrations that replaces the current funding package post 2025.From 2021, the UK Government maintained the funding available until the end of the current Parliament which is in effect the end of this financial year.There are options for every farm business under the new Farm Support and Development Programme regardless of size or scale of activity, but it requires them to change their behaviour and deliver better outcomes reflecting the transition towards a model that is coming closer to public monies for public goods.  To facilitate the Farm Sustainability Transition Payment (FSTP) in 2025 and subsequently the Farm sustainability Payment (FSP) coming into operation in 2026, current Basic Payment Scheme entitlements will be carried forward into the new Scheme and will be used to claim FSTP 2025. They will then expire and new FSP entitlements will be allocated in the same number and unit value to those farm businesses who qualify for FSTP. Over time and in line with the uptake of new schemes the budget set for these payments will reduce to a much lower level as funding is redirected to other schemes.  These schemes include agri-environment measures and others focused on delivering long-term resilience and environmental sustainability,  improving efficiency and encouraging the development of effective supply chains.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their discussions to maximise the economic, historical and reconciliation potential of the Maze/Long Kesh site.
answer_text: We recognise the huge economic, historical and reconciliation potential of the site and we look forward to discussing with the Board how we can fully maximise that potential. We would like to take the opportunity to reflect on the success of the recent Balmoral show, which we, along with Junior Minister Reilly and Junior Minister Cameron, were delighted to attend.  It is a fantastic showcase for the region.  It proved as popular as ever, with initial attendance figures of around one hundred and twenty two thousand visitors over the four days.  Indications are that people stayed longer making the most of the good weather.  The show is a major undertaking, with co-operation between RUAS, their stewards and contractors, the Development Corporation and emergency services.  We commend their hard work and dedication.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he will increase opportunities for students to study and attain Veterinary Medicine qualifications in the (i) north of Ireland; and (ii) south of Ireland.
answer_text: Whilst DAERA is a key employer within the veterinary sector and as such has a vested interest in securing such a talent pipeline, issues around workforce planning and education for the veterinary sector sits within the remit of Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS) and Veterinary Council of Ireland (VCI) and educational institutes in the north and south of Ireland respectively.				I have recently met with Ulster University to discuss their ambitions and intentions including their plans for introducing veterinary related courses.  This included the considerations around a veterinary school within Northern Ireland and the situational analysis previously initiated by former Minister Lyons, facilitated by my department and undertaken by the Strategic Investment Board (SIB). DAERA will where requested continue to discuss with and support educational institutes should they seek to progress a business case for a veterinary school in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has had any engagement with the University College Dublin on the number of places available for northern students to study Veterinary Medicine at the university.
answer_text: I can confirm that I have not engaged with the University College Dublin (UCD) on the number of places available to northern students to study Veterinary Medicine at UCD.		My officials have, however, previously established the position relating to places available for northern students. 	The Central Applications Office (CAO), founded in 1971 by the Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) in the Republic of Ireland (ROI) has responsibility for processing centrally all applications for admission to first year undergraduate courses, including veterinary medicine.Northern Ireland (NI) students apply under the same system as ROI students, using the CAO system with the main difference being that the majority of NI students apply with A-Level qualifications rather than Leaving Certificate Exam (LCE) results.There are two criteria for entry to an ROI HEI:Eligibility – Based on the number of points received in one sitting of the Leaving Certificate or A-Levels for UK applicants.  Different courses have different points, with the most popular courses such as veterinary medicine attracting the highest point requirements.Subjects – For a number of courses, certain subjects (with qualifications gained in them) are needed in order to gain access, eg Science subjects for veterinary medicine.Applicants are scored on the basis of their best four A levels or three A levels and an AS level in a different subject from the same or preceding year.  The maximum number of points that can be achieved is 600, which is the current entry requirement for veterinary medicine at University College Dublin (UCD).  For LCE students, the best six results they receive are considered, with a maximum number of points also 600.  Further details on the criteria applied and scoring matrix can be found at Central Applications Office (cao.ie).  It is my understanding that HEIs are currently reviewing the scoring scheme.A 2011 study carried out by the IBEC- CBI12 Joint Business Council (JBC) IBEC identified a number of barriers to students from NI studying in the ROI, the most notable of which was the change in equivalencies between A-Levels and Leaving Certificate grades.  This change stems back to 2005, when UCAS (the University and Colleges Admissions Service - the organisation responsible for managing applications to higher education courses in the UK) altered the A-Level/LCE equivalences.  Whereas each LCE subject was previously considered to have half the points value of an A-level, following a review this was moved to having two-thirds the value.  This effectively devalued A-levels against LCE results.Following this change a number of HEIs required NI students to have four A-Levels (with A, and later A*, grades) for high-demand and high-point courses such as veterinary medicine, medicine, dentistry and law).  They felt that to do otherwise would give NI students with top grade A-Level results an unfair advantage over their Southern counterparts with top grade Leaving Certificate result
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he is aware of concerns in relation to the failure to properly stock 27 designated Public Angling Estate waters with trout; and (ii) whether his Department plans to redress this.
answer_text: I am aware of the concerns regarding the suspension of trout stocking to the Public Angling Estate (PAE) arising from the discovery of zebra mussels, an invasive species, at the PAE’s fish farm at Movanagher;	My officials are working with the Agri Food and Biosciences Institute to develop a protocol to allow future stocking, which is compliant with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985 and minimises the risk of the spread of zebra mussels. In the interim, 76,000 juvenile Brown Trout fry, raised in the limited borehole water supply at the fish farm and unaffected by zebra mussels, have been stocked to a limited number of PAE waters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their plans to maximise the potential of the Maze/Long Kesh site.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to address the increase in unadopted roads and developments.
answer_text: With your permission speaker I would like to answer Question 9 and Question 13 together.The existing Private Streets Order (NI) 1980 allows my Department to exercise its powers throughout the process of planning, construction and adoption of new developments.The Order provides my Department with powers to commence enforcement action using a bond provided by the developer, to fund the completion of roads and footpaths.However, enforcement action can often be a protracted process, and liaison with developers to achieve completion of infrastructure works to an adoptable standard is the preferred approach.  The Department has no plans to introduce further legislation to deal with this issue.I do however, also recognise that there are often complex problems outside of my Department’s control that need to be resolved before the sewerage and roads infrastructure in a development can be adopted.My Roads officials continue to work with colleagues in NI Water to identify, prioritise, and progress the adoption of private development sites.
department: Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the grants available to landowners with no farm business accounts; and (ii) whether there are options available under the Future Agriculture Policy for landowners.
answer_text: In order for a land manager to avail of grants from my Department, they must be registered with us and have a unique business identification number (business ID).		Under the new programme of Farm Support and Development there may be options available for landowners through the Farming with Nature Package. This Package will be open to all landowners/managers with three hectares or more of eligible land who meet the scheme requirements. Scheme participants will need to comply with new Farm Sustainability Standards and participate in the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to address the impact of predation on all wildlife species, particularly ground nesting birds.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to address the impact of predation on all wildlife species as predators are an essential component of any healthy ecosystem. Where that ecosystem is man-modified and therefore unbalanced, as is the case across much of Northern Ireland, action against predators may be necessary to protect particularly vulnerable species such as ground-nesting wading birds.		Such action may involve modification of habitat to make it less attractive to generalist predators, physical deterrents, and/ or lethal control. Appropriate actions are case specific and are an integral part of site-specific conservation projects.  These may require a licence under Article 18 of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 as amended.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he will consider providing a budget for the development of the processes to enable use of the Protected Geographical Indication status for Irish grass-fed beef; and (ii) what engagement he is having with the Irish government on this matter.
answer_text: My understanding is that in order for producers in Northern Ireland to obtain verification for their product as ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’, a grass-fed database will have to be developed. Geographical Indications are industry-led and it is therefore up to industry to fund any such software development in line with its desire for NI’s inclusion in the PGI. There is no precedent in the UK for government providing funding of this magnitude for the purposes of verifying geographical indications and it would be undesirable to create one by doing so in this case. My Department and AFBI provided guidance on the opposition procedure and advice on how the grass-fed status of NI beef could be verified, but anything beyond that would not be the responsibility of government. DAFM officials are aware that further work is needed before NI-produced ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’ can appear on shelves. This does not prevent grass-fed beef solely produced in the Republic of Ireland from marketing under the ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’ name.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on the implementation of City and Growth Deals.
department: Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what actions he will take to address loss of earnings by farmers who can no longer graze large numbers of sheep during the autumn and winter months due to the number of migratory birds damaging the land; (ii) whether he will give consideration to a scheme similar to the Agri-Climate Rural Environment Scheme in the south of Ireland; and (iii) whether he will explore potential solutions under the Environmental Farming Scheme Higher Tranche 8.
answer_text: My Department’s current Farm Support and Development Programme is planned to contain similar elements to the Agri-Climate Rural Environment Scheme (ACRES) that is being introduced in Ireland.  I am always keen to learn from others about interventions that can support both farmers and our rich biodiversity and have asked my officials to liaise with their Irish counterparts as this scheme progresses so that any key learning can be captured and applied as we move forward our farm support programmes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will undertake a review of legislation with the aim of strengthening the protection of wildlife.
answer_text: I am keen to explore, within the resources available to me, all possible actions to protect wildlife. My officials are working to develop a new Nature Recovery Strategy which will contain objectives designed to halt the loss of biodiversity. I hope to bring forward a public consultation on this strategy later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the Future Agriculture Policy improves the position of the primary producer in the food chain.
answer_text: The new Farm Support and Development Programme (FSDP) is designed to help farm businesses improve their resilience, including to the effects of climate change, and to develop and thrive while also making their fair contribution to improving our environment and to reducing carbon emissions.  The Supply Chain Measure of the FSDP in particular will aim to support improved collaboration between producers and growers within the supply chain, encouraging them to innovate, problem-solve and value-add beyond what they can achieve in isolation.I hope to bring forward by mid-2025 a range of support schemes that can help our farm businesses grow in a sustainable manner, one that puts a clear focus on food supply and food security and that also contributes to delivering the environmental improvements we all want to see.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on proposals to introduce a scheme to invest in the agri-food sector.
department: Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consideration he has given to a support package for farmers experiencing fodder shortage during bad weather in March and April 2024.
answer_text: I am aware that this year’s very wet and late Spring is presenting challenges for farmers operating in almost every sector within the industry. I am conscious of the wider impact if wet weather continues in the time ahead and I have made representations to the UK Government about the need for central government intervention if the situation lasts for much longer.With all the challenges the very poor weather has created for farmers and growers, there is no evidence of a widespread fodder shortage in Northern Ireland which would necessitate a specific support package to be developed.Individual farmers who are experiencing difficulties arising from a shortage of fodder should contact their local CAFRE Advisor, who will provide help and support to assist them extend their forage supplies until the weather improves and animals can be turned out to grazing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the importance of sheep production to the rural economy.
answer_text: Sheep contribute to 3 per cent of the gross output of NI agriculture. In 2022 the entire flock totalled 2.1 million and the total value of output from the sector increased by 6.4 per cent, to £106 million.		I recognise the important contribution that sheep production makes to economic, environmental and social sustainability in Northern Ireland, generating wider economic activity and employment in rural communities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his Department has sought legal advice on the impact on the provision of agricultural relief as a result of land moving away from food production under the draft Peatlands Strategy; and (ii) what engagement he has had with HMRC on this matter.
answer_text: The issue of inheritance tax and eligibility for agricultural property relief is not a devolved matter and policy responsibility rests with HM Treasury.  My Department has not sought legal advice and I have not had any engagement with HMRC on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the amount of peat that has been imported; and (ii) for a breakdown of the country of origin, in the last five years.
answer_text: Under EU Plant Health legislation, which applies in Northern Ireland, there is no requirement for phytosanitary controls on peat imports or movements within the EU. DAERA therefore does not have any involvement in peat imports and does not have data on the origin or amounts of peat imported into Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the process for retired or retiring horses leaving the horse racing industry.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for this matter and has no involvement in any process for retired or retiring horses leaving the horse racing industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 8474/22-27, to detail any engagement or representations he has made in relation to the Seasonal Workers Scheme, particularly with reference to the mushroom sector.
answer_text: I wrote to the Home Secretary on 8th March 2024 to raise concerns regarding changes to the immigration system and highlight my interest in the Migration Advisory Committee’s inquiry of the Seasonal Worker Visa, which the mushroom industry utilises. I will be meeting with industry representatives to discuss this issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Joint Nature Conservation Committee December 2021 report Guidance on Decision-making Thresholds for Air Pollution, why the 0.08 per cent figure was not included in his Department's July 2023 call for evidence on the Future Operational Protocol to Assess the Impacts of Air Pollution on the Natural Environment.
answer_text: The Call for Evidence, published in July 2023, contained discussion of, and provided a link to the 2021 JNCC report, ‘Guidance on Decision-making Thresholds for Air Pollution’ in its review of available evidence for thresholds (Section 6 – part E). The Call Document subsequently invited respondents to submit any additional evidence for consideration in the determination of thresholds (Question 3). 0.1%  was proposed as a de minimis threshold in the example assessment processes included in the call for evidence.The JNCC report’s 0.08% de-minimis threshold figure was not explicitly cited, in order that any additional evidence in relation to thresholds could be submitted on its own merit, without comparison to any pre-existing evidence. 	On review of the scientific evidence and responses it was concluded the evidence supported use  of the 0.08% as the de minimis threshold as recommended in the JNCC report.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of groups and organisations in receipt of funding from his Department; and (ii) the amount of funding, broken down by whether it relates to (a) agriculture; (b) environment; and (c) rural affairs in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs has provided £301,797,588.99* of funding to 6,169 groups and organisations over the last five financial years.
