question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their plans for the Maze Long Kesh site.
answer_text: In the period 2018-19 to 2022-23, the Development Corporation has spent £606k on maintenance of the site.  This includes essential works to the listed and retained buildings, improvements to security, and grounds maintenance across the site.In addition, the Development Corporation has a separate programme of capital works.  There has been just over £3.6million capital expenditure in this period.  This includes significant repair and restoration of the World War 2 era hangars which house the Ulster Aviation Society collection.  It continues to prove popular and attracted some 3,000 visitors over the European Heritage Open Day weekend in September 2023.  Improvements to access have also been carried out, to help facilitate the continued success of the Balmoral Show.We recognise the immense economic, historical and reconciliation potential of the site, and look forward to working with the recently appointed Board to maximise that potential.  We hope to meet with the Board over the coming weeks.  This will be an opportunity to take stock of the current position and hear from the Board about their thinking on the development of a road map for the future.  That discussion will inform consideration of the way forward and the potential timeframe.
department: The Executive Office

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an assessment has been made on how attacks on shipping vessels in the Red Sea impacts the import of livestock to the North.
answer_text: An assessment of the impact of attacks on shipping vessels in the Red Sea has not been undertaken as we do not receive livestock consignments via this transport route.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has commissioned (i) financial; (ii) social; (iii) equality; (iv) economic; and (v) rural impact assessments on the effects of proposals and policies as required by the Climate Change Act.
answer_text: As part of work to develop a draft Climate Action Plan, my Department has commissioned and is developing a range of impact assessments on the effects of policies and proposals, as required by the Climate Change Act. These include financial, social, economic, equality and rural impact assessments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many planning cases associated with (a) livestock developments; (b) poultry; and (c) slurry tanks is the NI Environment Agency currently dealing with; and (ii) when they expect to clear this backlog.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency is currently dealing with:51 livestock developments and 35 poultry developments.  Specific information on individual slurry tank development is not recorded. The Department does not have a definitive timeframe for clearing this backlog as there are many dependencies which will influence the time taken to process this caseload such as complexity of cases, quality of application submitted and staff capacity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the impact of labour shortages due to Brexit on our agri-food industries.
answer_text: Many agri-food businesses rely on migrant labour and are concerned that the labour shortages experienced in 2021 could return, especially for butchers and in fishing, given the planned increase in the salary threshold for the skilled worker visa in April 2024. I have raised these concerns with the Home Office.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what measures his Department has taken to provide due regard in respect of the Climate Change Act under Section 29 requirements for proposals and policies where departments must give due regard to the special economic and social role of agriculture, including the distinct characteristics of biogenic methane; and (ii) how due regard duty will enable farmers to continue to produce safe high quality food and to make a positive contribution to both the rural and regional economy.
answer_text: The economic impact of Farm Support and Development Programme measures is being assessed by AFBI economists. The carbon reduction impact of the programme has been modelled by RSK ADAS and DAERA officials. 		DAERA intends to analyse and set out the science surrounding GWP* within the forthcoming Climate Action Plan and, where possible, will separately assess the impact of policies and proposals on emission levels using the GWP* metric. However for the purposes of compliance with the UN Climate Change Convention, as well UK and Northern Ireland legislation, the Department is required to report GHG levels using the emission metric GWP100, which refers to the global warming potential of GHGs over 100 years.	The Farm Support and Development Programme measures are designed to reduce carbon emissions through farm productivity improvements which will improve farm profitability and resilience while maintaining agricultural output and releasing agricultural land for alternative purposes including to capture carbon and improve biodiversity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many staff in Natural Environment Division are working on assessing planning applications; (ii) how many of these staff are focused on agricultural applications; and (iii) how many of these staff deal with ammonia-air quality assessments.
answer_text: At present there are 24 scientific staff in Natural Environment Division (NED) whose primary role is to assess planning applications. This total fluctuates over time due to maternity leave, temporary promotions, availability of temporary workers and other factors. Other specialist advice may be sought across NED as and when needed.Of these staff 5 deal with air quality assessments. A degree of subject matter expertise is also available to advise on complex cases.There are no NED staff focused solely on agricultural casework as staff respond to consultations across the development hierarchy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to his Department's withdrawal of its Ammonia Standing Advice, (i) what engagement the Environment Agency is having with individual farmers in respect of their planning application; (ii) to detail the current interim policy on engagement with individual farmers who are impacted by this decision; and (iii) whether his Department will make the interim policy publicly available.
answer_text: Since implementation of the interim case-by-case approach, NIEA has engaged with planning agents and consultants, as well as key stakeholders such as the UFU.  	 	(ii) Queries related to individual planning applications are made via the appropriate planning authority. Due to the prioritisation of processing applications, NIEA is not in a position to deal with individual queries. 		iii) The interim case-by-case approach has been provided to planning consultants, planning authorities, the Planning Appeals Commission and the OEP. I hope to issue updated air quality standing advice in the near future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how much agriculture is worth to the local economy.
answer_text: The latest statistics produced by my Department indicate that agriculture accounted for 1.9 per cent of total Gross Value Added (GVA) and 2.2 per cent of total employment in Northern Ireland. In addition, there are ancillary sectors associated with agriculture which also generate GVA and employment in the economy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 6384/22-27, whether his Department will conduct a further public consultation in advance of any formal policy decision to inform Future Operational Protocol.
answer_text: Following the Call for Evidence last year, my Department is developing the revised Operational Protocol and is considering its approach to stakeholder engagement and consultation as part of this process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why he did not consider equality under Section 75 of the NI Act 1998 when revising and reviewing its policy on Ammonia Standing Advice in accordance with Paragraph 4.5 in his Department's Equality Scheme.
answer_text: My Department is currently revising the draft Future Operational Protocol to Assess the Impacts of Air Pollution on the Natural Environment and, is at the same time, carrying out the Equality Impact Assessment and Rural Needs Impact Assessment.  These will be published, when finalised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Environmental Farming Scheme.
answer_text: The Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) is a voluntary agri-environment scheme that supports farmers and land managers to carry out environmentally beneficial farming practices on agricultural land. Each agreement lasts for 5 years.	EFS is made up of three levels - Wider, Higher and Group.		Over 6,200 EFS agreements have started across 7 tranches since 2018 (4,426 Wider and 1,852 Higher); of these 2,775 agreements from tranches 1 and 2 have completed their 5-year term.  At present there are 3,500 active agreements: 2,203 in Wider and 1,300 in Higher. There are 700 businesses registered with EFS Groups delivered by environmental partners: The Rivers Trust, Lough Neagh Partnership, Ulster Wildlife Trust and the RSPB.		Currently in EFS Higher over 58,000 ha of priority habitat are under favourable management, of which almost half is moorland. Across both Higher and Wider EFS there have been 1000km of hedgerows planted or enhanced, 2,700 km of waterways protected, and over half a million trees planted by scheme participants since 2018.		In total since 2018 EFS participants have been paid £57 million to manage designated sites and priority habitats, enhance biodiversity, protect waterways, and plant trees. A further £8.6 million will be paid in April 2024.		My Department is currently considering options for EFS Higher Tranche 8 and more details on this will be issued in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the independent external resilience review of the Agricultural Shows Sector, announced in May 2022.
answer_text: The Independent Resilience Review of Agricultural Shows in Northern Ireland, published in June 2023, brought forward recommendations for both industry and government aimed at maximising the sustainability of agricultural shows in the years ahead including future funding options.  In the absence of ministers and due to the financial constraints placed upon the Department by the 2023/24 Northern Ireland budget, it has not been possible to progress work to evaluate the options proffered by the Review.  	The Northern Ireland Executive is not yet in a position to agree a budget for 2024-25. Until this is known, I am not in a position to consider any proposals in relation to funding for shows which will be considered as part of the 2024/25 budget process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the total spend on Low Emissions Slurry Spreading Equipment in Tier 1 of the Farm Business Improvement Scheme - Capital Scheme (FBIS); and (ii) the total number of farmers who have purchased this equipment under FBIS.
answer_text: To date, £9.93m grant aid has been paid to 1,443 farm businesses for the purchase of Low Emissions Slurry Spreading Equipment in Tier 1 of the Farm Business Improvement Scheme - Capital Scheme (FBIS).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he has initiated with the Ulster Poultry Federation to address the challenges posed by Brexit to their industry.
answer_text: My office has recently received correspondence from the Chairperson of the Ulster Poultry Federation requesting a meeting in relation to challenges posed by EU exit to their industry. 		My officials will contact the Federation Chair in the coming days with a view to agreeing a meeting date to discuss these matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what financial schemes his Department has in place to support the regeneration of small towns and villages.
answer_text: My Department’s regeneration policy focus is on urban settlements of more than 5,000 people. It achieves this regeneration through public realm schemes to improve footpaths and streetscape; urban development grants to the private sector to see derelict or underused buildings regenerated, development schemes which involve land assembly and revitalisation schemes. However, by working in collaboration with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department for Infrastructure, a Covid Recovery Small Settlements Regeneration Programme was developed to include the regeneration of small and rural settlements (those with a population of between 1,000 and 4,999 people) to recover from the impact of the pandemic.  The programme will see a total investment of over £28m into small settlements, supporting regeneration projects designed to make small towns and villages more appealing, accessible and vibrant. Projects were developed in partnership with local councils and are due to complete by the end of March 2025. The Department also operates the Village Catalyst Grant scheme in partnership with Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, the Northern Ireland Housing Executive and the Architectural Heritage Fund. This Scheme supports village communities to identify and deliver sustainable uses for heritage at risk that tackle rural poverty and social isolation. In two out of four pilots, wider regeneration was also stimulated.
department: Department for Communities

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on Protected Geographical Indication status for grass-fed cattle.
answer_text: Protected Geographical Indication (PGI) status for ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’, which covers grass-fed beef produced anywhere on the island of Ireland, was registered within the EU on 29 November 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why he has not conducted an Equality Impact Assessment and Rural Needs Impact Assessment on the draft Future Operational Protocol to Assess the Impacts of Air Pollution on the Natural Environment.
answer_text: My Department has commenced the Equality Impact Assessment and Rural Needs Impact Assessment processes on the draft Future Operational Protocol to Assess the Impacts of Air Pollution on the Natural Environment. Publication will take place when the Impact Assessments have been finalised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why his Department did not equality screen the decision to withdraw Ammonia Standing Advice in accordance with Paragraph 4.1 in his Department's Equality Scheme 2021-2025.
answer_text: DAERA's decision to no longer rely on the published Standing Advice was taken in the public interest to comply with existing statutory obligations. As this is a temporary measure, while new procedures are being developed, DAERA had considered that the decision did not require Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) screening. However, having engaged with the Equality Commission NI, screening on the decision is now underway.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture; and (ii) what assurances he can provide that a future agricultural budget will not be reduced to finance a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture.
answer_text: The Just Transition Fund forms part of the Climate Action Plan (CAP) and will be included in the forthcoming consultation on the CAP.  Funding for the Just Transition Fund will need to be considered in the context of budgetary pressures and priorities across the NI Executive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to reinstate the TB Eradication Partnership.
answer_text: My Department has commissioned an independent strategic review of the TB Eradication Partnership (TBEP) under the auspices of the Strategic Investment Board (SIB).The tenure of the previous members of TBEP came to an end in December 2023.  I would like to thank the outgoing Chair and members of the TBEP for their valuable contribution since the TBEP’s establishment in 2018.A draft Report has recently been received from the SIB and this will help inform my decisions on the way forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 5326/22-27.
answer_text: Due to the Assembly not sitting, AQW5326/22-27 was unanswered at the time.In relation to the matter raised, I have no plans, at this point, to make a level 3 requirement compulsory under the Farm Support and Development Programme.  I see the value of agricultural education and my Department will be encouraging continuous professional development within the sector to drive its development.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's plans for investment in Carrickmore Health Centre.
answer_text: The capital plan for a number of Trust projects (including Carrickmore Health Centre) is currently being considered within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of the Department of Health (DoH).  Timescale for a solution for Carrickmore Health Centre will be dependent on the outcome of this capital plan and confirmation on availability of funding through the DoH’s overarching 10-year capital plan.  The project will be subject to the usual business case approvals.
department: Department of Health

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 5209/22-27.
answer_text: Due to the Assembly not sitting, AQW 5209/22-27 was unanswered at the time.My officials are completing a series of Conservation Management Plans (CMPs) for Northern Ireland’s Special Areas of Conservation (SAC), which will define the conservation measures necessary to move the features of the site towards favourable condition, in line with the Department’s statutory obligations for these sites of international importance.Stakeholder engagement is a central pillar of CMP development.  Landowners were contacted at the beginning of the process and encouraged to participate in the programme. Opportunities for face to face engagement, including planned one to ones and local drop-in sessions were curtailed by the introduction of COVID restrictions.  However, these were replaced by online webinars and meetings, email and written correspondence, and the opportunity to engage directly on the field survey and management findings.  There was also general awareness raising through FAS articles and attendance at Balmoral Show.Management Plans and habitat maps for 6 of our upland SACs will be published in March 2024: these are Carn/Glenshane Pass SAC, Cuilcagh Mountain SAC, Slieve Beagh SAC, Garron Plateau SAC, Pettigoe Plateau SAC and Teal Lough SAC, all important for their peatland and freshwater habitats, and associated species.  The CMPs remain live documents to support an adaptive management framework for these important conservation sites, with further engagement undertaken as the Department moves towards implementation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's assessment of the persistent humming sound in Omagh town.
answer_text: Environmental noise and statutory nuisance legislation is primarily enforced by the environmental health units of the district councils.  This is done so under the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (CNEA). I understand that Fermanagh and Omagh District Council is aware of the concerns raised by the public regarding the persistent humming sound in Omagh town, and that it is in contact with complainants and is proceeding with routine investigations.  Any assessment will be informed by the outcome of those investigations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's Future Agriculture Policy.
answer_text: Within my Department, work is on-going on a new Farm Support and Development Programme in co-design with stakeholder organisations. The overall objectives are to transition to a more sustainable farming sector by seeking to implement policies and strategies that benefit our climate and environment, while supporting our economically and socially significant agriculture sector. The planned schemes and measures will provide levers to contribute to statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act and other environmental pressures. I intend to take some time to consider the overall content and progress of the Programme and how it addresses my priorities for the sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what impact will the ending of the Basic Payment Scheme beyond 2025 have on food security; and (ii) what implications will there be for farm businesses in the north while farm payments will continue in the south of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a detailed update on food supply security in the north of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on whether the Department has decided on a level three requirement being made compulsory under the draft Future Agricultural Policy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether his Department will seek legal advice on the impact on the provision of agricultural relief as a result of land moving away from food production under the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs' draft Peatlands strategy; and (ii) whether he will engage with HMRC to seek clarification on this matter.
answer_text: Agricultural Relief for Inheritance Tax is an Excepted Matter determined by the British Treasury and administered by HMRC.As the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) has policy responsibility for agriculture locally, and developing the Peatland Strategy 2022-2040, this would be a matter for the DAERA Minister to consider in the first instance.I am of course then happy to engage with the DAERA Minister, and in turn Treasury Ministers, should that be appropriate.
department: Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 4833/22-27, whether he will assist this group in securing mainstream funding for the Menopause and Me Programme, in order to address the limited access to appropriate support around this issue for women in rural areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagements are being held with farmers and landowners regarding upland conservation management plans.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what is the total cost to date to employ Arup consultancy.
answer_text: In 2017 Ove Arup & Partners Limited was successful in a public procurement tender exercise to produce Conservation Management Plans (CMPs) for environmentally designated sites in Northern Ireland.  Under this contract Ove Arup & Partners Limited have produced the required 16 CMPs and to date have been paid £662,354.37. The contract will conclude shortly and a further payment of some £74,500 will be paid on completion.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, further to his statement on 1 September 2022 regarding direct payments, to (i) detail the agricultural, rural and environmental projects that have received funding; (ii) provide a breakdown of the amount of funding to each project; and (iii) confirm whether the funding allocated to rural projects must be spent by March 2023.
answer_text: My statement of 01 September, 2022 relates purely to direct payments to farmers under the Basic Payment Scheme.  I can confirm that £286.9 million was issued to 23,208 farmers on that date.  This money is now with farm businesses.  My statement did not cover rural or environmental projects.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how much land currently used for grazing in (i) Severely Disadvantaged Areas; (ii) Disadvantaged Areas; and (iii) lowlands, could be impacted by the draft Peatlands Strategy.
answer_text: The table below shows the total area of peatland, according to the Northern Ireland Peatland Survey map, that falls within fields that are recorded in my Department’s mapping system, the Land Parcel Identification System. This information has been broken down into severely disadvantaged, disadvantaged and lowland areas: Land classificationArea (hectares)Severely Disadvantaged Areas115,452Disadvantaged Areas10,145Lowlands2,777Total128,373This does not necessarily mean that all of the land is currently used for grazing, only that it falls within a field parcel recognised by my Department and that it could potentially be eligible for agricultural support.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of recommendations from the report, Breaking the Glass Ceiling: Challenges Women Experience in the Local Agriculture Sector.
answer_text: I am committed to recognising and supporting the vital role of females within the agriculture sector. The main recommendations within the report are being considered within the development of the Agriculture Policy Programme, in particularly, within the Generational Renewal Measure. I am also delighted that CAFRE continues to see a growing trend in young females undertaking Further and Higher Education qualifications in agriculture. This is particularly important as it sets the trajectory for future years where we have an agriculture sector which has a gender breakdown reflecting wider society.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme.
answer_text: The Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme closed for applications in June 2021 with 214 applications received.  To date from the 114 applications deemed eligible, 75 Letters of Offer have issued offering grant totalling £2.544m.  All 75 organisations completed their mandatory ‘Managing Your Space’ OCN accredited training prior to the issue of their grant aid Letter of Offer and much of the capital works on these 75 projects is either complete or underway.  Seven eligible applicants withdrew from the Scheme for varying reasons; therefore, it is anticipated that 32 further Letters of Offer to the value of £1.160m will issue by January 2023 to the remaining eligible applicants.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total amount of support his Department provided under the Rural Development Programme, over the lifetime of the Programme.
answer_text: At the end of June 2022 the Northern Ireland Rural Development Programme (NIRDP) 2014-2020 had spent a total of €402 million.Spending under the NIRDP 2014-2020 can continue until 31 December 2023 or until EU funds are exhausted (whichever is earlier).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the labour shortages within the agri-food sector.
answer_text: The NI agri-food sector has been experiencing significant labour shortages for over 20 months and while industry may now face new challenges in relation to soaring energy and input costs, our stakeholders continue to identify labour issues as a major area of concern.Stakeholders have noted that despite their best efforts to recruit from the domestic workforce, through offering competitive wages and other incentives, the industry is struggling to secure labour and thus their only viable solution is to continue to rely on migrant workers to meet the shortfall in their labour supply needs.Since the introduction of the new UK Immigration System in January 2021 however, the use of migrant labour to fill this shortfall has become more complex and for sectors that require lower skilled labour this is no longer a viable option.I have met with a significant number of stakeholders from across a range of sub-sectors during my time as DAERA Minister and I have listened to their concerns and attempted to provide support and reassurance where possible but ultimately, immigration policy is a reserved matter and as such my primary focus has been ongoing communication with the top levels of Government in Whitehall to highlight the severity of the problems facing our agri-food sector. I have made several representations to my relevant UK counterparts on the issue including raising concerns in person as well as providing written correspondence.  In January 2021, I provided an in-depth evidence paper to the Home Office which analysed both the seasonal and non-seasonal migrant labour need in the NI agri-food sector and over the past 20 months I have continued to highlight the issues and proposed solutions outlined in this document. I wrote to the Defra Secretary of State on 07 September 2022 regarding the challenges and opportunities facing the NI agri-food sector including labour concerns.  I received a response to this correspondence on 29 September 2022 stating that the United Kingdom is committed to becoming a high-skilled, high-wage economy and more must be done to attract British workers into the sector by offering training, career options, wage increases, and increased investment in automation technology.The former chancellor Rt Hon Kwasi Kwarteng MP delivered his 2022 Growth Plan speech in late September 2022 and committed to providing a planned statement on immigration policy in late October 2022. It is expected that the new chancellor Rt Hon Jeremy Hunt MP will now deliver this update on 31st October 2022.  It is hoped that this statement will include detail on how the Government plan to help address the labour shortage issues across the economy including the agricultural sector.My officials are currently collating evidence highlighting the significant labour shortage concerns in our agri-food sector that will be provided to the Migration Advisory Committee (MAC) when they commence their review of the Shortage Occupation List which is expected to launch fol
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the work the Department is undertaking to assess the impact of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform Bill), within its areas of responsibility.
answer_text: The work the Department is undertaking is focussed on identifying the relevant legislation within its scope.  Estimates are that there are over 600 separate pieces of legislation within the Department’s responsibility that could be affected by the Bill.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has sought legal advice on the impact on the provision of agricultural relief as a result of land moving away from food production under the draft Peatlands strategy; and what engagements he has had with HMRC on this matter.
answer_text: The issue of inheritance tax and eligibility for agricultural property relief is not a devolved matter and policy responsibility rests with HM Treasury.  My Department has not sought legal advice and I have not had any engagement with HMRC on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to list the legislation that is affected by the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill, within areas of the Department's responsibility.
answer_text: The Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill was introduced on 22 September 2022.  Officials are assessing the legislation that may fall under my Department’s remit that will be subject to revocation or reform under the Bill.  Initial estimates are that over 600 separate pieces of legislation could be affected by the Bill and the exercise will therefore take some time to complete but I can assure the Member that it is being conducted as expeditiously as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what engagement he is having with the British government on the replacement of Rural Development Funding.
answer_text: I have regularly engaged with the British Government in relation to replacement of EU funding, including the Rural Development Fund. The approach taken by the British Government to the replacement of the Rural Development Programme, as a result of netting EU Tails, created a loss of £20.5m across the 22-25 period, which includes the LEADER element of the Rural Development Fund.I continue to engage regularly with the British Government on the need to fully replace all EU funding in the North and recently met both the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities Secretary of State and the Chief Secretary to the Treasury to press this matter.I remain committed to securing as much funding for here as possible.
department: Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he is having with the Department of Health on mainstream funding for the Menopause and Me programme, delivered by NI Rural Women's Network.
answer_text: I have had no engagement with the Department of Health on mainstream funding for the Menopause and Me Programme which is delivered by the NI Rural Women’s Network.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the United Kingdom Food Security Report 2021, in relation to the north of Ireland.
answer_text: The UK Food Security Report (UKFSR) provides us with an impartial analysis of food security in the United Kingdom. My officials worked with Defra colleagues on producing this report.Recognising that food supply in Northern Ireland is a key issue, I have made Food Supply Security a vital objective for the foreseeable future.  In providing an analysis on food security in the United Kingdom, the UKFSR supports this objective. Northern Ireland is facing the same global issues as other devolved nations, with the main areas of concern being the cost and availability of grains, fertilisers, fuel and seasonal labour. The UKFSR gives us all the opportunity to provide innovative solutions which will deliver food security throughout the UK and lays the groundwork for future Food Security Reports.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of penalties handed out for environmental and animal welfare breaches in the last two years.
answer_text: The number of penalties applied to area-based scheme payments for environmental and animal welfare breaches in the last two years is detailed in the table below:YearNo. environmental and animal welfare breaches20204072021355Total762
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how agriculture is contributing to the UN sustainable development goal of zero hunger.
answer_text: My Department is dedicated to improving the sustainability, productivity and resilience of the agri food sector in Northern Ireland which will all contribute to the overall UN Sustainability Development Goal of zero hunger by 2030. My officials are progressing a range of new policies across agriculture and food. These include the development of Northern Ireland’s Future Agricultural policy, with the desired high level outcomes of increased productivity, environmental sustainability, improved resilience and an effective functioning supply chain. In addition, the NI Food Strategy Framework includes a guiding principle on the right to food, where everyone in society has the right to adequate, available and accessible, safe and nutritious food. My Department also retains responsibility for Food Supply Security and my officials have developed a number of measures that allow the continuous assessment of the health of Northern Ireland’s food supply system.My officials will continue to work with stakeholders and officials from other Departments in order to progress these important areas of work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking to enforce Cross-Compliance checks on farms.
answer_text: DAERA has worked hard to establish a Cross-Compliance inspection regime that allows inspections to meet both Cross-Compliance and domestic statutory requirements.The current system ensures that inspections are targeted effectively using appropriate risk criteria and random selection.In addition to Cross-Compliance inspections which are based on risk and random selection and domestic legislative requirements, DAERA has a further procedure in place.  This procedure ensures that any incidents of environmental damage or risk to animal or human health which are reported by persons outside of the Department are followed up and any breach or crime dealt with accordingly.Inspectorates will assess the extent, severity, permanence and intent of any breach found and report this to Area-based Schemes Payment Branch for the calculation and application of any resulting penalty.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Agricultural Outlook 2022-2031 report which advocates increased productivity while reducing emissions to address global food security.
answer_text: I have noted the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Agricultural Outlook 2022-2031 Report.  It provides a comprehensive assessment of the prospects for the coming decade of national, regional and global agricultural commodity markets.  The Report projects global agricultural production to increase by 1.1% per annum, with the additional output predominantly produced in low and middle income countries.  A large share of the projected 1.5% annual growth in livestock production will result from improvements in animal productivity, stemming from more efficient herd management and feeding while lowering emissions - outcomes I am seeking to achieve with new agricultural policy measures.  	The report also projects global food consumption to increase by 1.4% over the next 	decade, mainly driven by population growth.  Most of this additional demand for food is projected to originate from low and middle income countries.  It will be important that this increased demand for food is achieved through increased productivity and better environmental sustainability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans there are, under the draft Peatland Strategy, for rural dwellers who cut turf (i) on bogs where they have turbary rights; and (ii) have turf plots that are owned privately.
answer_text: My Department is currently finalising the Northern Ireland Peatland Strategy. An action in the draft Peatland Strategy commits the Department to “Conduct a review and publish a key issues paper on peat extraction and the use of peat and peat products by 2023 and take forward any recommendations made”.  This review will capture all relevant evidence for consideration which will include the use of turbary rights.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what discussions he has had with his Ministerial colleagues regarding the impact of (i) fuel poverty; and (ii) the cost of living crisis on rural areas.
answer_text: I am hugely concerned with the continuing rising food and energy prices and the impacts they are having on Northern Ireland households. I fully understand the significant pressures surrounding fuel and energy, the costs of which have been rising to unprecedented levels. 		I have previously written to Ministerial Colleagues, seeking support for those affected by the rapidly escalating costs across many sectors and in particular the exposure faced by the agri-food and fisheries sectors given the subsequent impact this has on rural areas. 	My Ministerial colleagues are aware of my Department’s Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework which has a budget for 2022/23 of £7.23m, and currently supports a range of initiatives tackling poverty and social isolation in rural areas. 		I continue to liaise with my UK Government counterparts on these issues and to press the Secretary of State to consider Government responses that would mitigate these impacts for everyone.		In support of our industry and in particular, difficulties with cash flows, I brought the date forward for full farm Direct Payments this year to help alleviate some of the pressures being felt in rural areas, a move which was well received. 		I also continue to provide timely and sound practical advice directly to our farmers and growers through the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise with the assistance of the specifically formed Industry Taskforce.		My Department also continues to produce a weekly Food Observatory Report, which monitors the overall health of the food supply chain, incorporating the impacts of the cost of living crisis and rising fuel and energy costs to keep these issues at the forefront of discussions across all Departments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what action he is taking to address the cost of living crisis faced by rural communities; and (ii) what impact is the absence of an Executive having on the draft Rural Policy Framework.
answer_text: I will address each of your points separately as follows:My department continues to work with other public authorities to support a range of measures under its Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation Framework aimed at reducing the effects of poverty and social isolation in rural areas.  However, my department is not solely responsible for rural areas and all departments should consider what steps they might take to help address the effects of the cost-of-living crisis both in urban areas and in rural areas.The final version of the Rural Policy Framework was published in March 2022.  Since then, my officials have been engaging with key rural stakeholders and other government departments to establish the Rural Advisory Group (RAG).  The progress of this work has not been impacted by the absence of an Executive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Farming for Nature Package under the draft Future Agricultural Policy Proposals, particularly the cap on support.
answer_text: Following a period of public consultation, I announced 54 policy decisions on Future Agricultural Policy for Northern Ireland, including 10 related to the Farming with Nature Package. The Farming with Nature Package is being developed through co-design with stakeholders, and in line with the principles stated in the consultation document.  My Officials are working to develop a number of Test and Learn Pilots which will help identify solutions and recommendations to inform future scheme design. The main scheme design elements, which will be informed by the Test and Learn pilots, are not sufficiently progressed to permit meaningful consideration of a payment capping mechanism at this stage.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what proportion of Peatlands, in terms of total land mass, is utilized for agriculture in the north.
answer_text: Based on analysis of the Northern Ireland Peatland Survey map, 128,373 ha of peatland is within my Department’s Land Parcel Identification System field parcels, which represents 9% of the total land area in Northern Ireland.This does not necessarily mean that all of the land is utilised for agriculture, only that it falls within a field parcel recognised by my Department and that it could potentially be eligible for agricultural support.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to (i) provide an update on Agricultural sector plans under the Climate Change Act 2022; (ii) outline who will be involved in the development of the sector's plans; and (iii) outline what information will be used to inform and develop these plans.
answer_text: My Department is working with all NICS Departments to bring forward proposals and policies for inclusion in Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan to be published by December 2023. These proposals and policies will ensure that the relevant carbon budget is met in each of ten sectors set out in the Climate Change Act, including in the agricultural sector, for which significant work is progressing.Those involved in the development of the agricultural sector plan will include DAERA officials and a range of external stakeholders to include farming unions, industry, environmental groups, and non-governmental organisations.The development of the agricultural sector plan will be informed by the outworking of the future agricultural policy decisions I announced in March 2022 and the contributions these policies can make to achieving carbon budget targets. My officials are also currently developing a carbon auditing policy to deliver on the carbon auditing requirements of the Climate Change Act.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether, under the British government's Shared Prosperity Fund, there be a dedicated ringfenced fund for rural communities in the North, similar to the dedicated ringfenced fund for rural England.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on engagement with Whitehall on the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) with the support of Executive Departments.  A SPF Investment Plan is being developed for Northern Ireland in conjunction with a local Partnership Group.  DoF has advised that it understands that there are no ring-fenced allocations within the SPF for rural communities.Work is ongoing in Northern Ireland in implementing the Rural Policy Framework. In 2022/23 my Department has allocated £10m in resource and capital funding to ensure continued investment in our rural communities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Department is in the process of making a bid to the British Government to administer the Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: The Shared Prosperity Fund is being administered by the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities.  My Department will continue to engage in relation to the administration of this Fund through the Department of Finance led cross-departmental Future Policy and Finance Workstream.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs with regards to the draft Peatland Strategy, what consideration has he given to research published by University of York, which estimates that 81 per cent of households in the north will be in fuel poverty by January 2023.
answer_text: An action in the draft Peatland Strategy commits the Department to “Conduct a review and publish a key issues paper on peat extraction and the use of peat and peat products by 2023 and take forward any recommendations made”.  During the course of this review, all relevant research will be considered.  The timescale for the review will be indicated in the Implementation Plan for the Strategy.  My officials have initiated work on the development of the Implementation Plan and it is anticipated that it will be published by March 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a timeframe for when he intends to establish a structure to advise on the implementation of the Peatland Strategy.
answer_text: The Peatland Strategy will be finalised after taking consideration of responses to the current Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) consultation, which closes on the 4 November 2022.  My Department has already initiated work on the development of the Peatland Strategy Implementation Plan and I anticipate that this plan will be finalised in March 2023. This Implementation Plan will provide for the creation of a Stakeholder Forum, with representative membership, to provide advice to my Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Equality Commission's Report on the Impact of Brexit on Section 75 Equality Groups: EU funding.
answer_text: I welcome publication of this report and note that the recommendations largely align with the NI Executive articulated position on the Shared Prosperity Fund, including that replacement funding should at least match historic EU funds, should be delivered using existing NI structures, should align with NI strategy and policy and must be compatible with our unique system of Government, including Section 75 of the NI Act 1998.The Commissions’ report has been provided to the UK Government and through the Department of Finance who lead on this issue, we have indicated a willingness to work to resolve these issues to ensure that there is no impediment to funding flowing to NI.
department: Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Equality Commission's report on the Impact of Brexit on Section 75 Equality Groups: EU funding.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the report and note that its recommendations broadly align with the position on the Shared Prosperity Fund that was adopted by the Executive, including that replacement funding should at least match equivalent EU funds. It is also important that this should be delivered using existing NI structures and be compatible with our unique system of Government, including s.75 of the NI Act 1998.The Department of Finance lead on this matter; however, my Department continues to engage on the issue of the replacement of EU funding, through the cross-departmental Future Policy and Finance Work stream.A number of our Voluntary and Community Sector partners have successfully bid to the Community Renewal Fund and existing European Social Fund Projects which has helped support individuals with convictions to secure employment.
department: Department of Justice

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the Equality Commission's report on the Impact of Brexit on Section 75 Equality Groups: EU funding.
answer_text: EU funding secured by the Department has been used to support major infrastructure projects in particular, roads schemes and rail network upgrades. Reduced funding to the Department, of any nature, has the potential to impact on our service delivery and impact all citizens including those in Section 75 groups.   While the Department of Finance lead on this matter, Departments here have taken a joined-up approach to the replacement of EU funds, co-ordinated through the cross-departmental Future Policy and Finance Workstream, which is attended by my officials.I note that the report’s recommendations largely align with the Executive’s position on the Shared Prosperity Fund, that replacement funding equivalent to EU funds should be delivered here using existing structures and must be compatible with our unique system of Government, including Section 75 of the NI Act 1998.
department: Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the Equality Commission's report on the Impact of Brexit on Section 75 Equality Groups: EU funding.
answer_text: I welcome publication of the Equality Commission’s report and share concerns about the British Government’s approach to the replacement of EU funding and the negative impacts on Section 75 groups.The Department of Finance leads on this matter on behalf of the Executive, and my Department continues to support representations to the British Government insisting that replacement funding should at least match equivalent EU funds, should be delivered using existing local structures and must be compatible with our unique situation, including compliance with Section 75.
department: Department for Communities

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Equality Commission's report on the impact of Brexit on Section 75 Equality Groups: EU funding.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the report and note that its recommendations largely align with the position on the Shared Prosperity Fund that has been adopted by the Executive.  This includes that replacement funding should at least match equivalent EU funds previously available.  Replacement funding should also be delivered using existing Northern Ireland structures and must be compatible with our unique system of Government, including Section 75 of the NI Act 1998.Executive Departments, including the Department of Health, have taken a joined-up approach to the replacement of EU Funds.  This approach is being led by the Department of Finance through the work of the Future Policy and Finance Workstream.  My officials will continue to work closely with the Department of Finance who continue to work with the UK Government to resolve these issues to ensure there is no impediment to funding flowing to NI.
department: Department of Health

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the Equality Commission's report on the impact of Brexit on Section 75 Equality Groups: EU funding.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the report and note that its recommendations largely align with the position on the Shared Prosperity Fund that was adopted by the former Executive. These include the recommendations that replacement funding should at least match equivalent EU funds, should be delivered using existing NI structures and must be compatible with our unique system of Government, including s.75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998. Commitments to continue support and funding for relevant ‘PEACE’ cross border initiatives were included in the Withdrawal Agreement between the UK and EU. Therefore, delivery of the then PEACE IV Shared Education programme and development of the successor PEACE PLUS programme were unaffected by Brexit. Education related programmes supported under PEACE PLUS will provide important opportunities for children and young people from different backgrounds to learn together, to support good relations, tackle educational disadvantage, and to address mental health and wellbeing needs. I also welcome the reference within the report to Community and Place, Supporting Local Business and People and Skills. There is an important early intervention role for education to play in delivering interventions to complement this work, which requires long-term sustainable funding placing Northern Ireland on a par with other parts of the UK.
department: Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the Equality Commission's report on the Impact of Brexit on Section 75 Equality Groups: EU funding.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of this report by the Equality Commission, it highlights a number of significant issues with the approach the British Government have taken to the replacement of EU funding and with the wider use of the new powers in Part 6 of the Internal Market Act 2020.The report highlights the failure by the British Government to fully replace equivalent EU funds through the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). The SPF will provide £19m in 22/23, £33m in 23/24 and £74m in 24/25 with no flexibility in this funding profile. This compares poorly with the average £64m per annum we received from comparable 2014-20 EU Funds and represents a loss over the three year period of around £65m before factoring in inflation. The report echoes my own and the Executive view that replacement funding should be delivered locally, according to local priorities through existing structures and in full compliance with s.75 rather than the approach the British Government are currently taking which is to deliver to priorities set by British Government Ministers through a Whitehall department which is not designated under s.75.The report also notes deficiencies in other funds which are being operated by the British Government, highlighting in particular the disappointing level of funding that round one of the Levelling Up Fund provided here and the failure to provide information on the governance of both the Levelling Up and Community Renewal Funds. I have raised these issues in my engagement with British Government Ministers on this fund.
department: Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the plans to develop programs under the draft Rural Policy Framework; and (ii) to outline how his Department intends to do develop those programs in partnership with rural communities.
answer_text: Please find an update to your requests as follows: -During 2022/23 my Department has developed and delivered the following initiatives under the Rural Policy Framework: Micro Food Business Investment Scheme;Platinum Jubilee Pollinator Garden Scheme;Rural Community Rescue Scheme;Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme; andSchool Pollinator Garden Scheme.Since the launch of the Rural Policy Framework, my officials have been engaging with key rural stakeholders and other government departments to establish the Rural Advisory Group (RAG).  Once established, the RAG will have principles of co-design at its core and will ensure a strong element of bottom-up involvement in the design and development of future rural programmes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what discussions he has had with HMRC on the potential rise in mileage prices for volunteers as the price of fuel impacts on volunteering for the community transport partnership social car scheme.
answer_text: I have not been involved in discussions with HMRC on the potential rise in mileage prices for volunteer drivers.  I fully appreciate the invaluable contribution and support that volunteer drivers provide to the community transport sector, particularly to those organisations funded by my Department.  I am aware of, and support, the Approved Mileage Allowance Payment (AMAP) Campaign led by Community Transport Association (CTA) asking the UK Government to increase the rate paid to volunteer drivers.  The campaign is particularly relevant given the impacts of the current high fuel costs, and general costs of living, crisis.
department: Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much funding has been provided to date by the British government's Shared Prosperity Fund to rural communities, as part of the agreement to replace the European Union's Common Agricultural Policy.
answer_text: No funding has been provided to date to rural or any other communities here from the British Government’s Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). The British Government’s promises to replace EU Funding have not been met and we are facing a significant shortfall in funding as a result. The SPF will provide £19m in 22/23, £33m in 23/24 and £74m in 24/25 with no flexibility in this funding profile. This compares poorly with the average £64m per annum the north received from comparable 2014-20 EU Funds and represents a loss over the three year period of around £65m before factoring in inflation. This figure may also have to be revised as the British Government continue to expand the scope of the EU funding that SPF will replace. This significantly lower level of funding is also not being provided to the Assembly or Executive to support those activities previously funded through EU Funds but will be delivered directly by Whitehall creating a significant risk of duplication and reduced impact.
department: Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Equality Commission's report on the Impact of Brexit on Section 75 Equality Groups: EU funding.
answer_text: I am not currently in a position to provide my assessment of this report as it is currently under consideration by officials. This will take some time to complete but I can assure the Member that it is being conducted as expeditiously as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what impact the Irish Bovine Viral Diarrhea Program will have on the export of cattle from the north to the south of Ireland; and (ii) will he make a commitment to an all-island approach to animal health and disease on the island of Ireland.
answer_text: The European Commission approved the Republic of Ireland (ROI) BVD eradication programme on 18 July 2022.  Since then, cattle moving from Northern Ireland (NI) to the ROI for breeding and production purposes must meet additional export criteria.  My Department has put in place new processes and published guidance to support NI exporters in meeting the additional requirements and enable trade to continue.  I am aware that the Irish Government aims to achieve BVD Free Status in 2023. When that happens, no animal that has been vaccinated for the disease will be permitted to move into the ROI.  My Department will issue further advice to exporters on this issue in advance of BVD Free status being achieved.  It also intends to consult shortly on proposals to introduce herd restrictions to reduce the prevalence of BVD in NI.  This should, in turn, go some way towards ameliorating the potential trade barriers arising.The island of Ireland is recognised as a single epidemiological unit.  As such, NI and the ROI adopt a similar approach to the surveillance, prevention and control of certain animal diseases.  Engagement between the two administrations is carried out under the auspices of the all-island Animal Health and Welfare Strategy, which was agreed in 2010.  The aim of the Strategy is to improve animal health through the alignment, in so far as is possible, of animal disease control policies within both jurisdictions.  Co-operation on the delivery of the action plan which underpins the Strategy is ongoing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans his Department has in place to support rural businesses (i) financially; (ii) via policy; and (iii) via legislation.
answer_text: My Department has a broad portfolio of responsibility. It leads on economic policy and strategy, working with delivery partners such as Invest NI and InterTradeIreland on the programmes and projects that support business development, investment and job creation across Northern Ireland.Through Invest NI, for example, there is significant existing support that may be available to rural businesses.  This includes but is not restricted to - assistance with product development, increasing export sales, R&D, employing new staff, skills development and operating more efficiently. Businesses seeking support should primarily contact Invest NI’s Business Support team on 0800 181 4422 or enquiry@investni.com to discuss what may be available. The Business Support Team can also provide information on a wide range of support options currently available from other organisations.Invest NI also provides a comprehensive range of information for businesses on www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk. The NIbusinessinfo site includes a business support finder tool which is a live database of all business support currently available throughout Northern Ireland.Nowhere is my ambition for inclusive growth more evident than Project Stratum. As part of the 2017 Conservative and DUP Confidence and Supply Agreement, £150m was secured to make Northern Ireland 100% fibre to the premises. The scheme rolling out the funding is called Project Stratum and is proving to be transformational for rural communities.Project Stratum is managed by my Department and is making gigabit capable broadband available to 85,000 primarily rural homes and businesses without access to a service of 30 Mbps. Where funding becomes available my Department would like to improve connectivity further, in line with the UK Government’s ambitions under Project Gigabit to ensure that every home and business across the UK has access to gigabit-capable infrastructure, regardless of location. It is expected that the majority of premises eligible to benefit from such an intervention would also be located in predominantly rural areas.
department: Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the supply of grain to the north of Ireland.
answer_text: I recently met with the Northern Ireland Grain Trade Association (NIGTA) and visited Belfast Dock where I saw for myself the scale and complexities around grain trade in Northern Ireland. 		I am fully aware of the importance of the grain trade to the NI Agri-food Industry, with some 90% of all ingredients for the 2.6m tonnes of compound feed manufactured in NI, imported from global marketplaces. I too am aware of the current challenges on the supply of feed raw materials, particularly as a result of the war in Ukraine.  Grain is traded on an all-island basis and my officials are in regular discussions with The Department of Agriculture Food and Marine (DAFM) and have ongoing engagement with NIGTA to monitor the situation.		NIGTA has indicated that prices of imported cereals are around those pre-war, however, the market remains volatile and current increases in energy prices will have a knock-on effect on compounding costs. 		My Officials continue to liaise with stakeholders on issues relating to feed material supply and maintain a programme of risk based Official Controls for imported feed. This is more important than ever given that, with the ongoing disruption to conventional global feed supply channels, feed materials may be sourced from locations presenting increased risks but in order to seek to ensure continuity of supply these risks will have to be minimised and managed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following his bid for £70 million to help farmers and farming with the rising costs of feed and energy, how he will ensure that support is provided to farmers and farming given the absence of an Executive.
answer_text: Northern Ireland producers are facing unprecedented rises in input costs.  Whilst a request was made to the Department of Finance for a funding envelope of £70M to help address challenges, no progress can be made until this is considered by the Executive alongside other pressures and demands on the Northern Ireland budget. 		Regional agricultural policy and support is a fully devolved matter, but I have written to and met with the former Secretary of State, Rt Hon George Eustice MP on a number of occasions to reiterate my concerns regarding the unprecedented increases in input costs which is impacting the agricultural sector across all of the UK.  I have restated those concerns to the new Secretary of State for DEFRA, Ranil Jayawardena MP and requested an urgent meeting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how farmers can access carbon audits; (ii) how many diverse types of carbon calculators are available, both north and south, on the island of Ireland; and (iii) whether the various tools to measure carbon on farms provide different results.
answer_text: My Department currently offers carbon audits to those farmers who are members of the CAFRE Business Development Group programme.  There are 3,000 farmers participating in this knowledge transfer programme which encourages farmers to discuss and share knowledge and aims to improve the technical efficiency of farm businesses.There are a wide range of carbon calculator tools available which vary in complexity and differences exist, for example, in the data required for different enterprises, emission factors and sequestration figures used, the detail of reports produced and the standard of accreditation. A small number of these are suitable for use within Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.Given the variation that exists in carbon calculator tools, the results produced from different calculators are not comparable.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 2444/22-27, for a breakdown of the different techniques, and the cost of each technique, employed for the Upland Peatland Projects at the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise; and to outline the value to farmers in terms of a public good.
answer_text: The Forest to Bog sites on deep peat have been completed to date at a cost of £4250/ha. This work entailed; stump flipping and ground smoothing to eliminate minor drains (3m apart) and forestry ridges, infilling of main drains (every 50m) with waste forestry timber, drain blocking with peat plugs, removal and burying of brash mats, turf relocation to assist with revegetation.Forest to Bog sites on shallow peat have been completed at a cost of £3500/ha. These sites have required a mix of techniques as site condition and peat depth has been variable within small areas. Works have included, mulching, brash mat removal for drain blocking, peat plugs and turf relocation to assist revegetation.  Shallow peat is normally unsuitable for stump flipping and ground smoothing techniques.My Department Officials have also demonstrated areas of cell bunding at this site which have been completed at a cost of £6000/ha.Open moorland has been re-wet at a cost of £300/ha. This cost covers the blocking of minor drains and the range of methods demonstrated at the CAFRE Hill Farm are; peat dams from borrow pits, breaking infilled drains, plastic film inserts, zippering and wave damming. There is minimal variation in costs across these methods. Higher intensity collector drains require a greater degree of engineering to effectively block flows of water without causing erosion. My Department Officials have demonstrated the installation of plastic piling within collector drains at a cost of £1000/100m. The two main variables impacting on the cost of any rewetting project are the slope of the site and the number of drains on the site to be blocked. The restoration of peatland is a key environmental objective to mitigating climate challenges, directly reducing carbon losses to air and groundwater which will maintain the value of the carbon stored in peatlands in Northern Ireland.Restored peatlands also provide valuable ecosystem services including improved biodiversity, water quality, water resource and flood alleviation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider setting up an all-island wool council; and whether he will make a commitment to engage with the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine on this issue.
answer_text: I am aware that the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM) has recently published its “Review of Market Opportunities for Irish-Grown Wool-Based Products” and that one of the recommendations in the report is to establish an All-Island “Irish Grown” Wool Council.My officials have been working closely with officials in Defra and the other devolved administrations to explore options for a review of British Wool.  In addition, they are currently considering the recommendations in the DAFM review and plan to engage with DAFM officials to consider the proposals and their impact on Northern Ireland in more detail.
