question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the all-island Protected Geographical Indicator scheme for beef; and whether he will consider a grass-fed beef and carbon footprint calculator.
answer_text: Following Defra’s opposition (on behalf of the NI beef industry) to Bord Bia’s application for Protected Geographical Indication (PGI) status for ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’, the European Commission invited both sides to enter into discussion to resolve the opposition. I’m pleased to confirm that agreement was reached on 11 July 2022 and a modified application, which now includes NI, was submitted to the European Commission on 3 August. In order to accommodate NI, a number of amendments were made. The key ones were:•	Inclusion of NI practices for ensuring traceability of cattle;•	Inclusion of NI farming and slaughter practices; and•	Updating statistics on practices and the quality of beef to include NI.The EC will scrutinise the modified documents and may seek amendment and/or clarification.  Once the EC is satisfied, the application will undergo a new opposition phase, where other Member States and Third Countries can oppose the application. Provided no admissible oppositions are raised, or those that are can be resolved, the EC will then grant the PGI on an all-island basis.In order to verify the grass fed status of beef produced in Northern Ireland, industry has developed a grass-fed data standard which could potentially be recognised and verified through the use of a carbon footprint calculator. My Department are currently gathering evidence to develop a policy for farm carbon auditing in Northern Ireland, part of this exercise will consider the use of carbon calculators to measure a farm carbon footprint.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: The Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 (the Act) came into operation for Northern Ireland departments and district councils on 01 June 2017 and for the other public authorities listed in the schedule to the Act on 01 July 2018. 		My Department published the 4th Rural Needs Annual Monitoring Report in December 2021 which contains information compiled by public authorities on the exercise of their functions under Section 1 of the Act. The report shows that the Act is becoming more firmly embedded within public authorities and that public authorities are routinely completing Rural Needs Impact Assessment templates in respect of policies, strategies, plans and public services. My Department is currently collating information for inclusion in the 5th Rural Needs Annual Monitoring Report which is scheduled to be published in December this year. 			My Department continues to provide support to other public authorities to help ensure the Act is implemented effectively and to help deliver better outcomes for rural dwellers. This includes the provision of advice, guidance, training and a range of other resources on the DAERA website. 		DAERA has also established a Rural Needs Network aimed at facilitating co-operation and the sharing of information between public authorities to which the Rural Needs Co-ordinator in each public authority is invited. My Department has also completed the first review of the list of bodies and persons set out in the Schedule to the Act.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for details of the roads in the Omagh district which are earmarked for resurfacing and improvements, following the awarding of the Department's resurfacing contract.
answer_text: Details of the resurfacing and road improvement programme for the Omagh district will be included within the Department’s Omagh & Fermanagh District Council Report, which is currently being finalised following recent confirmation of the Division’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation.  The Report will, in due course be presented to the Council, published on the Department’s website, and placed in the Assembly library.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.
department: Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what action his Department is taking to increase the use of renewable energy on farms; and (ii) what are the indicative costs for farms to implement such measures to reduce greenhouse gas emissions on-farm.
answer_text: As part of our future agricultural programme, we are seeking to improve energy efficiency on farms.  This will include the use of renewable energy as tariffs for renewable energy are generally no different from other energy tariffs.My Officials are also working with colleagues in the Department for the Economy (DfE) on the potential for renewable energy production using farm waste and other sources.  This work is at an early stage but offers potential.The Energy Strategy for Northern Ireland – ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’ outlines a roadmap to 2030 on the pathway to deliver the 2050 vision of net zero carbon and affordable energy.   It sets out an ambitious target of 70% renewable electricity consumption in Northern Ireland by 2030, which has since been increased to 80% under the NI Climate Change Act 2022.   To achieve this target, DfE is leading on bringing forward a new support mechanism to incentivise renewable electricity development in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide indicative costings of the different techniques employed for the Upland Peatland Projects at CAFRE.
answer_text: My Department officials have been working on two peatland restoration projects at the CAFRE Hill Farm Centre. The first is to restore an area of peatland which was forested and the second is to re-wet open moors. The Forest to Bog site has required a significant level of re-engineering, using heavy plant. This work involves stump flipping and ground smoothing to eliminate minor drains and ridges, the infilling of main drains, removal and burying of brash mats and turf relocation to assist revegetation. Indicative costs of this work are approximately £4250/ha.Re-wetting open moors is a simpler process using a combination of peat dams and other technologies to block minor drains. The process also involves breaking any buried stone strains on the site. Indicative costs of this work are approximately £300/ha.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's plans to address the lack of respite services for young people with severe and complex needs in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) acknowledges the current difficulties regarding residential short breaks for families with children with disabilities. Due to the increasing level of complexity presented by children and rate of placement breakdown, short breaks facilities have been temporarily repurposed to provide medium-long term residential care. Due to the limited availability of bed based Short Breaks over the last two years the frequency of access to this service has been determined by the social work assessment of need.  The service holds fortnightly panels to allocate places within the Short Breaks service.   Allocation of traditional break beds is carried out as equitable as possible so that where possible carers have some level of short break provision.The WHSCT has 2 Residential short breaks facilities for children, both of which have been unavailable for short breaks recently. This has been due to a need to respond to placement breakdowns by providing urgent residential accommodation in Avalon and an interim placement for a high needs/high risk child in Rosebud. The Trust recognises the stress this places on children with disability and their families, however Rosebud has recently re-opened for short breaks which will help alleviate some of this pressure. Work is ongoing to seek alternative long term accommodation for those children currently residing in Avalon with a view to providing residential short breaks at the earliest opportunity. The Trust is actively working on plans to develop capacity for emergency placement breakdowns and for children needing medium to long term residential care which will include developing a hybrid model for short term and long term capacity in one of their facilities as well as developing a new Respite care service similar to Respite Foster care. The Trust is liaising with RQIA and the Department on these plans. Children’s Disability managers have been meeting monthly with Rosebud parents since December 2021 to keep them informed of progress and have also now had two meetings with Avalon parents.To meet the future service needs, the HSCB (now the Department’s Strategic Planning & Performance Group) developed a Regional Framework for Children’s Disability Services, which proposes a new overarching model of care for children with a disability in Northern Ireland. This draft framework outlines a new model of practice based on the principles of: (i) early intervention; (ii) collaboration and multi-agency working; (iii) co-design and decision making with families; and (iv) person centred care.The implementation of the framework will be underpinned by four strategic work streams, which will require significant transformation across Health & Social Care services:Improving our early offer of help and community based supports; Developing additional effective residential short breaks, fostering and intensive family support;Rethinking our approach to residential provision and how we suppor
department: Department of Health

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what financial contribution his Department has made to the Loughs Agency in this financial year.
answer_text: The Loughs Agency is a NorthSouth Body, established under the Belfast/Good Friday Agreement 1998.  It is co-sponsored by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Irish Government’s Department of the Environment, Climate and Communications. The Loughs Agency has prepared a Business Plan for 2022 recommending the 2022 Budget/Grant provision of up to £6.246/€7.263 million. The Business Plan has been approved by the co-sponsors enabling funding to be released to the Agency, which is provided on a 50/50 basis by DAERA and DECC.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on (i) the potential outbreak of foot and mouth disease on a pig farm in Britain; and (ii) what action he is taking to ensure an all Ireland approach is adopted to negate this potential disease.
answer_text: A private veterinary practitioner (PVP) reported suspicion of Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD) at an outdoor pig unit of 1,000 sows in Norfolk, England on 23 June 2022.  An epizootic disease investigation was commenced immediately. Based on the initial clinical findings notifiable vesicular disease could not be ruled out and samples were taken for testing. As a precautionary measure the UK Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) took the decision to introduce a 10 km Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) around the suspect premises restricting the movement of susceptible animals and products which could transmit the disease. While initial lab results did not indicate the presence of notifiable vesicular disease further sampling and testing was required to complete the investigation.  On receipt of further test results from the UK national reference laboratory the UK CVO was able to negate the presence of FMD, swine vesicular disease and vesicular stomatitis with the 10km TCZ being revoked on 27 June 2022. My Officials worked closely with colleagues in Great Britain (GB), key stakeholders and their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland (ROI) throughout this investigation.Northern Ireland (NI) is recognised as a separate epidemiological unit from the rest of the UK.  For certain animal diseases NI and ROI adopt a similar approach to disease surveillance, prevention and control. Sustained co-operation between both administrations is essential to prevent the introduction or reduce the further spread of disease in the event of a suspect or confirmed case of epizootic disease.  Collaboration between both jurisdictions is ongoing and frequent and increases during suspect or confirmed cases of epizootic disease.DAERA and the Department of Agriculture, Fisheries and the Marine (DAFM) have agreed common chapters which set out the agreed approach that will be taken by both Departments for the main epizootic diseases, including FMD, Avian Influenza, African Horse Sickness and Bluetongue.  The common chapters cover a number of areas where co-operation and agreement will be of particular importance and of mutual benefit during a suspected or confirmed outbreak of disease including, cross-border communications,   tracings, trade and the management of external communications.While there have been no outbreaks of FMD in either jurisdiction since 2001, my officials have liaised very closely with their DAFM counterparts in managing communications during suspect cases of disease.  For example, on the most recent suspect case of FMD in Norfolk there was frequent sharing of information and during findings of avian influenza in the past year which resulted in the simultaneous implementation of the Avian Influenza Prevention Zones (AIPZ) and other measures in both jurisdictions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures he will put in place to help farmers meet the challenges facing the sector following Brexit and as it leaves Pillar 2 of the Common Agriculture Policy.
answer_text: Now that we have left the EU and the Common Agricultural Policy, we have the opportunity to design a more tailored agriculture policy which better meets the needs of Northern Ireland, meaning that our policies do not have to be constrained by the EU CAP Pillar 1 and Pillar 2 construct.On 24 March 2022, I announced 54 decisions on future agricultural support which fleshed out the Agricultural Policy Framework that I announced last August. These decisions are intended to provide a targeted approach to the provision of agricultural support to help farmers to develop their businesses, no matter where they farm, to become more productive, profitable and resilient, and to maximise the sustainable returns from the assets at their disposal.Decisions that are central to this policy focus on sustainability include a Farm Sustainability Payment which recognises the need for continuation of an income support ‘safety net’ and a Beef Sustainability Package with two parts: a Suckler Cow Measure and a Beef Carbon Reduction Measure. Measures also include a Farming with Nature Package initially focussing on actions to reverse the declining trends by creating and restoring habitats that are important for species diversity and Farming for Carbon measures which encourage low carbon emission farming practices.The Policy Framework also embraces other topics and areas of work including investment measures, knowledge transfer and innovation programmes, and the development of Supply Chain measures.  A Generational Renewal programme will encourage longer term planning for farm businesses; and DAERA is planning to invest in the initiation of an industry led Ruminant Genetics Programme.These policy decisions provide a clear direction of travel for future agricultural policy in Northern Ireland, however there is still significant work to be done, which my officials will continue to take forward, along with continued engagement with stakeholders.These new agricultural policy decisions and measures will be introduced over the next number of years, probably from 2024 at the earliest, though some pilot projects may commence before 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the volatility of the market on (i) suckler beef farmers, and (ii) sheep farmers.
answer_text: I am concerned by the significant rise in input costs, particularly for feed, fertiliser and fuel, which have the likelihood to have a severe impact on the profitability of beef and sheep farm businesses.  It is welcomed that beef and sheep producer prices have risen and are now 10% and 9% respectively above the price at the start of 2022 although, I do note that the sheep price has fallen recently which is a concern.  It is also encouraging that there have been high yields of first cut silage which will go some way to ease the fodder supply situation in the winter months. We will monitor the situation with respect to second cut.		Managing the rising costs of production and making the optimum use of inputs has never been more important. Officials at The College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) lead the Industry Taskforce that is working closely together to ensure a joined up approach of advisory messages to the agriculture industry and provide the sector with practical help and support.  CAFRE has a dedicated webpage providing advice on managing the rising costs of production and has delivered a series of on-farm information events.		In May 2022, AgriSearch and the UFU commissioned an industry-wide survey and received responses from over 800 farmers on the impact of costs and availability of inputs on their farm businesses. One of the outcomes of the survey showed almost three quarters of those farmers who purchased fertiliser intended sowing more than 10% less than the previous year. Subsequently incredible volumes of first cut silage have been ensiled, due to excellent growing conditions and cut later due to the weather constraints.  However, concerns have been raised regarding quality and that is being assessed. A further industry survey will be completed by the end of the summer to determine the quality and quantity of silage produced and this will help inform those advisory messages to the industry during the autumn and winter months.		I recognise that there continues to be volatility in the market and the overall situation remains difficult given the scale of the input cost rises. There needs to be confidence within the industry over the coming months and to assist with cash flows, you will be aware that I announced my intention to bring forward full Direct Payments from 16 October 2022 to 01 September 2022, subject to the necessary legislation changes, financial approvals and system upgrades.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the External Independent Review into how the Department addressed concerns raised during the recent industrial tribunal case taken by Dr Tamara Bronckaers.
answer_text: The review is progressing well and I understand that the first phase is almost complete.  As indicated in the review terms of reference, the nature of the findings of that first phase will inform the timings for the second phase but it remains the intention to complete that phase quickly and ideally by the end of July so that any recommendations or lessons for the future can be applied not just in my Department but across all departments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the ammonia action plan and the timescale for the publication of the consultation; (ii) how the Department will ensure the action plan meets its (a) equality; (b) human rights; and (c) rural needs obligations; and (iii) for his assessment of the impact this action plan will have on farms in areas of natural constraint.
answer_text: My Department has developed a comprehensive draft ammonia strategy which I am currently finalising for publication. My Department will be fulfilling all of its obligations under equality, human rights and rural needs legislation and the appropriate screening documents will be published with the consultation. Impacts will depend on the final mix of measures and approaches chosen. However, I am determined that the ammonia strategy delivers sustained and tangible reductions in emissions and supports nature conservation actions in a way which is fair, proportionate and as cost-effective as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he will publish a food strategy to protect domestic food security in the context of the war in Ukraine and the impact of Brexit, including the loss of basic payment scheme, trade deals and labour shortages faced by farmers and farming; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact that the absence of an Executive is having on the draft food strategy.
answer_text: My Officials carried out an eight week public consultation exercise on proposals for a Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework in autumn 2021. The consultation sought views on the proposed vision, principles and goals of a Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework. Overall, the responses indicated a strong endorsement for the proposed approach. The summary of responses has been published on the DAERA website, enabling stakeholders to see the overall views that were expressed in the consultation exercise.As the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework cuts across the responsibilities of a number of Northern Ireland Departments, it requires Executive approval before publication. In the interim, my officials will continue to work with officials from other Departments through the recently formed Food Programme Board to develop detailed proposals for an extensive programme of work.I have written to other Ministerial colleagues to update them on the outcome of the consultation, and to ask if they are content with the proposed final version of the Framework which will be for future Executive sign-off. I have sought their agreement that officials will continue working with DAERA to work up the underlying implementation plans for future consideration. To date, positive responses have been received from departments of Economy, Health, Education, Justice and Infrastructure.The negotiation of trade deals falls under international relations and therefore is not a devolved matter.  The draft Food Strategy Framework will take into account the overall trading environment as it impacts on Northern Ireland.Although there is no Executive at present, I continue to engage on a very regular basis with the UK Government Ministers. I have met with Secretary of State, Rt. Hon George Eustice MP, recently in person and pressed home repeatedly the need for Government intervention to support farmers and growers right across the UK.Through the College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise, and the Industry Taskforce, I will continue to ensure that my Department provides timely and sound practical support to our farmers and growers.The NI Protocol continues to hinder how NI produced foods with additional checks adding costs to business, leading to the withdrawal of goods and some businesses from the NI market. Components for local businesses are harder to get and materials imported to GB distribution hubs are double tariffed to NI consumers and business.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the draft Audit of Inequalities 2021-2025 and Action Plan.
answer_text: The consultation for the Audit of Inequalities 2021-2025 and Action Plan closed in March 2022.		A Consultation Outcomes Report, taking account of responses from various groups, was completed.  The Audit of Inequalities 2021-25 and Action Plan has been published on the DAERA Internet and is available at this web page: 	https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/daeras-equality-scheme#toc-1.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what commitment he can give to address the financial crisis being faced by farmers; and for his assessment of the impact of the absence of an Executive on farmers and farming.
answer_text: Northern Ireland producers are facing significant pressures in relation to unprecedented rises in input costs of energy, feed and fertiliser. While markets for agricultural products have been responding and prices rising, this has not been sufficient across all sectors to addresses the significant increase in the costs of production.  There still needs to be increased farm gate prices to restore balance and underpin the economic viability of primary production as a whole. The market flux that we are witnessing is as a direct result of global influences over which we have no control.  Although there is no Executive at present, I continue to engage on a very regular basis with the UK Government at a senior level. You will be aware, for example, that I meet and correspond frequently with Secretary of State, George Eustice MP, most recently meeting him in person in London, and press home repeatedly the need for Government intervention to support farmers and growers right across the UK. In order to assist our local industry with difficulties in cash flows, I have announced my intention to bring forward the commencement of full Direct Payments from 16 October 2022 to 01 September 2022, subject to the necessary legislation changes, financial approvals and system upgrades.I have delivered a pig sector support scheme to address some of the challenges affecting pig producers. The Scheme opened on 19th May and payments of over £1.2m have been made to approximately 130 producers.Through the College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise, and the Industry Taskforce, I will continue to ensure that my Department provides timely and sound practical support to our farmers and growers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of (i) the British Government's Levelling Up agenda; and (ii) Brexit and the withdrawal of EU funding, on rural communities.
answer_text: As you will be aware, overall responsibility for funding falls under the remit of Minister Murphy and the Department of Finance (DoF). DoF officials have provided an overall assessment of the impact of the Levelling Up agenda and this is attached at Annex A (a copy will be placed in the Assembly Library).In order to ensure that rural communities continue to receive financial support as we transition from EU funding, my Department has provided a wide range of schemes and initiatives. In addition to support from the LEADER programme which amounted to £7.3m in 2021/22, my Department also provided the following funding:SchemeAmount21/22Planned22/23Rural Tourism scheme£3.9m£0.4mRural Business and Community Investment Fund£3.5m£4.9mTackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation framework£7.4m£6.7mProject Stratum£7.5m£10.25mLEADER£7.3m£0.7mCovid funding #£8.2m-This funding supported the development of a Small Settlement scheme in conjunction with DfC and DfI, a Village Pollinator Garden Scheme, Community Rescue scheme and a Rural Tourism Collaboration Experiences Programme. This has helped to ensure that rural communities have benefitted from a wide range of support and more flexible delivery mechanisms that help to address rural need in a much more flexible way.  Looking ahead my Department will continue to seek out funding opportunities from a broad range of funding streams and has already secured a share of €80m funding from PEACE Plus, £7.4m has also been earmarked for DAERA from the Complimentary fund and at least an estimated £12m is earmarked to come through from other City Deal and Complementary fund projects. In addition my Department is developing the Rural Business and Community Investment Fund which will help to ensure that funding previously provided through Priority 6 of the Rural Development Programme is replaced and by schemes and initiatives that are more flexible, more efficient and more responsive to the needs of rural communities across Northern Ireland.  Annex ALevelling Up Overview	The majority of funding delivered under the levelling up agenda will be via the Financial Assistance Powers of the UK Internal Market Act. This is a novel power and is currently being used to redirect spending away from our departments to Whitehall.	DoF Officials have established and are maintaining networks and partnerships across the NICS and Whitehall and are engaging with local councils and delivery groups and partners to understand the impacts and influence the use of the Levelling Up suite of funds.	Prior to the White Paper being published the Chancellor of the Exchequer announced on the 4th of March 2021 the following funds under the Levelling up agenda;	The Community Renewal Fund (Total £12m in 1 FY)	Levelling Up Fund (Round 1 - £49m)	The Community Ownership Fund (Round 1 - £782k)	The Shared Prosperity Fund (Total £127m across 3 FY)	Throughout the last year there have also been other funds facilitated under the Levelling Up Agenda and dealt with thr
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what the barriers are to introducing an all-Ireland animal cruelty register.
answer_text: An all-Ireland animal cruelty register could only be created if establishing a register were considered feasible in both jurisdictions. 	The Department of Justice holds the information that would be needed to establish such a register in Northern Ireland. However, as any register would be animal welfare related, it indicated that my Department should lead on exploring whether or not establishing one is feasible. Following detailed consideration, my Department has regrettably concluded that the creation of a register is untenable because a clear evidential need has not been identified, the costs of establishing and maintaining a register would be significant and the data protection implications involved are prohibitive. 	No animal cruelty register exists in the Republic of Ireland nor are there any plans to create one there.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what is the total amount of funding that his Department has received from Horizon Europe Program for Agriculture science research.
answer_text: My Department’s arms-length body the Agri-Food and Bioscience Institute (AFBI) has a number of active applications under consideration for Horizon Europe research funding in the 2021 Cluster 6 call which relates to Food, Bioeconomy, Natural Resources, Agriculture and Environment. The results of the first Horizon Europe funding calls have not been published to date so there is no data on the total amount of funding awards for agriculture science research. Under Horizon 2020 which preceded Horizon Europe, AFBI has received awards totalling €3.94m for it is contributions to 12 research projects. Northern Ireland researchers across academia, the private and public sectors have successfully secured a total of €95.12m through Horizon 2020 in respect of 254 grants through 306 participations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what commitment he can give to conducting an economic impact assessment on the ending of the Basic Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The Basic Payment Scheme (BPS) is an EU tool which has provided income support payments to farmers.  A more targeted approach is needed to help farmers to develop their businesses, no matter where they farm, to become more productive, profitable and resilient, and to maximise the sustainable returns from the assets at their disposal.  Farm support payments will, therefore, not end but will be redefined to better meet local needs.  The roll out of future agricultural support schemes will not impact the overall agricultural support budget as this is ring fenced.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the challenges facing the pig industry; and what actions he is taken to address these challenges.
answer_text: The local pig industry is currently facing a number of issues and challenges that are impacting simultaneously and I have met with stakeholders on several occasions over the last year to discuss these matters. They include escalating costs for feed and energy due to the crisis in Ukraine, weakened markets due to supply and demand issues across Europe, and a general shortage of labour in the processing sector causing a backlog of pigs on farm. This has resulted in production costs exceeding market returns and cash flow difficulties on many farms.In March 2022, I provided the Permanent Secretary with a Ministerial Direction to develop and deliver a pig sector support scheme to address some of these issues. The Scheme was targeted at those pig producers who incurred price penalties on pigs that were outside contract specifications from September 2021 to February 2022 (above normal baseline levels), and to those who were charged £52.50 per pig for pigs slaughtered by Cranswick Country Foods on Saturday shifts in December 2021. The Scheme opened on 19th May and payments of over £1.2m have been made to approximately 130 producers.The NI agri-food industry has been experiencing enormous pressure with regards to labour problems, particularly in relation to the number of slaughter plant operatives and butchers in our abattoirs and processing plants. Industry representatives have articulated that the implementation of the new UK Immigration System was the main catalyst for these labour shortages. I have formally raised these issues with the Defra Secretary of State, George Eustice MP and the Minister for Safe and Legal Migration, Kevin Foster MP. I have also urged the Home Office to consider a number of recommendations to help mitigate the issue and I understand butchers have recently started to work pork processing plants via the UK Skilled Worker Visa Route.Increasing costs of production must be balanced by price adjustments throughout the supply chain if the economic viability of pig production and domestic supply is to be sustained. I note that the retail market for pig meat is slowly beginning to respond, to offset these rising costs that have caused a major squeeze on cash flow and loss of profitability. This has undermined confidence throughout the industry and will inevitably result in a contraction in production due to mounting losses if the situation is not corrected very soon. I have therefore written to retailers to voice my concern regarding farm gate prices for primary food producers, as I believe a significant realignment of markets is the only long term sustainable solution to the current input cost challenge.I continue to engage with the UK Government and continue to raise many of the current issues that our pig industry is facing at the Inter-Ministerial Group meetings. Since the situation that our pig industry is facing is a UK-wide problem I have written to the Secretary of State, George Eustice MP, to urgently consider a range of measu
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what commitment he can give that he will address the British government's free trade deals with New Zealand & Australia in the draft food strategy framework.
answer_text: The negotiation of trade deals falls under international relations and therefore is not a devolved matter. Consequently responsibility for the content of Free Trade Agreements and their impact on the UK rests with the UK Government.	I have long maintained that tariff and quota protection should be retained for sensitive agricultural products and have emphasised the importance of this to UK Government Ministers including the Defra Secretary of State George Eustice MP and Ministers in the Department for International Trade.The draft Food Strategy Framework will take into account the overall trading environment as it impacts on Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on avian influenza.
answer_text: To date, 30 June 2022, there have been 123 cases of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) H5N1 across the UK since the first case confirmed in England on 26 October 2021.  There have been 102 cases in England, 10 in Scotland, five in Wales and six in Northern Ireland (NI).  There have been six confirmed cases in the Republic of Ireland. Following a Veterinary Risk Assessment indicating the decreasing risk level, the NI Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) was lifted on 1 June 2022 with poultry gatherings, shows and sales now permitted under general licence.  The AIPZ remains in place across Great Britain (GB) although the requirement for mandatory housing for birds, as part of that AIPZ, have been lifted. There have been no further cases of HPAI in poultry in NI since early February 2022.  Cleansing and disinfection has been finished at five sites with the final site nearing completion. There have been a small number of wild bird submissions since April 2022 when the wild survey recommenced with two testing positive for HPAI H5N1.Given that there is still an ongoing low level risk of an AI incursion into poultry flocks the biosecurity requirements set out in the AIPZ are still considered good practice and should be adhered to as much as practically possible by flock keepers. My Department continues to communicate this message widely to industry stakeholders and the general public through a range of communication channels.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what impact the closure of the fertiliser plant, CF Industries Holdings Inc, in Britain, will have on the supply of fertiliser to the north of Ireland.
answer_text: CF Fertilisers UK is the UK's largest producer of ammonium nitrate (AN) fertilisers and recently announced its intention to close its manufacturing plant at Ince, as part of 'proposals to restructure its operations'. The Ince plant has not been operational since September 2021 and the company will continue to manufacture AN fertilisers exclusively at its Billingham plant in Teesside.AN cannot be used for agricultural purposes in Northern Ireland (NI) and the proposed closure of the Ince plant will not have a direct impact in NI. However, any reduction in the availability of AN in GB has the potential to increase demand for other nitrogenous fertilisers and impact on their availability in NI. Defra are examining various mitigation measures to ensure that there is sufficient GB supply, including sourcing AN products from other countries. DAERA officials continue to liaise with Defra and the fertiliser industry in NI to monitor fertiliser availability and price. At present, supply in NI is sufficient to meet currently demand. However, fertiliser companies will base future supplies on confirmed orders. Therefore, it is important that farmers order future fertiliser requirements in advance.Since January this year, CAFRE have been engaged in extensive advisory work for farmers in the area of rising costs including efficient use of fertilisers.  Economic use of fertilisers, best practice grazing management, making high quality silage and longer term mitigations have been some of the messages communicated.  This has been delivered through webinars, press articles, online forums, Business Development Group training events, sixteen on farm demonstration events and a dedicated webpage with technical articles. CAFREs Knowledge Advisory Service will continue to communicate out messages on nutrient management and options to mitigate the impact of rising costs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement his Department has had with the Department of Education to progress educational awareness programmes on animal welfare, in schools.
answer_text: My Department has worked with the Education Authority to develop and publish resources for teachers on the welfare of animals and buying and caring for a puppy. These materials are available for all teachers to download and use from the Education Authority’s online library facility.I am convinced that there is significant benefit to be gained from embedding animal welfare within the school curriculum. I, therefore, intend to write to the Minister of Education shortly to canvass her views on the matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Shared Prosperity Fund; and (ii) what engagement he is having with the British government to ensure there is support for rural communities.
answer_text: Part (i)The Department of Finance (DoF), leading on the cross-departmental Future Policy and Finance working group, has been actively engaging with the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) on the funding streams associated with the Levelling Up agenda including the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF).The SPF prospectus which was launched on 14 April 2022, did not provide substantial detail on the development of the Northern Ireland Investment Plan or delivery structures. However, the Northern Ireland allocation has been set at £127m across the three years with Whitehall top slicing the allocation by 17% (£21.9m)  to fund their “Multiply” UK-wide adult numeracy programme.It is anticipated that this will be broken down into yearly allocations of £19m in 2022-23, £34m in 2023-24 and £74m in 2024-25.DoF has advised that the allocation does not meet the manifesto commitment for full replacement of the European Social fund (ESF) and the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) and falls short of the £65m per annum Northern Ireland would have received on average from these funds. As lead department, DoF officials have continued to work to gain a substantive role for the Executive in the design and delivery of these funds, while seeking to understand the role of all stakeholders.DoF has stressed to DLUHC that any funding delivered here must adhere to our equalities legislation, be complementary to other spend, align to local strategies, meet local needs, provide value for money, avoid duplication and ensure maximum impact. DLUHC intends to establish a Partnership Group and in concert with local bodies develop and endorse an overarching investment plan which will ultimately be agreed by Whitehall Ministers. To date there has been very little information on roles, responsibilities and accountability or governance structures.  It is expected that DLUHC will start to reach out to organisations and bodies here shortly on this Partnership Group. DLUHC had indicated that the investment plan must be finalised in August. However, DoF anticipates that this will not be a hard date for Northern Ireland.Part (ii)I continue to engage, where applicable, with the UK Government on a number of matters in relation to EU Exit.  My Department receives EU replacement funding, from HM Treasury, in relation to the UK Government’s 2019 manifesto commitment.  This has been confirmed as almost £1 billion over the 2022-23 to 2024-25 period.  This funding is to replace previous allocations that were provided under the Common Agriculture Policy (CAP) under the 2014-20 Multiannual Financial Framework, in relation to agriculture production support and rural development.  Under Pillar 2 of the CAP my Department delivered the 2014-20 Rural Development Programme, which provided support to Rural Communities and my officials are currently working on proposals as to how best to utilise this replacement EU Funding to continue to provide support to Rural Communities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the economic difficulties faced by the mushroom industry.
answer_text: The latest NI mushroom industry benchmarking report compiled by CAFRE indicates that (as of May 2022) mushroom growers are facing input price inflation rates of 16.4%, compared with January 2021.The key drivers of inflation for mushroom businesses are linked to the increasing variable costs of packaging, labour, fuel and energy on the cost of production of a kilo of mushrooms. The benchmarking data does not include any interest repayments or depreciation. According to the latest benchmarking report, labour continues to be the biggest cost for mushroom businesses, representing on average 37.5% of turnover. NI total mushroom sector output in 2021 is provisionally estimated at £30million, according to NI agricultural aggregate account data. I met recently with a number of stakeholders from across the NI mushroom growing sector. Industry representatives stated that their primary concern is that of labour access. Coupled with challenges including increasing energy costs and problems regarding access to a sustainable labour supply, these issues are putting significant pressure on the sustainability and competitiveness of the sector. The NI mushroom growing sector believe that in order to remain competitive with counterparts in the Republic of Ireland and further afield, they require access to a more sustainable labour supply chain. It is their view that the skills classification of the occupation is underrated and they are therefore planning to provide evidence to the Migration Advisory Committee (MAC) for the reclassification of mushroom harvesters as a skilled occupation (i.e. RQF Level 3), which would allow this occupation to be eligible for the UK Immigration System’s Skilled Worker Visa. They also want to be able to make a case for this occupation to be listed under the NI specific Shortage Occupational List (SOL).I subsequently wrote to the Minister of State for Safe and Legal Migration regarding the impact of the UK’s Immigration System on the NI horticulture sector, requesting an update on the upcoming review of the SOL by the MAC and asking for the rationale for the Home Office decision to increase the wage rate for the Seasonal Agricultural Workers scheme to the same level as the Skilled Workers Visa salary threshold (minimum of £10.10 per hour), which came as a shock to the industry. This decision will have a far-reaching impact on the industry given the context of escalating input costs for the agri-food sector. I await a response to this correspondence from the Home Office.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action he is taking to increase food security given developments in Ukraine.
answer_text: I remain incredibly concerned about the developments within Ukraine as a result of the Russian invasion.  Both countries have crucial roles in global agri-food markets and the longer the conflict continues, the more likely we are to see lasting market implications. Northern Ireland producers are facing unprecedented rises in input costs of energy, feed and fertiliser which simply cannot be absorbed by farm businesses.  Unfortunately, end markets for agricultural produce and food are currently responding too slowly to offset these rising costs, causing a major squeeze on cash flow and loss of profitability.  This is undermining confidence throughout the industry and will inevitably result in a contraction of production due to mounting losses if the situation is not corrected very soon.  This correction must come in the form of increased farm gate prices for all sectors or the economic viability of primary production and domestic supplies of agricultural goods cannot be sustained.  I have written to retailers to voice my concern regarding farm gate prices for primary food producers.I continue to engage with the UK Government and raised the current issues that our industry is facing at the last Inter-Ministerial Group meeting.  I have also engaged with a wide range of agri-food stakeholders and industry representatives to see what mitigation measures are possible.  My officials have significant ongoing engagement and intelligence gathering from industry stakeholders, DEFRA, other devolved administrations and the Department of Agriculture, Food and Marine (ROI) on a number of cross cutting issues relating to reducing the impacts of volatility in price and supply of key industry inputs.  Officials at The College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) lead an industry taskforce (banks, producer and supply chain organisations, farming unions and Rural Support) to enhance the communication of advisory messages to the agriculture industry around the cost and availability of inputs and provide the sector with practical help and support.  CAFRE has a dedicated webpage providing advice on managing the rising costs of production and delivered a series of on-farm events. Food security is, of course, a matter that cannot meaningfully be addressed at a regional level and is something that must be considered at a national and supranational level.  I have raised the issue of food security with Defra Secretary of State, George Eustice MP.  I have also attended a Four Nation Fuel summit as I am concerned that the significant rise in fuel cost is jeopardising our ability to provide resilience in food supply.  I have asked Mr Eustice to consider urgently a range of measures, including financial assistance for UK farmers to support them through the crisis.  I have also written to the Finance Minister, Conor Murphy seeking funding to help address the challenges currently facing the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland.  These pressures on the industry are n
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action he is taking to address Bovine Viral Diarrhoea in cattle.
answer_text: My Department works closely with Animal Health and Welfare Northern Ireland to support the delivery of the industry led Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) eradication scheme. Good progress has been made in reducing the incidence of BVD in Northern Ireland since my Department introduced this compulsory scheme in 2016.  However, it is clear that more needs to be done to eradicate the disease.The imposition of herd restrictions is considered the most effective tool to be used against BVD. Work is therefore, currently ongoing within my Department to finalise policy proposals on the matter. These proposals will be subject to a public consultation and require legislative changes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the effectiveness of technology used in the agriculture sector to reduce the use of antibiotics in livestock.
answer_text: The key step to reduce the use of antibiotics in livestock is to improve the overall health status of the animals thereby reducing the need for antibiotics.Technology in agriculture can be a tool in achieving improvements in animal health through improving animal husbandry or as an early detection method for ill-health. Technology can also play a key role assisting farmers capture data on antibiotic usage. In collaboration with their private veterinary practitioner, this information can be used to identify those target areas for intervention in order to reduce and optimise antibiotic usage.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the potential economic and financial impacts of the proposed Peatlands Strategy on (i) horticulture; (ii) farming and rural communities; and (iii) commercial peat producers.
answer_text: Officials are currently finalising the Peatland Strategy document, seeking internal agreement on the Objectives and Actions and ensuring resources are in place for delivery.  I intend to seek Executive approval for the Strategy prior to publication.Within the Strategy, there is a proposed action to “Conduct a review and publish a key issues paper on peat extraction and the use of peat and peat products by 2023 and subsequently take forward any recommendations made”. As part of this review, stakeholder input will play a vital role in assisting the Department in determining all potential impacts and opportunities for the horticultural and peat extraction industries and rural communities.  The review process will include the completion of all necessary economic and equality impact assessments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Future Agriculture Policy; and (ii) whether he intends to introduce a sheep measure scheme.
answer_text: My Department is designing a more tailored agricultural policy, which better meets the needs of Northern Ireland. In March, I announced 54 decisions which define future agricultural policy and allow schemes to be developed that will provide opportunities for all Northern Ireland’s farmers.  The new agricultural policy decisions will be introduced over the next number of years, probably from 2024 at the earliest.  Some pilot projects may commence before 2024, but no decisions on this have yet been made.These policy decisions provide a clear direction of travel for future agricultural policy in Northern Ireland, however there is still significant work to be done, which my officials will continue to take forward, along with continued engagement with stakeholders.At this stage, no further proposals are being brought forward for support to incentivise productivity in breeding ewes, however my Department is undertaking further work to explore support options that will improve the overall performance and resilience of the sheep sector.  This includes exploring how the sheep sector could be incentivised to provide baseline performance data to inform future sector support measures and engage in a future Ruminant Genetics Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Rural Policy Framework.
answer_text: As you will be aware, I launched the new Rural Policy Framework on 24th March 2022.   My officials have now started to draft the new programme and progress the work necessary to form the Rural Stakeholder Oversight Committee and associated sub-groups.In establishing the Rural Stakeholder Oversight Committee, my officials are engaging with key rural stakeholders to ensure that membership of the Oversight Committee is representative and supports strong community involvement in the development and delivery of the new programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the draft Peatlands Strategy 2021-2040.
answer_text: Officials are currently finalising the Peatland Strategy document, seeking internal agreement on the Objectives and Actions and ensuring resources are in place for delivery. An Equality Impact Assessment has been drafted to accompany the strategy and I intend to carry out a 12 week public consultation on this assessment over the summer. Following my final and Executive approval, I anticipate publication of an agreed Peatland Strategy in Autumn 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the implementation of the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, including the dates for each of the four zones to be soil sampled; and (ii) to outline the benefits of this scheme to farmers.
answer_text: (i) The Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) opened for online registrations to farmers (approximately 6,800) in Zone 1 on 27 May 2022. The online registration process is accessible through the DAERA Online Services portal for farmers or their agents (with farmer agent approval) to complete. Assistance is also available to farmers through DAERA Customer Service Branch staff in DAERA direct offices located within Zone 1 (Newtownards, Downpatrick, Newry, Armagh and Mallusk) should they require help completing the online registration process. Registered farmers in Zone 1 will have their soils sampled by a contractor sometime in the period between November 2022 and March 2023 and receive their analysis results normally 4-6 weeks after their samples are taken. The zonal approach over the 4 year period of the project was established during pre-market engagement with potential soil sample collection and analysis contractors and it was deemed that splitting NI into 4 equal zones would be the most feasible way to complete the work, as it will require approximately 250 sample collectors, each 12 week winter closed period to gather the samples. A useful zonal map is attached at Annex A which describes the 4 zones and what winters each will be completed:Zone 1 – South East of the province	Winter 2022Zone 2 – South West of the province	Winter 2023Zone 3 – North West of the province 	Winter 2024Zone 4 – North East of the province 	Winter 2025(ii) The benefits of the SNHS scheme to farmers include:Receiving detailed information on the nutrient status of the soils for each field on their farm and including pH (lime requirement), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K), Magnesium (Mg), Sulphur (S) and Loss on Ignition (LOI) (organic matter) which could result in savings via better targeting of  nutrients.Nutrient and lime recommendations for crop production for each field, which could improve grass yield and reduce concentrate feed. Receiving runoff risk maps for nutrient loss to waterbodies for each field which will help in planning fertiliser and manure applications and help reduce losses of nutrients to waterbodies and help market NI food products in a positive environmental light. Receiving estimates of the carbon stored in the soils, hedgerows and trees on their farm which may be a baseline for measuring any change of practices that improve the carbon footprint of the farm.Receiving CAFRE-led training on the interpretation of the soil nutrient reports and recommendations, generation of nutrient management plans and an overview of the carbon stock estimates for the whole farm.My Department have produced a SNHS Privacy Notice which highlights the uses of the data at regional, catchment level and at farm level.
